Ayahs

طسٓمٓ (١)
Ta, Seen, Meem. (1)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (2)
نَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكَ مِن نَّبَإِ مُوسَىٰ وَفِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣)
We relate to you truly some of the account of Moses and Pharaoh for a people who have faith. (3)
إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا يَسْتَضْعِفُ طَآئِفَةً مِّنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَيَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٤)
Indeed Pharaoh tyrannized over the land, reducing its people to factions, abasing one group of them, slaughtering their sons, and sparing their women. Indeed, He was one of the agents of corruption. (4)
وَنُرِيدُ أَن نَّمُنَّ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٥)
And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs, (5)
وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنُرِىَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَحْذَرُونَ (٦)
and to establish them in the land, and to show Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts from them that of which they were apprehensive. (6)

Content of Surah al-Qasas

It is well-known that this Surah was revealed in Makkah; therefore, its general content and main themes align with those of other Makkan Surahs [1]. Although some commentators have excepted verse 85 of this Surah, or verses 52 to 55, believing that the former was revealed in "Juhfah" (a land between Makkah and Madinah) and the other five verses in Madinah, there is no clear evidence for their claim.

Perhaps the content of the five verses, which speak of the People of the Book—who mostly resided in Madinah—led to such a notion. However, it is not the case that the Quran in Makkah spoke only of the polytheists, especially since the people of Makkah and Madinah had frequent interactions.

Of course, a context of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) has been mentioned for verses 52 to 55 that is consistent with them being Madinan, and God willing, we will discuss it in its proper place.

Regarding verse 85, which speaks of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) return to his original homeland, Makkah, there is no obstacle to it having been revealed near this holy land during his migration and departure from Makkah. This is because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) cherished the land of Makkah, which was Allah’s secure sanctuary and the center of the House of Monotheism, and in this verse, Allah gives him the glad tidings that He will eventually return him to this city.

Therefore, the aforementioned verse can also be Makkan, and even assuming it was revealed in the land of "Juhfah," that location is closer to Makkah than to Madinah. Thus, in the dual classification of verses into "Makkan" and "Madinan," one cannot find a place for verse 85 outside of the "Makkan" verses.

Indeed, this Surah was revealed in Makkah at a time when the believers were trapped in the clutches of powerful enemies—enemies who surpassed them in both population and numbers, as well as power and strength. This Muslim minority was under such pressure from that majority that some seemed fearful and concerned about the future of Islam.

Since this situation bore a great resemblance to the state of the Children of Israel in the clutches of the Pharaohs, a portion of this Surah’s content consists of the story of "Moses," the "Children of Israel," and the "Pharaohs"—a portion that encompasses about half of the verses of this Surah.

Specifically, it speaks of the time when Moses was a weak, suckling infant in the clutches of the Pharaohs. Yet, that invincible Power which overshadows the entire universe raised this weak infant in the lap of his powerful enemies and eventually granted him such power and strength that he dismantled the apparatus of the Pharaohs and overturned their palace of injustice.

This was so that the Muslims would be heartened by the grace of their Lord and His infinite power, and not allow fear to enter their hearts due to the enemy's superior numbers and strength.

Indeed, the first part of this Surah consists of this meaningful and instructive history, and particularly at its beginning, it recounts the promise of the government of truth and justice for the oppressed and the glad tidings of shattering the glory of the oppressors—a soothing and power-generating promise.

It says that as long as the Children of Israel had no leader or guide, were not under the umbrella of faith and monotheism, and lacked any organized and united movement or effort, they wore the chains of captivity and slavery around their necks. However, when they found their leader and enlightened their hearts with the light of knowledge and monotheism, they charged against the Pharaohs such that they took the government from them forever and liberated the Children of Israel.

Another part of this Surah recounts the story of "Qarun" (Korah), that arrogant wealthy man who relied on his knowledge and wealth, and who, due to pride, met a fate similar to that of Pharaoh. One sank into the water and the other into the earth; one relied on military power and governance, and the other relied on wealth.

This was to make it clear that neither the "wealthy of Makkah" nor the "powerful polytheists" in that land, nor the political players of that environment, have the ability to show the slightest resistance against the Will of Allah, which is committed to the victory of the "oppressed" over the "arrogant." This section appears toward the end of the Surah.

Between these two parts, there are living and valuable lessons on monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), the importance of the Quran, the state of the polytheists on the Day of Judgment, the issue of guidance and misguidance, and responses to the excuses of weak individuals—which are, in fact, a conclusion to the first part and a preamble to the second.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Qasas

In a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "Whoever recites Ta-Sin-Mim al-Qasas will be given ten rewards for each person who believed in Moses or denied him, and there is no angel in the heavens and the earth except that he will testify for him on the Day of Resurrection that he was truthful." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), it is stated: "Whoever recites the three Tawasins (Surahs al-Qasas, al-Naml, and al-Shu'ara) on Friday night will be among the friends of Allah, in His proximity, and under His protection. In this world, he will never face poverty, insecurity, or severe distress, and in the Hereafter, Allah will grant him so much of His bounties that he will be satisfied and even beyond satisfaction." [2]

It is obvious that all these rewards and prizes belong to those who, by reading this Surah, place themselves in the ranks of Moses (عليه السلام) and the true believers, and on the path of struggle against the Pharaohs and Qaruns of the time. They do not kneel before the enemy in times of hardship and do not accept the humiliation of surrender. Such great bounties are not given cheaply to anyone; they are specifically for those who read, reflect, and make it their program of action.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, at the beginning of Surah al-Qasas. [2]: Thawab al-A'mal, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, at the beginning of Surah al-Qasas.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ طسم (1) تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ (2) نَتْلُوا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ نَبَإِ مُوسى‌ وَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (3) إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ جَعَلَ أَهْلَها شِيَعاً يَسْتَضْعِفُ طائِفَةً مِنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْناءَهُمْ وَ يَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ (4) وَ نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ نَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَ نَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ (5) وَ نُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ نُرِيَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ هامانَ وَ جُنُودَهُما مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَحْذَرُونَ (6)

Commentary

Our Will is Committed to the Victory of the Oppressed!

This is the fourteenth time we encounter the "Disconnected Letters" (Huruof al-Muqatta'ah) at the beginning of Quranic Surahs, and specifically, "Ta-Sin-Mim" appears for the third and final time.

As we have said many times, the Disconnected Letters of the Quran have various interpretations, which we discussed in detail at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-A'raf.

Furthermore, regarding "Ta-Sin-Mim," it appears from several narrations that these letters are abbreviations for the attributes of Allah or sacred places. Yet, at the same time, this does not preclude the well-known interpretation we have repeatedly emphasized: that Allah wants to make this reality clear to everyone—that this great Heavenly Book, which became the source of a great revolution in human history and contains the complete program for a prosperous human life, is composed of simple means such as the letters of the alphabet (Alif, Ba...) which any child can pronounce. It is the ultimate greatness to create such an extraordinarily significant product from such simple materials that are available to everyone.

Perhaps for this reason, immediately after these Disconnected Letters, the greatness of the Quran is mentioned, saying: "These majestic verses are the verses of the Clear Book"—a book that is both clear in itself and clarifies the path to prosperity for humanity (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ).

Although "al-Kitab al-Mubin" in some verses of the Quran, such as verse 61 of Surah Yunus and verse 6 of Surah Hud, is interpreted as the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), in the verse under discussion, by the context of the mention of "verses" and also the phrase "We recite to you" in the following verse, it means the Quran.

Here, the Quran is described as "Mubin." As understood from the language, "Mubin" can be both intransitive and transitive: something that is both manifest and manifesting. The Holy Quran, with its clear content, makes truth manifest from falsehood and the path from the deviation [1].

After mentioning this short introduction, the Quran enters into the narration of the story of "Moses" and "Pharaoh," saying: "We recite to you with truth from the news of Moses and Pharaoh for a people who believe" (نَتْلُوا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ نَبَإِ مُوسى‌ وَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

The use of "min" (from), which is technically "partitive" (tab'idiyyah), points to the fact that what is mentioned here is a portion of this eventful story, the expression of which was appropriate and necessary.

The expression "bi-al-haqq" (with truth) indicates that what is mentioned here is free from any superstitions, vanities, myths, or unrealistic matters. It is a recitation coupled with truth and the very essence of reality. The phrase "for a people who believe" is an emphasis on the fact that the believers of that day who were under pressure in Makkah—and those like them—must, upon hearing this story, arrive at the truth that no matter how great the enemy's power, and no matter how numerous their population, personnel, and forces, and no matter how much the people of faith are in the minority and under pressure and seemingly powerless, they must never allow weakness or lethargy to enter them. For in the face of Allah’s power, everything is easy.

The God who raised Moses in the lap of Pharaoh to destroy Pharaoh; the God who brought the oppressed slaves to the governance of the earth and humiliated and destroyed the stubborn tyrants; and the God who protected a suckling infant amidst the raging waves and buried thousands upon thousands of the powerful Pharaohs in the waves, is capable of saving you.

Yes, the primary target of these verses is the believers, and this recitation has taken place for their sake and because of them—believers who can draw inspiration from it and open their way toward the goal amidst a mass of difficulties.

This was, in fact, a summary statement; then He proceeds to its detail, saying: "Pharaoh exalted himself and sought dominance and superiority in the land" (إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ).

He was a weak servant who, due to ignorance, lost his sense of self and progressed to the point of claiming divinity. The term "al-Ard" (the land) refers to the land of Egypt and its surroundings, and since a significant inhabited part of the earth at that time was that region, this word appears in an absolute form. It is also possible that the definite article (al-) is for reference to the land of Egypt.

In any case, to strengthen the foundations of his arrogance, he committed several major crimes: First, "he sought to sow division among the people of Egypt" (وَ جَعَلَ أَهْلَها شِيَعاً).

This is the same policy that throughout history has formed the main foundation of the rule of the arrogant. For the rule of a tiny minority over a large majority is not possible except through the program of "divide and rule"! They have always feared and continue to fear the "unity of the word" and the "word of unity." They are intensely afraid of the connection between the ranks of the people, and for this reason, a class-based government is their only way of preservation—the same thing Pharaoh and the Pharaohs of every age and time have done and continue to do.

Yes, Pharaoh specifically divided the people of Egypt into two distinct groups: the "Copts," who were the natives of that land and had all the comforts, palaces, wealth, and government posts at their disposal; and the "Israelites," namely the migrant Children of Israel, who were trapped in their clutches as slaves, servants, and bondmaids.

Poverty and deprivation had enveloped their entire existence, and the hardest labors were on their shoulders without them receiving any benefit (and the term "people/inhabitants" regarding both groups is because the Children of Israel had resided in that land for a long time and had truly become its inhabitants). When we hear that some of the Pharaohs of Egypt employed one hundred thousand slaves over twenty years to build a tomb for themselves (the famous Pyramid of "Khufu" located near the Egyptian capital, Cairo) and killed thousands of them in this process by the lash or from the pressure of work, we should understand the full story from this brief mention!

His second crime was the oppression of a group of people in that land. The Quran says: "He so reduced this group to weakness and helplessness that he would slaughter their sons and keep their women alive for service" (يَسْتَضْعِفُ طائِفَةً مِنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْناءَهُمْ وَ يَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ).

He had ordered that they closely watch the children born to the Children of Israel; if they were boys, they were to be put to the sword! And if they were girls, they were to be kept alive for domestic service and slavery! Truly, what did he intend to achieve with this action?

It is well-known that he had seen in a dream a flame of fire rising from Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis) and enveloping all the houses of Egypt; it burned the houses of the Copts but the houses of the Children of Israel remained safe! He asked the knowledgeable ones and dream interpreters for an explanation. They said: "A man will emerge from this land of Jerusalem whose hand will bring about the destruction of Egypt and the rule of the Pharaohs!" [1] It is also narrated that some priests told him: "A boy will be born among the Children of Israel who will blow your government to the winds!" [2] Ultimately, this very matter caused Pharaoh to decide to kill the male infants of the Children of Israel.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that previous prophets had given glad tidings of the appearance of Moses and his characteristics, and the Pharaohs, being aware of this, were fearful and rose to combat it [3]. However, the placement of the phrase "slaughtering their sons" after the phrase "he oppressed a group of them" recounts another matter. it says the Pharaohs had designed this plan to weaken the Children of Israel: to eliminate their male generation who could rise up and fight the Pharaohs, and to keep only the girls and women alive for service, as they alone did not have the power to rise and struggle.

Another proof of this is that from verse 25 of Surah "Ghafir" (al-Mu'min), it is clearly understood that the program of killing sons and keeping daughters alive continued even after the uprising of Moses (عليه السلام): "So when he brought them the truth from Us, they said: 'Kill the sons of those who have believed with him and keep their women alive.' But the plot of the disbelievers is nothing but in error."

The phrase "keeping their women alive" (يَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ) is apparent in that they insisted on the survival of the girls and women, either for domestic service or sexual exploitation, or both.

In the final sentence of this verse, as a summary and also a statement of the cause, He says: "Indeed, he was of the corrupters" (إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ ... كانَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

In short, Pharaoh's work was summarized in corruption on earth: his seeking of superiority was corruption; creating a class-based life in Egypt was another corruption; the torture of the Children of Israel, as well as killing their sons and enslaving their daughters, was a third corruption; and besides these, he had many other corruptions.

It is natural that superior-seeking and self-worshipping individuals only protect their own interests, and the protection of personal interests will never be in harmony with the protection of the interests of society, which requires justice, sacrifice, and altruism. Therefore, whatever it may be, the result is corruption in all dimensions of life.

Additionally, the term "yudhabihu," which comes from the root "dhabh" (slaughter), shows that the Pharaohs' behavior toward the Children of Israel was like the behavior toward sheep and livestock, and they slaughtered these innocent humans like animals [1]. Regarding this criminal program of the Pharaohs, stories have been told; some say Pharaoh ordered that pregnant women of the Children of Israel be kept under close surveillance, and only Coptic and Pharaonic midwives were commissioned for the delivery, so that if the newborn was a boy, they would immediately inform the Egyptian government authorities and the executioners would come and take their victim [1]. Exactly how many infants of the Children of Israel were sacrificed in this program is not precisely clear; some have said ninety thousand! And some hundreds of thousands! They imagined that with these horrific crimes, they could prevent the uprising of the Children of Israel and the realization of Allah’s certain Will.

Immediately after this verse, He says: "And Our Will and desire was to bestow favor upon those who were oppressed in the land and make them the recipients of Our bounties" (وَ نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ). "And to make them leaders and make them the inheritors of the earth" (وَ نَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَ نَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ).

"And to establish them in the land with strength and power, and make their government firm and stable" (وَ نُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ). "And to show Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts that which they feared from these oppressed ones!" (وَ نُرِيَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ هامانَ وَ جُنُودَهُما مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَحْذَرُونَ).

How eloquent and hope-inspiring are these two verses! For they are expressed as a general law and in the form of the present/continuous tense (mudari'), so that it is not imagined to be exclusive to the oppressed of the Children of Israel and the rule of the Pharaohs. It says: "We want to do such..."

That is, Pharaoh wanted to scatter the Children of Israel and break their power and glory, but We wanted them to become strong and victorious. He wanted the government to remain in the hands of the arrogant forever, but We had willed to entrust the government to the oppressed! And eventually, it happened so.

Additionally, the expression "mann" (favor), as we have said before, means bestowing bounties and blessings, and this is very different from verbal "mann" (taunting), which is recounting a blessing with the intention of belittling the other party and is certainly a blameworthy act.

In these two verses, Allah has pulled back the curtain from His Will and desire regarding the oppressed and states five matters in this regard that are closely linked and connected. First, that We want to include them in Our blessings (وَ نُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ...). Second, that We want to make them leaders (نَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً). Third, that We want to make them the inheritors of the government of the tyrants (وَ نَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ). Fourth, that We give them a strong and stable government (وَ نُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ). And finally, fifth, that We show their enemies exactly what they feared and against which they had mobilized all their forces (وَ نُرِيَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ هامانَ وَ جُنُودَهُما مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَحْذَرُونَ).

Such is the grace and care of the Lord regarding the "oppressed." But who are they? And what are their attributes? In the "Key Points" section, God willing, we will speak of it.

"Haman" was Pharaoh's famous minister and had such influence in his apparatus that in the verse above, the Egyptian hosts are referred to as the hosts of Pharaoh and Haman (more details about Haman will come, God willing, under verse 38 of this Surah).

Key Points

1- The Global Government of the Oppressed

We said that the above verses never speak of a local and private program related only to the Children of Israel; rather, they express a general law for all ages, centuries, and all peoples and groups. It says: "We will to bestow favor upon the oppressed and make them leaders and inheritors of the government of the earth."

This is a glad tiding regarding the victory of truth over falsehood and faith over disbelief. It is a glad tiding for all free humans who desire a government of justice and equity and the dismantling of the apparatus of oppression and tyranny.

An example of the realization of this Divine Will was the government of the Children of Israel and the demise of the rule of the Pharaohs. A more complete example was the government of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions after the emergence of Islam—the government of the barefooted, the faithful poor, and the pure-hearted oppressed who were constantly belittled and mocked by the Pharaohs of their time and were under pressure, oppression, and tyranny. Eventually, by the hand of this very group, Allah opened the gates of the palaces of the Caesars and Khosrows, brought them down from the throne of power, and rubbed the noses of the arrogant in the dust.

And its "most extensive" example is the emergence of the government of truth and justice across the entire globe by "the Mahdi" (may our souls be sacrificed for him). These verses are among those that clearly give the glad tidings of the emergence of such a government; hence, in Islamic narrations, we read that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), in interpreting this verse, have pointed to this great emergence.

In Nahj al-Balagha, it is narrated from Ali (عليه السلام): "The world will certainly turn back to us after its stubbornness—just as a biting camel turns to its young (refusing to give milk to the milker and keeping it for its calf)." Then he recited the verse: "And We will to bestow favor upon those who were oppressed in the land..." [1]

In another Hadith from the same great Imam (عليه السلام), we read that in interpreting the above verse he said: "They are the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله); Allah will raise their Mahdi after their struggle and hardship, then He will grant them honor and humiliate their enemy." [2]

And in a Hadith from Imam Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام), we read: "By the One who sent Muhammad with the truth as a bearer of glad tidings and a warner, the righteous among us, the Ahl al-Bayt, and their followers are in the position of Moses and his followers, and our enemy and their followers are in the position of Pharaoh and his followers." (Eventually, we will be victorious and they will be destroyed, and the government of truth and justice will be ours). [3]

Of course, the global government of the Mahdi (عليه السلام) at the end of time never prevents Islamic governments on more limited scales before that, by the oppressed against the arrogant. Whenever they provide the conditions for it, the certain promise and Divine Will regarding them will be realized and this victory will be their lot.

2- Who are the "Oppressed" (Mustad'afin) and the "Arrogant" (Mustakbirin)?

We know that the word "Mustad'af" comes from the root "du'f" (weakness), but because it is in the Istif'al form, it means someone who has been reduced to weakness and has been placed in bonds and chains.

In other words, a "Mustad'af" is not someone who is weak, helpless, or lacking power and strength. A Mustad'af is someone who has actual and potential powers, but has been placed under severe pressure by tyrants and oppressors. Yet, despite this, they are not silent or submissive before the bonds and chains placed on their hands and feet; they constantly strive to break the chains and become free, to shorten the hands of the tyrants and oppressors, and to establish the religion of truth and justice.

Allah has promised such a group help and governance on earth, not to the helpless, cowardly, and fearful individuals who are not even willing to let out a cry, let alone step into the field of struggle and make sacrifices.

The Children of Israel were able to inherit the government of the Pharaohs on that day because they gathered around their leader Moses (عليه السلام), mobilized their forces, and all formed a single rank. The remnants of faith they had inherited from their forefather Abraham were completed by the call of Moses (عليه السلام), and they purged superstitions from their minds and prepared for the uprising.

Of course, "Mustad'af" has various types: "intellectual" and "cultural" oppressed, "economic" oppressed, "moral" oppressed, and "political" oppressed. What the Quran emphasizes most is the political and moral oppressed.

Undoubtedly, to consolidate the foundations of their tyrannical policy, the arrogant tyrants first and foremost try to lead their victims into intellectual and cultural oppression, then into economic oppression, so that no power or strength remains for them, and so they do not nurture the thought of rising up and taking the reins of government in their hands and minds.

In the Holy Quran, the "Mustad'afin" are mentioned in five places, generally speaking of believers who were under the pressure of tyrants. In one place, it invites the believers to struggle and Jihad in the way of Allah and the faithful oppressed, saying: "And what is [the matter] with you that you fight not in the cause of Allah and [for] the oppressed among men, women, and children who say, 'Our Lord, take us out of this city whose people are oppressors and appoint for us from Yourself a protector and appoint for us from Yourself a helper?'" (An-Nisa: 75).

Only in one case does it speak of those who are oppressors and cooperate with the disbelievers while claiming to be oppressed, and the Quran rejects their claim, saying: "You could have escaped their clutches by migrating from the region of disbelief and corruption, but since you did not do so, your abode is Hell" (content of verse 97, An-Nisa).

But in any case, the Quran everywhere rises to support the oppressed and mentions them with goodness, considering them as believers under pressure—striving, mujahid believers who are subject to Allah’s grace.

3- The General Method of the Arrogant of History

It was not only Pharaoh who, to enslave the Children of Israel, killed their men and kept their women alive for service; throughout history, all tyrants have been such, using every means to disable active forces.

Where they could not kill the men, they killed "manliness." By spreading means of corruption, narcotics, developing indecency and sexual licentiousness, expanding wine and gambling, and various types of unhealthy entertainment, they smothered the spirit of bravery, chivalry, and faith in them, so they could continue their autocratic rule with a mind at ease.

However, the Divine Prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), sought to awaken and liberate the dormant forces of the youth, and even taught women lessons in manliness, placing them in the ranks of men against the arrogant.

The evidence of these two programs is well-apparent in past and present history in all Islamic countries, such that we see no need to mention them.

Ayahs

وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ ۖ فَإِذَا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ وَلَا تَخَافِى وَلَا تَحْزَنِىٓ ۖ إِنَّا رَآدُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ وَجَاعِلُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٧)
We revealed to Moses’ mother, [saying], ‘Nurse him; then, when you fear for him, cast him into the river, and do not fear or grieve, for We will restore him to you and make him one of the apostles.’ (7)
فَٱلْتَقَطَهُۥٓ ءَالُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰطِـِٔينَ (٨)
Then Pharaoh’s kinsmen picked him up that he might be an enemy and a cause of grief to them. Indeed Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were iniquitous. (8)
وَقَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِّى وَلَكَ ۖ لَا تَقْتُلُوهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩)
Pharaoh’s wife said [to him], ‘[This infant will be] a [source of] comfort to me and to you. Do not kill him. Maybe he will benefit us, or we will adopt him as a son.’ But they were not aware. (9)

Commentary

In the Embrace of Pharaoh!

From here, the Holy Quran enters the detailed narrative of the story of Moses and Pharaoh to depict a living example of the victory of the oppressed (Mustad'afin) over the arrogant (Mustakbirin). It specifically focuses on those parts that show Moses in his weakest state and Pharaoh in his most powerful condition, in order to manifest the victory of Allah’s Will over the will of tyrants in the most sublime manner.

First, It says: "And We inspired the mother of Moses (and suggested to her): 'Suckle him; but when you fear for him, cast him into the river!'" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ أُمِّ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ فَإِذا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِي الْيَمِ‌).

"And do not fear, nor grieve" (وَ لا تَخافِي وَ لا تَحْزَنِي‌).

"For We shall surely restore him to you, and shall make him one of the messengers" (إِنَّا رَادُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ وَ جاعِلُوهُ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

This short verse contains two commands, two prohibitions, and two glad tidings, which together form a summary of a great and eventful story, the essence of which is as follows:

Pharaoh’s apparatus had organized an extensive program for killing the "newborn boys" of the Israelites, and even Pharaonic midwives were monitoring the pregnant women of the Israelites.

Among them, one of these midwives had a friendship with the mother of Moses (the pregnancy of Moses was kept secret, and not many signs of pregnancy were visible in the mother). When she felt that the birth of the infant was near, she sent for her midwife friend and said: "My situation is such; I have a child in my womb, and today I am in need of your kindness and friendship."

When Moses was born, a mysterious light shone from his eyes, such that the midwife’s body trembled, and a spark of love settled deep within her heart, illuminating all the corners of her soul.

The midwife turned to the mother of Moses and said: "I intended to report the birth of this infant to the government apparatus so that the executioners would come and kill this boy (and I would receive my reward), but what can I do, for I feel an intense love for this infant within my heart! I am not even willing for a hair to be lost from his head; protect him with care. I think our ultimate enemy might eventually be him!"

The midwife left the house of Moses' mother. Some government spies saw her and decided to enter the house. Moses' sister informed the mother of the situation; the mother became so frantic that she did not know what to do.

Amidst this intense terror that had deprived her of her senses, she wrapped the infant in a cloth and threw him into the oven. The officers entered and saw nothing there but a fiery oven! They began questioning the mother of Moses, saying: "What was this midwife doing here?" She said: "She is my friend and had come for a visit." The officers became disappointed and left.

Moses' mother regained her senses and asked Moses' sister, "Where is the infant?" She expressed her ignorance. Suddenly, the sound of crying rose from within the oven. The mother ran toward the oven and saw that Allah had made the fire "coolness and peace" for him (the same God who made Nimrod’s fire cool and safe for Abraham). She reached in and brought her infant out safely.

But the mother was still not safe, for officers were moving and searching left and right, and hearing the cry of an infant was enough for a great danger to occur.

At this point, a Divine inspiration illuminated the mother's heart—an inspiration that seemingly invited her to a dangerous act, yet she felt a sense of peace from it nonetheless.

This was a Divine mission that had to be carried out regardless, and she decided to put this inspiration into action and entrust her infant to the Nile!

She went to an Egyptian carpenter (a carpenter who was also from the Copts and the Pharaonists!) and requested him to build a small chest for her.

The carpenter said: "With these specifications you describe, what do you want the chest for?!" A mother whose tongue was not accustomed to lying could not say anything here except: "I am from the Israelites, I have a newborn son, and I want to hide my infant in it."

The Coptic carpenter decided to report this news to the executioners. He went to them, but such a terror overcame his heart that his tongue ceased to function. He could only gesture with his hands and wanted to convey the matter through signs. The officers, who apparently interpreted his movements as a kind of mockery and derision, beat him and threw him out.

When he came out, he regained his normal state. This incident was repeated, and as a result, he realized that a Divine secret lay hidden here. He built the chest and delivered it to the mother of Moses.

Perhaps it was an early morning when the eyes of the people of Egypt were still asleep and the air had just begun to brighten. The mother brought her infant along with the chest to the bank of the Nile. She placed her breast in the infant's mouth and gave him the last milk, then she placed him in that special chest—which, like a small boat, was capable of moving on the water—and set it upon the waves.

The roaring waves of the Nile soon carried the chest away from the shore. The mother stood by the side and watched this scene. In a moment, she felt as if her heart had been separated from her and was moving upon the waves. Had Divine grace not steadied her heart, she would have cried out and everything would have been revealed!

No one can accurately depict the state of this mother in those sensitive moments, but a certain Persian poetess has visualized this scene to some extent in her beautiful and soulful verses, saying:

When the mother of Moses, following the word of the Sublime Lord, cast Moses into the Nile, She looked from the shore with regret and said: "O innocent little child! If God's grace forgets you, how will you escape from this captainless boat!" The revelation came: "What vain thought is this! Our traveler is now at his destination! We have taken what you cast away! You saw the Hand of Truth but did not recognize it! The surface of the water is better than his cradle; the flood is his nurse and the wave is his mother! The rivers do not overflow of their own accord; they do what We command! We give the command of the storm to the sea; We give the order to the flood and the wave! The pattern of existence is a design from Our palace; dust, wind, and water are Our wanderers. Better that you return and entrust him to Us; when would you ever love him more than We do?!" [^1]

All this was on one side, but let us see what was happening in Pharaoh's palace. It is mentioned in reports: Pharaoh had a daughter, and she was his only child. She suffered from a severe illness. He sought help from physicians but achieved no result. He turned to the priests, and they said: "O Pharaoh, we predict that from within this river, a human will step into this palace, and if they rub his saliva on the body of this patient, she will recover!" Pharaoh and his wife Asiya were waiting for such an event when suddenly one day, a small chest moving on the waves caught their attention. He ordered the officers to immediately go for the chest and take it from the water to see what was inside.

The mysterious chest was placed before Pharaoh. Others were unable to open its lid. Indeed, the lid of the chest of Moses' salvation had to be opened by Pharaoh's own hand, and it was opened! When the eyes of Pharaoh's wife met the eyes of the child, a spark leapt from them and illuminated the depths of her heart, and everyone—especially Pharaoh's wife—took a love for him in their hearts. And when the saliva of this infant became the means of healing the patient, this love increased. [^1] Now we return to the Quran and hear the summary of this event from the language of the Quran:

[^1]: From the Divan of "Parvin E'tesami".

The Quran says: "Then the household of Pharaoh picked him up (from the waves of the Nile), that he might become to them an enemy and a cause of grief!" (فَالْتَقَطَهُ آلُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَ حَزَناً).

"Iltiqat" (التقاط) originally means to come upon something without effort or striving, and the reason why lost items that a person finds are called "Luqatah" (لقطة) is for this same reason.

It is obvious that the Pharaonists did not take this infant's swaddle from the waves for the purpose of nurturing their fierce enemy in their own embrace; rather, as Pharaoh's wife said, they wanted to choose a "comfort for the eye" for themselves.

But the end and the consequence of the matter became such, and in the terminology of literary scholars, the "Lam" (ل) here is the "Lam of consequence" (Lam al-Aqibah), not the "Lam of cause" (Lam al-Illah). The elegance of this expression lies in the fact that Allah wants to show His power: how He compels this group—who had mobilized all their forces to kill the sons of the Israelites—to embrace and nurture like a sweet soul the very person for whose destruction all those preparations were made!

Furthermore, the expression "the household of Pharaoh" (Al-i Fir'awn) shows that not just one person, but a group of Pharaonists participated in taking the chest from the water, and this is evidence that they were expecting such a thing.

And at the end of the verse, It adds: "Surely Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were wrongdoers" (إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ هامانَ وَ جُنُودَهُما كانُوا خاطِئِينَ‌).

They were wrongdoers in everything. What error is greater than leaving the path of truth and justice and basing the foundations of their government on oppression, injustice, and polytheism?

And what error is clearer than this: they beheaded thousands of infants to destroy the Kalim-Allah (the one who spoke with Allah), but Allah entrusted him to their own hands and said: "Take him and nurture and raise this enemy of yours!" [^1]

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: The difference between Khati' (خاطئ) and Mukhti' (مخطئ) is that Khati' refers to someone who undertakes a task they cannot handle well and follows the path of "error" (Khata'), whereas Mukhti' refers to someone who undertakes a task they handle well but happens to make a mistake and ruins it.

From the next verse, it is understood that a dispute and conflict had broken out between Pharaoh and his wife, and possibly some of those around them, over this infant, for the Quran says: "And Pharaoh's wife said: '(He is) a comfort of the eye for me and for you. Do not kill him; perhaps he may be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son!'" (وَ قالَتِ امْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَ لَكَ لا تَقْتُلُوهُ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَنْفَعَنا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَداً).

It seems that Pharaoh, from the infant's face and other signs—including his being placed in a chest and released into the waves of the Nile—had realized that this infant was from the Israelites. Suddenly, the nightmare of the uprising of an Israelite man and the downfall of his kingdom at that man's hand cast a shadow over his soul, and he demanded the execution of his criminal law regarding Israelite infants in this case!

The sycophantic and flattering courtiers also encouraged Pharaoh in this way of thinking and said there was no reason why the law should not be executed regarding this child. But Asiya, Pharaoh's wife, who had no son and whose pure heart—which was not of the same fabric as Pharaoh's courtiers—had become a center of love for this infant, stood against all of them. And since in such family conflicts victory often belongs to women, she was victorious in her endeavor.

And if the story of the healing of Pharaoh's daughter is added to it, the reason for Asiya's victory in this conflict will become clearer.

But the Quran says with one short and meaningful sentence at the end of the verse: "And they perceived not" (وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

Yes, they did not know that the effective Divine command and the invincible Will of Allah had determined to nurture this infant in the most significant center of danger, and no one has the power to oppose this Will and Determination!

Key Point: The Wondrous Divine Plan

A display of power is not that if Allah wishes to destroy a powerful and tyrannical people, He mobilizes the hosts of the heavens and the earth for their destruction.

A display of power is to make those very arrogant tyrants the agents of their own destruction, and to influence their hearts and thoughts in such a way that they eagerly gather the wood with whose fire they must burn, build the prison in which they must die, and erect the gallows upon which they must be executed!

And so it was in the case of the powerful, rebellious Pharaonists; the nurturing and salvation of Moses at all stages took place at their own hands:

The midwife of Moses was from the Copts! The maker of the chest for Moses' salvation was a Coptic carpenter! The ones who took the chest of salvation from the waves of the Nile were the "household of Pharaoh"! The one who opened the lid of the chest was Pharaoh himself or his wife Asiya! And finally, the center of safety, peace, and the nurturing of the heroic and Pharaoh-shattering Moses was that very palace of Pharaoh! And this is the display of the Lord's power.

Ayahs

وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤَادُ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ فَـٰرِغًا ۖ إِن كَادَتْ لَتُبْدِى بِهِۦ لَوْلَآ أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠)
The heart of Moses’ mother became desolate, and indeed she was about to divulge it had We not fortified her heart so that she might have faith [in Allah’s promise]. (10)
وَقَالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِۦ قُصِّيهِ ۖ فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِۦ عَن جُنُبٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١١)
She said to his sister, ‘Follow him.’ So she watched him from a distance, while they were not aware. (11)
۞ وَحَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَرَاضِعَ مِن قَبْلُ فَقَالَتْ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ يَكْفُلُونَهُۥ لَكُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ نَـٰصِحُونَ (١٢)
Since before We had forbidden him to be suckled by any nurse. So she said, ‘Shall I show you a household that will take care of him for you and they will be his well-wishers?’ (12)
فَرَدَدْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّهِۦ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣)
That is how We restored him to his mother so that she might be comforted and not grieve, and that she might know that Allah’s promise is true, but most of them do not know. (13)

Commentary

Moses’ Return to His Mother’s Bosom

In these verses, another scene of this story is depicted:

The mother of Moses عليه السلام entrusted her child to the waves of the Nile in the manner we previously described. However, after this event, a violent storm began to blow within her heart; the void left by the infant, who had filled her entire heart, was deeply felt.

She was on the verge of crying out and revealing the secrets of her heart.

She was close to screaming and wailing over the separation from her child.

But Divine Grace came to her aid, and as the Quran states: "The heart of the mother of Moses became void [of everything except the thought of her son]. Had We not strengthened her heart [with the light of faith and hope] so that she might remain of the believers, she would have nearly disclosed the matter" (وَ أَصْبَحَ فُؤادُ أُمِّ مُوسى‌ فارِغاً إِنْ كادَتْ لَتُبْدِي بِهِ لَوْ لا أَنْ رَبَطْنا عَلى‌ قَلْبِها لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

"Farigh" (فارغ) means empty, and here it refers to being empty of everything except the memory of Moses, although some commentators have interpreted it as being empty of grief and sorrow, or empty of the inspiration and glad tidings previously given to her. However, considering the context of the sentences, these interpretations do not seem correct.

It is entirely natural for a mother who separates from her infant in such a manner to forget everything except her baby, and to lose her composure to the point that, without considering the dangers threatening herself and her child, she might cry out and reveal the inner secrets.

But Allah, who assigned this heavy mission to this kind mother, strengthened her heart so that she would have faith in the Divine promise and know that her child is in the hand of God, and will eventually return to her and become a Prophet! "Rabata" (ربطنا) is from the root "rabt" (ربط), which originally means tying an animal or the like to a place so it remains securely kept, and thus the place for such animals is called a "ribat" (رباط). Subsequently, it came to have a broader meaning of preservation, reinforcement, and strengthening. The meaning of "strengthening the heart" (ربط قلب) here is the fortification of this mother's heart so that she would believe in the Divine revelation and endure this great event.

Through this grace of the Lord, the mother regained her composure, but she wanted to know about her son's fate. Therefore, "she said to his sister: 'Follow him'" (وَ قالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ‌). "Qussih" (قصيه) is from the root "qass" (قص) meaning to search for the traces of something. The reason a story is called a "qissah" (قصه) is that it involves following various news and events.

The sister of Moses carried out her mother's instruction and searched from a significant distance, "and she saw him from afar [as Pharaoh's people were taking his ark of salvation from the water and holding Moses in their arms after taking him out]" (فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِ عَنْ جُنُبٍ‌).

"But they were unaware [of this sister's situation]" (وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

Some have said: Pharaoh's special servants had brought the child out of the palace to search for a wet nurse for him, and it was exactly at these moments that Moses' sister saw her brother from a distance.

However, the first interpretation seems closer. Thus, after Moses' mother returned to her home, the sister monitored the situation from a distance along the Nile and saw with her own eyes how Pharaoh's people took him from the water and how he was saved from the great danger that threatened the infant.

Other interpretations have been given for the phrase "they were unaware" (هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ). The late Tabarsi specifically considers it not unlikely that the repetition of this phrase in the previous verses and here regarding Pharaoh is to point to the fact: how could he, who was so unaware of matters, claim divinity? How did he intend to fight against the will and providence of the Lord?

In any case, Allah's will was set upon returning this infant to his mother quickly to soothe her heart. Therefore, He says: "And We had already forbidden all foster mothers to him" (وَ حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِ الْمَراضِعَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌) [1]. Naturally, after a few hours, a nursing infant becomes hungry, cries, and becomes restless; a wet nurse must be found for him, especially since the Queen of Egypt is deeply attached to him and loves him like her own sweet soul! The officers set out and searched everywhere for a wet nurse, but strangely, he would not take the breast of any nurse. Perhaps he was terrified by their appearance, or the taste of their milk, which was unfamiliar to his palate, seemed bitter and undesirable. It was as if he wanted to throw himself from the laps of the wet nurses. This was the same Divine ontological prohibition (takhwini) that had made all wet nurses forbidden to him.

[1]: "Maradi'" (مراضع) is the plural of "murdi'" (مرضع) meaning a nursing woman. Some have also considered it the plural of "marda'" (مرضع) meaning the place of nursing, i.e., the breast. It has also been suggested that it is a mim-infinitive meaning "rada'" (nursing), but the first meaning is more appropriate.

The child becomes hungrier and more restless by the moment. He cries continuously, and his cries echo through Pharaoh's palace, causing the Queen's heart to tremble.

The officers increase their efforts. Suddenly, at a short distance, they encounter a girl who "says: 'Shall I direct you to a household who will take care of him for you and who will be sincere towards him?'" (فَقالَتْ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى‌ أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ يَكْفُلُونَهُ لَكُمْ وَ هُمْ لَهُ ناصِحُونَ‌).

I know a woman from the Israelites who has breasts full of milk and a heart full of love; she has lost her own infant and is willing to undertake the nursing of the palace's infant.

The officers were delighted and took Moses' mother to Pharaoh's palace. When the infant smelled his mother's scent, he firmly pressed her breast in his mouth, and from the essence of his mother's soul, he found new life. A spark of joy leapt from their eyes; especially the exhausted officers, who had reached their goal, were the happiest of all. Pharaoh's wife also could not hide her joy at this matter.

Perhaps they said to the wet nurse: "Where were you while we searched so much for you? If only you had come sooner! Blessings upon you and your problem-solving milk!" In some narrations, it is mentioned that when Moses accepted this mother's breast, Haman, Pharaoh's minister, said: "I think you are his real mother! Why, among all these women, did he only accept your breast?" She replied: "O King! It is because I am a pleasant-smelling woman and my milk is very sweet; until now, no child has been entrusted to me without accepting my breast!" Those present confirmed this statement, and each gave her a valuable gift and offering! [1] In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said: "It did not take more than three days for Allah to return the infant to his mother!" Some have said this ontological prohibition of others' milk for Moses was because God did not want this pure Prophet to drink from milk tainted by the forbidden, tainted by stolen property, crime, bribery, and the usurpation of others' rights. He had to be nourished by pure milk like his mother's milk so that he could rise against impurities and fight against the impure.

And thus, "We restored him to his mother so that her eye might be comforted and she might not grieve, and that she might know that the promise of Allah is true, though most of them do not know" (فَرَدَدْناهُ إِلى‌ أُمِّهِ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَ لا تَحْزَنَ وَ لِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌) [1]. Here a question arises: Did the Pharaohs entrust Moses to his mother to nurse him, and during this time, she would bring the child to Pharaoh's court every day or occasionally so the Queen of Egypt could see him, or did they keep the child in the court and the mother of Moses came at specific intervals to nurse him? We have no clear evidence for either of these two possibilities, but the first possibility seems closer.

Also, after the nursing period ended, was Moses moved to Pharaoh's palace, or did he maintain his relationship with his mother and family and move back and forth between the two?

Some have said that after the nursing period, she entrusted him to Pharaoh and his wife Asiya, and Moses was raised in their laps and by their hands. Here, other stories of Moses' childhood but meaningful actions toward Pharaoh are narrated, the mention of all of which would be lengthy. However, the sentence that Pharaoh said to Moses after he was commissioned with prophethood: "Did we not bring you up among us as a child, and did you not remain among us for years of your life?" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 18) shows that Moses lived in Pharaoh's palace for a period and stayed there for years.

From the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is understood that Moses remained in Pharaoh's palace with the utmost respect until the age of maturity, but his monotheistic words greatly upset Pharaoh, to the point that he decided to kill him. Moses left the palace and entered the city, where he encountered a fight between two men, one an Egyptian (Copt) and the other an Israelite (the details of which will come in the following verses). [1]

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 24, p. 231. [1]: Regarding the linguistic root of "taqarra 'aynuha," we have discussed it in detail in Volume 15 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 74 of Surah Al-Furqan. [1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 117.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَٱسْتَوَىٰٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٤)
When he came of age and became fully matured, We gave him judgement and knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. (14)
وَدَخَلَ ٱلْمَدِينَةَ عَلَىٰ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَا فَوَجَدَ فِيهَا رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلَانِ هَـٰذَا مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ وَهَـٰذَا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ ۖ فَٱسْتَغَـٰثَهُ ٱلَّذِى مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ عَلَى ٱلَّذِى مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ فَوَكَزَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ فَقَضَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
[One day] he entered the city at a time when its people were not likely to take notice. He found there two men fighting, this one from among his followers, and that one from his enemies. The one who was from his followers sought his help against him who was from his enemies. So Moses hit him with his fist, whereupon he expired. He said, ‘This is of Satan’s doing. Indeed he is clearly a misguiding enemy.’ (15)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى فَٱغْفِرْ لِى فَغَفَرَ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦)
He said, ‘My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me!’ So He forgave him. Indeed, He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (16)
قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty.’ (17)

Commentary

Musa in the Path of Supporting the Oppressed

Here, we encounter the third part of the eventful life of Musa عليه السلام, which discusses matters related to his period of maturity before he departed from Egypt for Midian, and the motives behind his migration.

First, it says: "And when he reached his full strength and became mature, We gave him wisdom and knowledge. And thus do We reward the doers of good" (وَ لَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَ اسْتَوى‌ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

Ashudd is derived from the root shiddah, meaning to become strong, and istawa is from the root istiwa, meaning the perfection and balance of creation.

Commentators have various discussions regarding the difference between these two:

Some have said that "reaching ashudd" is when a person reaches the peak of physical strength, which often occurs at the age of 18, and istiwa is that very balance and stability in the affairs of life which is usually achieved after the perfection of physical strength.

Others have considered "reaching ashudd" to mean physical perfection, and istiwa to mean intellectual and mental perfection.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrated in the book Ma'ani al-Akhbar, we read: "Ashudd is 18 years, and istiwa is the time when the beard appears."

There is not much difference between these expressions; from the collection of them, considering the linguistic meaning of these two words, the perfection of physical, intellectual, and spiritual powers is understood.

The difference between hukm (wisdom/judgment) and ‘ilm (knowledge) might be that hukm refers to intellect, understanding, and the power of correct judgment, while ‘ilm means awareness and knowledge that is not accompanied by ignorance.

The expression "وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌" (And thus do We reward the doers of good) clearly shows that Musa عليه السلام, due to his God-wariness (taqwa) and his good and pure deeds, had gained the merit for Allah to grant him the reward of knowledge and wisdom. It is clear that this knowledge and wisdom do not refer to revelation (wahy) and prophethood, as Musa was still far from the time of revelation and prophethood at that stage.

Rather, it refers to that awareness, insight, and power of correct judgment and the like, which God gave to Musa as a result of his chastity, integrity, and righteousness. From this expression, it can be generally inferred that while Musa was in Pharaoh's palace, he never took on the color of that environment and rushed to the aid of truth and justice as much as he could, although the details of that are not clear to us today.

In any case, "Musa عليه السلام entered the city at a time when its people were heedless" (وَ دَخَلَ الْمَدِينَةَ عَلى‌ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ أَهْلِها). Which city was this? It is not clear, but most likely it was the capital of Egypt. According to some commentators, due to his increasing opposition to Pharaoh and his administration, Musa had been sentenced to exile from the capital, but he took advantage of a specific opportunity when the people were heedless to enter the capital.

There is also the possibility that it means entering the city from Pharaoh's palace, as the palaces of the Pharaohs were usually built on the edge of the city so they could better control the entry and exit routes.

The meaning of the phrase "عَلى‌ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ أَهْلِها" (at a time of heedlessness of its people) was a time when the people of the city had closed their businesses and no one was closely monitoring the city's affairs. But when was this? Some have said it was at the beginning of the night when people close their businesses; some head home, and some engage in recreation, entertainment, and night gatherings. This is the same hour that is referred to in some Islamic narrations as the "hour of heedlessness" (sa'at al-ghaflah).

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said: "Perform supererogatory prayers (tanaffalu) in the hour of heedlessness, even if it is two brief units (rak'ahs)." And at the end of this hadith, it says: "And the hour of heedlessness is between Maghrib and Isha." [1]

Truly, this hour is an hour of heedlessness, and many crimes, wicked acts, and moral deviations are committed during these early hours of the night. People are neither busy with business nor asleep and resting; rather, a state of general heedlessness usually dominates the cities, and the activity of centers of corruption also flourishes during this hour.

Some have also suggested that the "hour of heedlessness" is midday during the people's rest and the temporary closure of daily work, but the first interpretation seems more correct and precise.

In any case, Musa entered the city and there he encountered a scene: "He found two men fighting; one was of his own party (Shi'a) and followers, and the other was of his enemies" (فَوَجَدَ فِيها رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلانِ هذا مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ وَ هذا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ‌).

The expression "شِيعَتِهِ" (his party/Shi'a) shows that Musa had already established connections with the Israelites by that time and had a group of followers, and he had likely chosen them as a core cell for the struggle against Pharaoh's tyrannical apparatus.

When the Israelite man saw Musa, "he who was of his party asked Musa (who was a strong and powerful young man) for help against him who was of his enemies" (فَاسْتَغاثَهُ الَّذِي مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ عَلَى الَّذِي مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ‌).

Musa عليه السلام rushed to his aid to save him from the clutches of this cruel enemy—who some say was one of Pharaoh's cooks and wanted to force the Israelite man into corvée labor to carry firewood. "At this point, Musa struck the Pharaonic man with a firm fist, but that single blow finished him; he fell to the ground and died" (فَوَكَزَهُ مُوسى‌ فَقَضى‌ عَلَيْهِ‌). [1] Undoubtedly, Musa did not intend to kill the Pharaonic man, and this meaning is clearly evident from the subsequent verses; not because they did not deserve death, but because of the consequences this act could have for Musa and the Israelites.

Therefore, Musa immediately "said: This is of Satan's doing; indeed, he is an enemy, a manifest misleader" (قالَ هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُضِلٌّ مُبِينٌ‌).

In other words, he wanted to separate the Pharaonic man's hand from the Israelite's collar; although the Pharaonic group deserved more than this, under those conditions, taking such an action was not prudent. As we shall see, this very incident caused him to be unable to stay in Egypt any longer, and he set out for Midian.

Then the Quran quotes Musa as saying: "He said: My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, so forgive me. So He forgave him; indeed He is the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful" (قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي فَغَفَرَ لَهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ‌). Certainly, Musa did not commit a sin here; rather, in reality, a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) occurred on his part, in that he should not have been so incautious as to fall into trouble, hardship, and suffering. He asked God for forgiveness for this very tark al-awla, and God included him in His grace.

"Musa said: My Lord, for the favor You have bestowed upon me (by including me in Your forgiveness and not letting me fall into the clutches of enemies), and for all the favors You have granted me from the beginning until now, I will never be a supporter of the criminals" (قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ‌). Rather, I will always rush to the aid of the oppressed and the suffering. His meaning by this sentence was that I will never cooperate with the criminal and sinful Pharaohs, but will be on the side of the oppressed Israelites. The suggestion by some that "criminal" referred to the Israelite man seems very unlikely.

Key Points

1- Is this act of Musa contrary to the station of Infallibility (Ismah)?

Commentators have had extensive discussions regarding the quarrel between the Coptic man and the Israelite and the killing of the Copt at the hand of Musa.

Of course, the essence of this act was not a major issue, as the Pharaonic criminals were ruthless evildoers who had beheaded thousands of Israelite infants and did not hesitate to commit any crime against the Israelites. Thus, they were not individuals whose blood was respected, especially by the Israelites.

What has created a problem for the scholars of commentary are the expressions Musa عليه السلام himself uses in this incident:

In one place he says: "هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ‌" (This is of Satan's doing). In another place he says: "رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي‌" (My Lord! I have wronged myself, so forgive me).

How are these expressions compatible with the infallibility of the Prophets, who must possess the station of infallibility even before their prophethood and mission?

However, with the explanation we gave in the commentary of the above verses, it becomes clear that what occurred from Musa was nothing more than a tark al-awla (leaving the better). By this act, he put himself in hardship, because the killing of a Copt by Musa was not something the Pharaohs would easily overlook. We know that tark al-awla means an act that is not inherently forbidden, but causes a better act to be left undone, without any transgression being committed.

A similar expression appears in the story of some other Prophets, including Prophet Adam عليه السلام, the explanation of which was given under verse 19 of Surah Al-A'raf (Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 122 onwards).

In a hadith in ‘Uyun al-Akhbar from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of these verses, we read: "The meaning of the phrase 'هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ‌' was the dispute and quarrel between those two men, which was considered a satanic act, not the act of Musa. And the meaning of the phrase 'رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي‌' is that I placed myself where I should not have; I should not have entered this city. And the meaning of the phrase 'فَاغْفِرْ لِي‌' is: hide me from Your enemies so they do not get hold of me (one of the meanings of ghufran is to cover/hide)." [1]

2- Supporting criminals is among the greatest sins

In Islamic jurisprudence, we have an extensive chapter regarding "assisting in sin" (i'anat 'ala al-ithm) and "assisting oppressors" (mu'awanat al-zalama) with numerous hadiths showing that one of the ugliest sins is helping oppressors, tyrants, and criminals, which causes a person to share in their grim fate.

Fundamentally, oppressors, tyrants, and individuals like Pharaoh are specific individuals in any society; if the masses of the population do not cooperate with them, Pharaohs do not become Pharaohs. It is a group of weak, base, or opportunistic and world-loving people who surround them and become their hands and wings, or at least their "black army" (numerical strength), until they provide that satanic power for them.

In the Holy Quran, this Islamic and human principle is repeatedly emphasized. In the second verse of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read: "وَ تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَ التَّقْوى‌ وَ لا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ‌" (Cooperate in righteousness and piety, and do not cooperate in sin and aggression).

The Quran explicitly says: "Leaning toward the oppressors causes the punishment of the Fire of Hell" (وَ لا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ - Hud: 113).

Whether rukon (leaning) means an internal inclination of the heart, or outward cooperation, or expressing satisfaction, or friendship and well-wishing, or obedience—each of which commentators have offered as an interpretation—or a concept that encompasses all of these, which is reliance, trust, and dependency, it is a living witness to our point.

In a hadith from Imam Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read: Addressing Muhammad ibn Muslim al-Zuhri, who was a scholar cooperating with the Umayyad apparatus, especially Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik, after warning him against assisting oppressors, he said in his soul-shaking words:

"Did they not call you and make you a pivot around which the mill of their injustices turns? Did they not make you a bridge to cross over to their calamities, a ladder to their misguidance, a caller to their error, and a traveler on their path? Through you, they cast doubt upon the scholars and lead the hearts of the ignorant toward themselves! ... How little they gave you compared to what they took from you! And how trivial is what they built for you compared to what they destroyed of yours! So look to yourself, for no one else will look to it, and hold it to account like a man who is responsible!" [1]

Truly, this eloquent and resonant logic of the Imam عليه السلام can make any court-affiliated or dependent scholar realize their grim end.

Ibn Abbas says: The verse "رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ‌" is among the verses that testify that supporting criminals is a crime and a sin, and helping the believers is obedience to God's command.

It was said to one of the scholars: "So-and-so has become a scribe for such-and-such oppressor, and the only thing he writes is his income and expenses; if he takes a salary for this work, his life will be provided for, otherwise he and his family will be severely afflicted by poverty."

The scholar, in response to this question, only stated this sentence: "Have you not heard the words of that righteous man (Musa) who said: 'رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ‌' (My Lord, for the favors You have bestowed upon me, I will never be a supporter of the criminals)?" [2]

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 5, p. 249 (Chapter 20 of the chapters on the remaining recommended prayers). [1]: Wakazahu is from the root wakaz (rhyming with ramz), meaning to strike with a fist. Other meanings have been suggested which do not seem correct. [1]: ‘Uyun al-Akhbar, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 119. [1]: Tuhaf al-Uqul, p. 66. [2]: Regarding cooperation and assisting oppressors, we have had two detailed discussions in Volume 4, p. 253 (under verse 2 of Al-Ma'idah) and Volume 9, p. 261 (under verse 113 of Hud).

Ayahs

فَأَصْبَحَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَنصَرَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّكَ لَغَوِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٨)
He rose at dawn in the city, fearful and vigilant, when behold, the one who had sought his help the day before, shouted for his help [once again]. Moses said to him, ‘You are indeed clearly perverse!’ (18)
فَلَمَّآ أَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَبْطِشَ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ عَدُوٌّ لَّهُمَا قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَتُرِيدُ أَن تَقْتُلَنِى كَمَا قَتَلْتَ نَفْسًۢا بِٱلْأَمْسِ ۖ إِن تُرِيدُ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ جَبَّارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا تُرِيدُ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ (١٩)
But when he wanted to strike him who was an enemy of both of them, he said, ‘Moses, do you want to kill me, just like the one you killed yesterday? You only want to be a tyrant in the land, and you do not desire to be one who brings about reform.’ (19)
وَجَآءَ رَجُلٌ مِّنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱلْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ فَٱخْرُجْ إِنِّى لَكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٢٠)
There came a man from the city outskirts, hurrying. He said, ‘Moses! The elite are indeed conspiring to kill you. So leave. I am indeed your well-wisher.’ (20)
فَخَرَجَ مِنْهَا خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢١)
So he left the city, fearful and vigilant. He said, ‘My Lord! Deliver me from the wrongdoing lot.’ (21)
وَلَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقَآءَ مَدْيَنَ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِى سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٢٢)
And when he turned his face toward Midian, he said, ‘Maybe my Lord will show me the right way.’ (22)

Commentary

Moses Secretly Departs for Midian

In these verses, we encounter the fourth scene of this eventful story.

The news of the killing of one of the Pharaoh’s men quickly spread throughout Egypt. It was perhaps more or less evident from the circumstances that his killer was an Israelite man, and the name of Moses (peace be upon him) might have been on people's lips.

Of course, this killing was not a simple homicide; it was considered a spark for a revolution or a prelude to it. The government apparatus could not easily overlook the fact that the Israelite slaves intended to take the lives of their masters! Therefore, in the first verse, we read: "Following this incident, Moses was in the city, fearful and vigilant, expecting an event at any moment, and searching for news" (فَأَصْبَحَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ خائِفاً يَتَرَقَّبُ‌) [1].

[1]: "Yataratqabu" is derived from the root "taratqub," meaning to wait or expect. Here, Moses was waiting for the consequences of this incident and also for news regarding it. Grammatically, this phrase serves as a second predicate (khabar) after the first, although some have suggested it might be a circumstantial clause (hal) after another, but this is very unlikely.

Suddenly, he was faced with a new scene: "And he saw the same Israelite who had sought his help yesterday crying out and asking him for help" (as he was grappling with another Copt) (فَإِذَا الَّذِي اسْتَنْصَرَهُ بِالْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُ‌) [1]. However, "Moses said to him: You are clearly an ignorant and misguided person!" (قالَ لَهُ مُوسى‌ إِنَّكَ لَغَوِيٌّ مُبِينٌ‌).

Every day you grapple with someone, creating trouble and engaging in actions for which now is not the time. We are still suffering from the consequences of yesterday's program, and today you have renewed it!

Nevertheless, he was an oppressed person caught in the clutches of an oppressor (whether he was at fault in the preliminaries or not), and Moses had to rush to his aid and not leave him alone. "But when Moses intended to seize the Copt—who was an enemy to both of them—with power and defend the Israelite, the Copt cried out and said: O Moses, do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday?" (فَلَمَّا أَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَبْطِشَ بِالَّذِي هُوَ عَدُوٌّ لَهُما قالَ يا مُوسى‌ أَ تُرِيدُ أَنْ تَقْتُلَنِي كَما قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً بِالْأَمْسِ‌).

Apparently, "You only wish to be a tyrant in the land, and you do not wish

[1]: "Yastasrikhuhu" is from the root "istasrakh," meaning to seek help, but originally it means to cry out or to request another to cry out, which is usually associated with seeking help.

to be among the reformers!" (إِنْ تُرِيدُ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ جَبَّاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما تُرِيدُ أَنْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِينَ‌) [1]. This sentence shows that Moses had previously expressed his reformist intentions both inside Pharaoh's palace and outside it. In some narrations, we read that he had confrontations with Pharaoh in this regard. Thus, the Copt says: You want to kill a person every day; what kind of reformism is this?! Whereas, if Moses had intended to kill this tyrant as well, it would have been a step in the direction of reform.

In any case, Moses realized that yesterday's incident had been exposed, and to avoid further complications, he desisted.

The news reached Pharaoh and his entourage, and they perceived the repetition of this act as a threat to their status. They held a consultative meeting, and the death sentence for Moses was issued. At this moment, an unexpected event saved Moses from certain death: "A man came running from the farthest part of the city (from the center of the Pharaohs and Pharaoh's palace) and said: O Moses, the assembly is consulting to kill you; leave the city immediately, for I am among your sincere advisers" (وَ جاءَ رَجُلٌ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعى‌ قالَ يا مُوسى‌ إِنَّ الْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ فَاخْرُجْ إِنِّي لَكَ مِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ‌).

This man was apparently the same person who later became known as the "Believer of the People of Pharaoh."

[1]: A group of commentators believe that this sentence was spoken by the Israelite man, as he imagined Moses intended to kill him, but there are numerous indications in the verse that refute this meaning.

It is said his name was "Hezekiah" and he was considered a close relative of Pharaoh, having such a relationship with them that he participated in such meetings.

He suffered from the state of Pharaoh's crimes and was waiting for an uprising to take place against him so that he could join this divine movement. Apparently, he had fixed his hopes on Moses and saw in his face the features of a divine revolutionary man. For this reason, when he felt Moses was in danger, he rushed to him and saved him from the clutches of peril. We will see later that he was a support for Moses عليه السلام not only in this incident but in other events as well, serving as a keen eye for the Israelites within Pharaoh's palace.

Moses took this news very seriously, valued the goodwill of this man of faith, and following his recommendation, "he left the city while he was fearful and vigilant, expecting an event at any moment" (فَخَرَجَ مِنْها خائِفاً يَتَرَقَّبُ‌). He turned his entire heart toward his Lord and, to solve this great problem, reached out to His grace and "said: My Lord, save me from the wrongdoing people" (قالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

I know they are oppressive and ruthless, and I rose to defend the oppressed and was a stranger to the oppressors. Just as I have, to the best of my ability, curtailed the evil of the oppressors from the oppressed, You too, O Great God, repel the evil of the oppressors from me.

Moses decided to head toward the land of "Midian," which was a city south of Sham (Levant) and north of Hijaz, considered outside the territory of Egypt and the rule of the Pharaohs. However, the situation of a young man who was raised in luxury and ease, embarking on a journey unprecedented in his life—without provisions, without a mount, without a friend or guide, and constantly fearing that the officials might arrive, capture him, and kill him—is clear.

Yes, Moses had to pass through a period of hardship and intensity, to emerge from the webs that Pharaoh's palace had spun around his personality, to stand beside the downtrodden (Mustad'afin), to feel their pain with his entire being, and to become prepared for a divine uprising in their favor and against the arrogant (Mustakbirin).

But on this path, he carried a great asset: the asset of faith and trust in Allah! Therefore, "when he turned his face toward Midian, he said: I hope that my Lord will guide me to the right path" (وَ لَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقاءَ مَدْيَنَ قالَ عَسى‌ رَبِّي أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِي سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ‌) [1].

[1]: "Tilqa'" is an infinitive or a noun of place, and here it means direction or side.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَآءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ وَوَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمُ ٱمْرَأَتَيْنِ تَذُودَانِ ۖ قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُمَا ۖ قَالَتَا لَا نَسْقِى حَتَّىٰ يُصْدِرَ ٱلرِّعَآءُ ۖ وَأَبُونَا شَيْخٌ كَبِيرٌ (٢٣)
When he arrived at the well of Midian, he found there a throng of people watering [their flocks], and he found, besides them, two women holding back [their flock]. He said, ‘What is your business?’ They said, ‘We do not water [our flock] until the shepherds have driven out [their flocks], and our father is an aged man.’ (23)
فَسَقَىٰ لَهُمَا ثُمَّ تَوَلَّىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلظِّلِّ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ (٢٤)
So he watered [their flock] for them. Then he withdrew toward the shade and said, ‘My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me!’ (24)
فَجَآءَتْهُ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا تَمْشِى عَلَى ٱسْتِحْيَآءٍ قَالَتْ إِنَّ أَبِى يَدْعُوكَ لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا ۚ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُۥ وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَصَصَ قَالَ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٥)
Then one of the two women approached him, walking bashfully. She said, ‘Indeed my father invites you to pay you the wages for watering [our flock] for us.’ So when he came to him and recounted the story to him, he said, ‘Do not be afraid. You have been delivered from the wrongdoing lot.’ (25)

Commentary

A Single Good Deed Opened the Doors of Blessings for Musa!

Here, we face the "fifth scene" of this story, which is the scene of Musa’s entry into the city of Madyan...
