This pure-hearted young man had been on the road for several days—a path he had never traveled before and with which he had no familiarity. According to some accounts, he was even forced to traverse this path barefoot. It is said he was on the road for eight days, walking so much that his feet became blistered.

To satisfy his hunger, he consumed desert plants and tree leaves, and amidst all these hardships and discomforts, he had only one consolation: that by the grace of his Lord, he had been delivered from the clutches of Pharaonic tyranny.

Gradually, the silhouette of "Madyan" appeared on the horizon, and a wave of tranquility settled upon his heart. As he approached the city, a gathering of people caught his attention. He soon realized they were shepherds gathered around a well to water their flocks.

"And when he arrived at the water of Madyan, he found there a group of people watering [their flocks]" (وَ لَمَّا وَرَدَ ماءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِنَ النَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ‌).

"And he found aside from them two women keeping back [their flocks]" (وَ وَجَدَ مِنْ دُونِهِمُ امْرَأَتَيْنِ تَذُودانِ‌) [1]. The situation of these chaste girls—standing in a corner while no one came to their aid, and a group of burly shepherds thought only of their own sheep and would not give others a turn—caught Musa’s attention. He approached them and "said, 'What is your business?'" (قالَ ما خَطْبُكُما) [2]. Why do you not move forward and water the sheep?

For Musa, this discrimination, oppression, and injustice—this lack of fairness and disregard for the rights of the oppressed which was visible at the very entrance of the city of Madyan—was intolerable. He was a defender of the oppressed, and for this very cause, he had turned his back on Pharaoh’s palace and its luxuries and had become a displaced wanderer from his homeland. He could not abandon his principles and remain silent in the face of injustice.

In response to him, the girls "said: 'We do not water [our flocks] until the shepherds drive [their flocks] away'" and we use the remaining water (قالَتا لا نَسْقِي حَتَّى يُصْدِرَ الرِّعاءُ) [3]. And so that Musa’s unspoken question—as to why the father of these chaste girls would send them for such work—would not remain unanswered, they added: "And our father is a very old man" (وَ أَبُونا شَيْخٌ كَبِيرٌ).

Neither is he himself capable of watering the sheep, nor do we have a brother to bear this burden; and so as not to be a dependent on others, we have no choice but to perform this task ourselves.

[1]: "Tadhudan" (تذودان) is from the root "dhawd" (ذود), meaning to prevent or hold back. They were making sure their sheep did not scatter or mix with other flocks. [2]: "Khatb" (خطب) means business, purpose, or intention. [3]: "Yusdir" (يصدر) is from the root "sadr" (صدر), meaning to leave the watering place, and "ri'a" (رعاء) is the plural of "ra'i" (راعي), meaning shepherd.

Musa was deeply saddened to hear this. What unfair people these are, who think only of themselves and offer the least support to the oppressed! He stepped forward, took the heavy bucket, and cast it into the well—a bucket that they say required several men to pull out. With the strength of his powerful arms, he pulled it out alone and "watered [the flocks] for them" (فَسَقى‌ لَهُما).

It is said: when he approached and pushed the crowd aside, he said to them, "What kind of people are you that you think of no one but yourselves?" The crowd moved aside, gave him the bucket, and said, "Bismillah! If you can, draw the water," for they knew the bucket was so heavy that it could only be pulled from the well by the strength of ten men. They left Musa alone, but although Musa was tired, hungry, and distressed, the power of faith came to his aid, increased his physical strength, and by drawing one bucket from the well, he watered all their sheep.

"Then he turned away to the shade and said, 'My Lord, indeed I am in need of whatever good You would send down to me'" (ثُمَّ تَوَلَّى إِلَى الظِّلِّ فَقالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ).

Yes, he was tired and hungry; he was a stranger in that city, alone and without shelter. Yet, at the same time, he does not show restlessness. He is so polite that even when praying, he does not explicitly say, "O Allah, do such and such," but rather says: "I am in need of whatever good You send down to me," meaning he only expresses his need and leaves the rest to the grace of the Lord.

But look at the power of a good deed! What extraordinary blessings it holds! Taking one step for Allah and drawing one bucket of water from a well to support an unknown oppressed person opens a new chapter in Musa’s life and brings him a world of material and spiritual blessings. It places within his reach that which he might have had to search for for many years.

The beginning of this program was when he noticed that "one of the two [women] came to him, walking with bashfulness," and it was evident she was shy about speaking to a strange young man. She said only this sentence: "Indeed, my father invites you that he may reward you with the wage for having watered [the flocks] for us!" (فَجاءَتْهُ إِحْداهُما تَمْشِي عَلَى اسْتِحْياءٍ قالَتْ إِنَّ أَبِي يَدْعُوكَ لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ ما سَقَيْتَ لَنا).

A spark of hope leaped in his heart. It was as if he felt a significant event was about to unfold and that he would meet a great man—a grateful man who was not even willing to let a human effort, even as small as drawing a bucket of water, go unrewarded. He must be an exemplary human being, a heavenly and divine man. O my God! What a precious opportunity!

Yes, that old man was none other than Shu'ayb عليه السلام, the Prophet of Allah, who for many years had invited the people in this city to Allah and was an exemplar of gratitude and devotion to Truth. Today, seeing his daughters return home earlier than usual, he inquired, and when he learned of the event, he decided to fulfill his debt to this unknown young man, whoever he might be.

Musa set out and came toward the house of Shu'ayb. According to some narrations, the daughter walked ahead to guide him, and Musa walked behind her. The wind blew against the girl's clothes and might have shifted them; the modesty and chastity of Musa عليه السلام would not allow this. He said to the girl, "I will go in front; guide me at the crossroads and junctions." [1] Musa entered the house of Shu'ayb—a house from which the light of prophethood radiated and spirituality was evident everywhere. A dignified old man with white hair sat in a corner and welcomed Musa.

Where do you come from? What is your occupation? What are you doing in this city? What is your goal and purpose? Why are you alone?

And questions of this sort.

Musa recounted his story to Shu'ayb.

The Quran says: "And when he came to him and narrated to him the story, he said, 'Fear not. You have escaped from the wrongdoing people'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُ وَ قَصَّ عَلَيْهِ الْقَصَصَ قالَ لا تَخَفْ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Our land is outside their territory, and they have no access here. Let not the slightest fear enter your heart; you are in a region of safety and security. Do not suffer from being a stranger or alone; everything will be resolved by the grace of Allah.

Musa soon realized he had found a great master from whose being flowed clear springs of knowledge, gnosis, piety, and spirituality, who could well quench his thirst for knowledge.

Shu'ayb also felt he had found a worthy and talented student to whom he could transfer the knowledge and experiences of a lifetime. Indeed, just as much as a student enjoys finding a great master, a master is also happy to find a worthy student.

[1]: "Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," under the verses in question.

Key Points

1- Where was Madyan?

"Madyan" was the name of the city where Shu'ayb and his tribe lived. This city was located east of the Gulf of Aqaba (north of Hijaz and south of the Levant). Its people were descendants of Isma'il and traded with Egypt, Lebanon, and Palestine. Today, the city of Madyan is called "Ma'an."

Some have also applied the name "Madyan" to the people who lived between the Gulf of Aqaba and Mount Sinai. In the Torah, it appears as "Midian" [1]. Some also believe the city was named Madyan because one of the sons of Ibrahim named Madyan lived there [2]. If we look closely at a geographical map, we see that this city was not a great distance from Egypt, and Musa was able to reach it in several days.

Today, on the geographical maps of Jordan, the name Ma'an appears as one of the southwestern cities, which corresponds to the descriptions mentioned above.

2- Many Instructive Lessons

In this part of Musa’s عليه السلام story, there are many instructive lessons:

A- Divine Prophets have always been supporters of the oppressed. Whether Musa was in Egypt or when he came to Madyan, wherever he saw a scene of oppression and tyranny, he became distressed and rushed to the aid of the oppressed. Fundamentally, one of the goals of the mission of the Prophets is this very support for the oppressed.

B- How blessed is the performance of a small deed for Allah! Musa drew one bucket of water from the well and had no motive other than seeking the pleasure of the Creator. But how blessed was this small act? For it became the reason he found his way to the house of Shu'ayb, the great Prophet of Allah, was delivered from being a stranger, found a secure shelter, was granted food, clothing, and a chaste wife, and most importantly, spent ten years in the character-building school of Shu'ayb—that enlightened elder—and became prepared for the leadership of the people.

C- Men of God do not leave any service—especially the service of the hardworking—without reward. For this reason, as soon as Prophet Shu'ayb heard of this unknown young man's service, he did not rest and immediately sent for him to give him his wage.

D- This point is also noteworthy in the life of Musa: he was always mindful of Allah and turned toward His threshold, seeking the solution to every problem from Him.

When he killed the Coptic man and a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) occurred, he immediately asked Allah for pardon and forgiveness, saying: "My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, so forgive me" (رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي).

And when he left Egypt, he said: "My Lord, save me from the wrongdoing people" (رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ).

And when he turned toward the land of Madyan, he said: "I hope my Lord will guide me to the right way" (عَسى‌ رَبِّي أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِي سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ).

And when he watered Shu'ayb’s sheep and rested in the shade, he said: "My Lord! Indeed I am in need of whatever good You would send down to me" (رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ).

Especially this last prayer, which was in the most critical moments of his life, was so polite and accompanied by such tranquility and composure that he did not even say, "O Allah, fulfill my needs," but only said: "I am in need of Your good and bounty."

E- Let it not be thought that Musa only thought of the Lord during hardships; even in Pharaoh’s palace amidst those luxuries, he did not forget Allah. Thus, we read in narrations that one day he sneezed in front of Pharaoh and immediately said, "Alhamdulillah Rabbil 'Alamin" (Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds). Pharaoh was upset to hear this and slapped him, and Musa in turn grabbed and pulled his long beard. Pharaoh became very angry and decided to kill him, but his wife saved him from death on the grounds that he was a young child and unaware of his actions! [3]

[1]: "A'lam al-Quran," page 573. [2]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," vol. 20, page 51. [3]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," vol. 4, page 117.

Ayahs

قَالَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱسْتَـْٔجِرْهُ ۖ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ ٱسْتَـْٔجَرْتَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْأَمِينُ (٢٦)
One of the two women said, ‘Father, hire him. Indeed the best you can hire is a powerful and trustworthy man.’ (26)
قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ٱبْنَتَىَّ هَـٰتَيْنِ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَأْجُرَنِى ثَمَـٰنِىَ حِجَجٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِندِكَ ۖ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ ۚ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٢٧)
He said, ‘Indeed I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years. And if you complete ten, that will be up to you, and I do not want to be hard on you. God willing, you will find me to a righteous person.’ (27)
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ ۖ أَيَّمَا ٱلْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ عَلَىَّ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ (٢٨)
He said, ‘This will be [by consent] between you and me. Whichever of the two terms I complete, there shall be no imposition upon me, and Allah is witness over what we say.’ (28)

Commentary

Musa in the House of Shu’ayb

This is the sixth scene from the life of Musa (peace be upon him) in this great saga.

Musa (peace be upon him) entered the house of Shu’ayb (peace be upon him)—a simple, rural home, pure and brimming with spirituality. After he recounted his life story to Shu’ayb (peace be upon him), one of the daughters spoke up. With a short yet profound statement, she suggested to her father that he hire Musa (peace be upon him) to tend the sheep: "She said: 'O my father! Hire him, for the best person you can hire is the one who is strong and trustworthy.'" He had already proven his strength as well as his purity and integrity (قَالَتْ إِحْدَاهُمَا يَا أَبَتِ اسْتَأْجِرْهُ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ اسْتَأْجَرْتَ الْقَوِيُّ الْأَمِينُ).

A daughter raised in the lap of a great Prophet (peace be upon him) must speak so politely and thoughtfully, fulfilling the requirements of eloquence with the fewest words.

How did this girl know that this young man was both strong and trustworthy, given that she had only seen him for the first time at the well and was not familiar with his background?

The answer to this question is clear: she recognized his strength when he pushed aside the shepherds at the well to secure the rights of the oppressed and single-handedly drew the heavy bucket from the well. His trustworthiness and integrity became apparent when, on the way to Shu’ayb’s house, he was not willing to let a young woman walk in front of him, as the wind might displace her clothing [1].

[1]: This same theme is mentioned in a narration quoted in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim: When Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) asked his daughter, "His strength is evident from drawing the large bucket from the well, but how do you know of his trustworthiness?" she replied, "Because he was unwilling to look at the backs of women" (Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 123).

Furthermore, through the honest account he gave to Shu’ayb (peace be upon him), his strength in struggling against the Egyptians became clear, as did his integrity, for he never compromised with tyrants nor showed them any favor.

At this point, Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) welcomed his daughter's suggestion. Turning to Musa (peace be upon him), he said: "I intend to wed one of these two daughters of mine to you, on the condition that you serve me for eight years" (قَالَ إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنْكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هَاتَيْنِ عَلَىٰ أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمَانِيَ حِجَجٍ) [1]. He then added: "But if you complete ten years, it will be out of your own grace, for I do not wish to impose a hardship upon you" (فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِنْدِكَ). In any case, "I do not want to make things difficult for you, and Allah willing, you will soon find me to be among the righteous" (وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

I am faithful to my covenant and will never be harsh; I will treat you with goodness and kindness.

Regarding this proposal of marriage, the dowry (mahr), and its other specifics, many questions arise which, Allah willing, will be addressed in the discussion on marriage (nikah).

[1]: "Hijaj" is the plural of "hujjah", and "hujjah" means one year, in view of the fact that it was the custom of the Arabs to perform one Hajj every year, a tradition preserved since the time of Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

Musa (peace be upon him), as a sign of agreement and acceptance of this contract, said: "Let this be the agreement between me and you" (قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ).

Of course, "Whichever of the two terms (eight or ten years) I fulfill, there shall be no injustice against me, and I am free in that choice" (أَيَّمَا الْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَانَ عَلَيَّ).

To solidify the matter and seek help through the name of the Lord, he added: "And Allah is Witness over what we say" (وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ).

And thus, with such simplicity, Musa (peace be upon him) became the son-in-law of Shu’ayb (peace be upon him)!

Key Points

1- Two Essential Conditions for Proper Management

In the short sentence uttered by Shu’ayb’s daughter regarding the hiring of Musa (peace be upon him), the most important and fundamental conditions of management were summarized in a general and concise form: Strength and Trustworthiness.

It is obvious that "strength" does not refer solely to physical power, but rather to the capability and power to fulfill the responsibility.

A "strong and trustworthy" physician is one who has sufficient knowledge of his work and complete mastery over it.

A "strong" manager is one who knows his area of mission well, is aware of "motivations," is proficient in "planning," possesses a sufficient share of "innovation," and has the necessary skill in "organizing tasks." He "clarifies goals" and "mobilizes" forces to reach those goals.

At the same time, he must be compassionate, well-wishing, trustworthy, and honest.

Those who rely solely on trustworthiness and purity when assigning responsibilities and tasks are just as mistaken as those who consider having expertise alone sufficient for accepting responsibility.

"Treacherous experts and dishonest knowledgeable ones" cause the same damage as "honest but ignorant and uninformed ones"! If we wish to destroy a country, we should entrust its affairs to one of these two groups: treacherous managers, or pure individuals who are not managers—the result of both is the same! The logic of Islam is that every task must be in the hands of individuals who are strong, capable, and trustworthy, so that the social order may be set right. If we reflect on the causes of the downfall of governments throughout history, we see the primary factor was entrusting work to one of the two aforementioned groups.

Interestingly, in Islamic programs, "knowledge" and "piety" always stand side by side. A Marja' al-Taqlid (Source of Emulation) must be a "mujtahid" and "adil" (just). A judge and a leader must be a "mujtahid" and "adil". (Of course, alongside these two conditions, there are others, but the foundation and base are these two: "knowledge and awareness" coupled with "justice and piety").

2- Answering Several Questions Regarding the Marriage of Shu’ayb’s Daughter to Musa

We mentioned that the above verses have raised many questions, to which we must provide concise answers:

A: From a jurisprudential perspective, is it correct for the daughter who is to be married to not be precisely identified, but rather for it to be said during the marriage formula (sighah), "I marry one of these two daughters to you"?

Answer: It is not certain that the above phrase was said during the execution of the marriage formula; rather, it appears to be a preliminary discussion or "negotiation," so that after Musa’s agreement, the parties could choose one another and then the marriage formula would be recited.

B: Can the "dowry" (mahr) be left vague or uncertain between a lesser and a greater amount?

Answer: The tone of the verse clearly indicates that the actual dowry was eight years of service, and the additional two years was a matter left to the will and desire of Musa (peace be upon him).

C: Fundamentally, can "labor and services" be set as a dowry? And how can one cohabit with such a spouse when the time for paying the full dowry has not yet arrived, and he does not even have the power to pay it all at once?

Answer: There is no evidence for the impermissibility of such a dowry; rather, the general proofs for dowry in our Sharia include anything that has value. It is also not necessary to pay the entire dowry at once; it is sufficient that the whole of it becomes a debt upon the husband and the woman becomes its owner. The principle of well-being and Istishab (presumption of continuity) also dictates that this husband will remain alive and have the ability to perform this service.

D: Fundamentally, how is it possible for service to the father to be made the daughter's dowry? Is the daughter a commodity to be sold for that service? [1]

Answer: Without a doubt, Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) had ascertained his daughter's consent in this matter and had the authority (wakalah) to execute such a contract. In other words, the actual owner of the debt upon Musa (peace be upon him) was the daughter of Shu’ayb herself. However, since their lives were communal and passed with ultimate sincerity and purity, and there was no separation between them (as is still seen today in many traditional or rural families where the life of a family is completely intertwined), the question of how this debt should be paid was not an issue. In short, the owner of the dowry is only the daughter, not the father, and Musa’s services were directed toward this end.

[1]: The late "Muhaqqiq al-Hilli" says in Shara'i': "The contract is valid upon the benefit of a free person, such as teaching a craft or a Surah of the Quran, or any permissible work, and upon the husband hiring himself for a specific period." The great jurist, the author of Jawahir, after mentioning this statement, says: "In accordance with the famous opinion" (Jawahir al-Kalam, Vol. 31, p. 4).

E: The dowry of Shu’ayb’s daughter was a relatively heavy one, for if we calculate the labor of an ordinary worker today over a month and a year and then multiply it by eight, it becomes a significant sum.

Answer: Firstly, this marriage was not a simple marriage; it was a prelude for Musa (peace be upon him) to remain in the school of Shu’ayb (peace be upon him)—a prelude for Musa (peace be upon him) to go through a great university during this long period. Allah knows what Musa (peace be upon him) learned from the "Elder of Madyan" during this time.

Furthermore, if Musa (peace be upon him) worked for Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) during this period, in return, Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) provided for the entire livelihood of Musa and his wife through this very means. Therefore, if we subtract the expenses of Musa and his wife from the wages of this work, not much of a sum will remain, and we will realize it was a simple and light dowry!

3- Additionally, it is inferred from this story that the common practice among us today—where it is considered a flaw for the father or relatives of a girl to propose marriage to a boy—is not correct. There is no obstacle to the girl's relatives finding a person they deem worthy of being their child's spouse and proposing to him, just as Shu’ayb (peace be upon him) did. Similar instances have been seen in the lives of some great figures of Islam.

4- The names of Shu’ayb’s daughters have been recorded as "Saffurah" (or Zipporah) and "Liyya," the former of whom married Musa (peace be upon him) [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 249.

Ayahs

۞ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَجَلَ وَسَارَ بِأَهْلِهِۦٓ ءَانَسَ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ نَارًا قَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ جَذْوَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ (٢٩)
So when Moses completed the term and set out with his family, he descried a fire on the side of the mountain. He said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire! Maybe I will bring you some news from it, or a brand of fire so that you may warm yourselves.’ (29)
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ مِن شَـٰطِئِ ٱلْوَادِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ فِى ٱلْبُقْعَةِ ٱلْمُبَـٰرَكَةِ مِنَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ أَن يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٠)
When he approached it, he was called from the right bank of the valley in that blessed site from the tree: ‘Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (30)
وَأَنْ أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَقْبِلْ وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔامِنِينَ (٣١)
And: ‘Throw down your staff!’ And when he saw it wriggling as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], without looking back. ‘Moses! Come forward, and do not be afraid. Indeed you are safe.’ (31)
ٱسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ وَٱضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَنَاحَكَ مِنَ ٱلرَّهْبِ ۖ فَذَٰنِكَ بُرْهَـٰنَانِ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٣٢)
‘Insert your hand into your shirt. It will emerge white, without any fault, and keep your arms drawn in awe to your sides. These shall be two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his elite. They are indeed a transgressing lot.’ (32)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْسًا فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ (٣٣)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed, I have killed one of their men, so I fear they will kill me. (33)
وَأَخِى هَـٰرُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّى لِسَانًا فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِىَ رِدْءًا يُصَدِّقُنِىٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ (٣٤)
Aaron, my brother—he is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me, for I fear that they will impugn me.’ (34)
قَالَ سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُمَا سُلْطَـٰنًا فَلَا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُمَا ۚ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنتُمَا وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكُمَا ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٣٥)
He said, ‘We will strengthen your arm by means of your brother, and invest both of you with such authority that they will not touch you. With the help of Our signs, you two, and those who follow the two of you, shall be the victors.’ (35)

Commentary

The First Spark of Revelation!

Here we reach the seventh scene of this story:

No one knows exactly what transpired for Musa during these ten years [^1]; however, without a doubt, these ten years were among the best years of Musa's life—years that were pleasant, sweet, and tranquil; years of self-construction and preparation for a great mission.

In truth, it was necessary for Musa (عليه السلام) to spend a ten-year period in exile, alongside a great Prophet, engaged in shepherding. This was so that if the temperament of palace-dwelling had affected his mind or soul, it would be completely washed away. Musa had to be among the dwellers of humble huts, become aware of their pains, and prepare himself to struggle against the palace-dwellers.

On the other hand, Musa needed a long duration of time for contemplating the secrets of creation and for self-purification. Where better than the wilderness of Madyan? And where better than the house of Shu’ayb?

The mission of an "Ulul-’Azm Prophet" (Arch-Prophet) is not a simple task that one can easily undertake. Rather, it can be said that Musa's mission, after that of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), was in one respect the heaviest among the Prophets: to struggle against the greatest tyrants on earth, to end the enslavement of a great nation, and to cleanse the effects of the culture of captivity from their souls is no easy feat.

It is mentioned in the Torah and also in Islamic narrations that Shu’ayb, to show appreciation for Musa's efforts, had agreed to grant him the sheep born with specific markings. Coincidentally, in the final year when Musa intended to bid farewell to Shu’ayb and return to Egypt, all or most of the newborn lambs were born with those very characteristics [^1], and Shu’ayb gave them to Musa with full willingness.

It is obvious that Musa would not be content to remain a shepherd until the end of his life—even though Shu’ayb’s presence was highly precious to him. He had to hasten to the aid of his people, who were trapped in the chains of captivity and submerged in ignorance and unawareness.

He had to put an end to the injustices in Egypt, break the idols, humiliate the tyrants, and honor the oppressed by the help of Allah. An inner feeling encouraged Musa toward this journey.

Finally, he gathered his belongings, goods, and sheep, and prepared for the journey.

Furthermore, from the use of the term "family" (أهل) which appears in several verses of the Quran, it is inferred that Musa had a child or children with him there besides his wife. Islamic narrations also confirm this meaning, and it is explicitly stated in the Torah in the Book of Exodus. Additionally, his wife was pregnant at that time.

Upon his return, he lost his way, perhaps because he was taking a bypass to avoid being caught by the Syrian aggressors.

In any case, the Quran says in the first verse under discussion: "Then when Musa had fulfilled the term and was traveling with his family, he perceived from the direction of the Mount (Tur) a fire!" (فَلَمَّا قَضى‌ مُوسَى الْأَجَلَ وَ سارَ بِأَهْلِهِ آنَسَ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ ناراً).

"He said to his family: 'Stay here; indeed, I have perceived a fire. Perhaps I may bring you from there some information or a burning brand from the fire that you may warm yourselves.'" (قالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ جَذْوَةٍ مِنَ النَّارِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ‌).

"Anasta" (آنست) is from the root "Inas," meaning to observe and see accompanied by a kind of tranquility and intimacy. "Jadhwah" (جذوة) means a piece of fire, and some have said it refers to a large piece of firewood.

From the phrase "bring you from there some information," it is understood that he had lost his way, and from the phrase "that you may warm yourselves," it is gathered that it was a cold and uncomfortable night.

The verse does not mention the condition of Musa's wife, but it is well-known in commentaries and narrations that she was pregnant and at that moment went into labor, and Musa was anxious from this perspective as well.

"When he came to it, (he saw it was a fire unlike other fires, devoid of heat and burning, a singular mass of light and purity. While Musa was deeply immersed in wonder,) suddenly he was called from the right bank of the valley in the blessed spot, from the tree: 'O Musa! Indeed, I am Allah, the Lord of the worlds!'" (فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ أَنْ يا مُوسى‌ إِنِّي أَنَا اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

"Shati’" (شاطئ) means bank or shore, and "Wadi" (وادى) means "valley" or "the path of a flood stream." "Ayman" (ايمن) means right and is an adjective for "Shati’." "Buq’ah" (بقعه) means a piece of land that is distinct from its surroundings.

Undoubtedly, Allah has the power to create sound waves in whatever He wills; here He created them within the tree, because He wished to speak with Musa, and Musa is a physical being possessing ears and in need of sound waves. Of course, many times the Prophets received revelation through inner inspiration, and sometimes in dreams, but occasionally it was through hearing sound waves. In any case, there is absolutely no room for the delusion of attributing a body to Allah.

In some narrations, it is stated that when Musa approached the fire, he looked closely and saw a fire glowing from within a green branch, becoming more radiant and beautiful moment by moment. He leaned forward with a small stick he had in his hand to take some of it; the fire moved toward him! He became frightened and stepped back. Sometimes he would approach the fire and sometimes the fire would approach him, until suddenly a call arose and gave him the glad tidings of revelation. Thus, through undeniable signs, it became clear to Musa that this call was a Divine call and nothing else.

However, considering the great and heavy mission Musa is charged with, great miracles proportionate to it must be placed at his disposal by Allah, two important parts of which are mentioned in these verses:

First: "And [he was told], 'Throw down your staff.' But when he saw it writhing as if it were a snake, he turned in flight and did not look back!" (وَ أَنْ أَلْقِ عَصاكَ فَلَمَّا رَآها تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ وَلَّى مُدْبِراً وَ لَمْ يُعَقِّبْ‌).

On the day Musa chose this staff for himself to lean upon when tired and to shake down leaves for his sheep, he did not believe that such a great power lay hidden within it by the command of Allah, and that this simple shepherd's staff would shake the palaces of the oppressors. Such are the entities of this world: sometimes they seem small in our eyes, but they hold great potentials within that manifest by the command of Allah.

At this moment, Musa heard the call once more saying to him: "O Musa, come forward and do not fear. Indeed, you are of the secure!" (أَقْبِلْ وَ لا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ‌).

"Jann" (جان) originally means a hidden being, and small snakes are called "Jann" because they pass through the grass and crevices of the earth in a hidden manner. Of course, in some other verses of the Quran, the expression "Thub’anun Mubin" (a manifest serpent/dragon) is used (Surah Al-A'raf: 107 and Ash-Shu'ara: 32). As we have said previously, these differences in expression may describe the various states of that snake, which was small at first and then turned into a massive dragon. It is also possible that the first time Musa saw it in the valley of Tur, it was in a smaller form and became larger in subsequent stages.

In any case, Musa had to become acquainted with this truth: that in the presence of the Lord, absolute security prevails; that is not a place for fear or dread.

The first miracle was a sign of awe; then he is commanded to go toward his other miracle, which is a sign of light and hope, and the combination of the two will be a blend of "warning" and "glad tidings." He was commanded: "Insert your hand into the opening of your garment; it will come out white, without disease," (اسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ).

This whiteness and radiance was not due to leprosy or the like; it was a Divine light that was entirely new.

Observing these extraordinary wonders on that dark night and in that empty wilderness shook Musa deeply. To regain his composure, another command was given to him: "And draw your hand [literally: wing] close to your side to be free from fear," (وَ اضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَناحَكَ مِنَ الرَّهْبِ‌).

Some have also said this sentence is a metaphor for the necessity of decisiveness and firm resolve in fulfilling the responsibility of the messengership and not fearing any position or power.

Others have suggested that when the staff turned into a snake, Musa opened his arms to defend himself, but Allah commanded him to fold his arms and not fear—there is no need for defense!

The use of the term "Janah" (wing) instead of hand is a beautiful expression, the purpose of which might be to liken the state of human tranquility to that of a bird which flaps its wings upon seeing something terrifying but folds its wings once it regains its calm.

Then the same call said to Musa: "These are two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his establishment. Indeed, they have been a people defiantly disobedient," (فَذانِكَ بُرْهانانِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ مَلَائِهِ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ‌).

Yes, this group has departed from the obedience of the Lord and has reached the peak of rebellion. It is your duty to advise and counsel them, and if it is not effective, to struggle against them.

At this point, Musa (عليه السلام) remembered the significant event of his life in Egypt: the incident of killing the Coptic man and the mobilization of Pharaoh's forces to retaliate for his blood. Although Musa had grappled with this oppressor to support an oppressed person, this had no meaning in Pharaoh's logic; he still intended to kill Musa without question if he found him.

Therefore, here "He said, 'My Lord, indeed I killed from among them a person, and I fear they will kill me,'" and this mission would remain unfinished (قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْساً فَأَخافُ أَنْ يقتلونِ).

Furthermore, I am alone and my tongue is not that eloquent: "And my brother Harun is more fluent than me in tongue, so send him with me as a support, verifying me. Indeed, I fear that they will deny me," (and this great task will not be completed) (وَ أَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِي رِدْءاً يُصَدِّقُنِي إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُكَذِّبُونِ‌).

"Afsah" (افصح) is from the root "Fasih," which originally means something being pure. Pure and expressive speech that is free from any redundancy is called "Fasih." "Rid’" (ردء) means a helper and assistant.

In any case, since this mission was very great and heavy, and Musa wanted never to face failure, he made this request to the Great Lord.

Allah also accepted his call and gave him sufficient assurance, saying:

"We will strengthen your arm through your brother (Harun)," (قالَ سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ‌).

"And We will grant you both authority and superiority in all stages," (وَ نَجْعَلُ لَكُما سُلْطاناً).

Be completely certain: "they will not reach you; through Our signs," they will not get to you and will not triumph over you (فَلا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُما بِآياتِنا).

Rather, "you and those who follow you will be the predominant," (أَنْتُما وَ مَنِ اتَّبَعَكُمَا الْغالِبُونَ‌).

What a great promise! And what a magnificent glad tiding! A promise and glad tiding that warmed Musa's heart, made his resolve firm, and his will strong and iron-like, the clear effects of which we will see in the future episodes of this story. [^1]

[^1]: It appears from Islamic narrations that Musa worked for Shu’ayb for ten years; this matter is mentioned in a Hadith in the book Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 15, p. 34 (Kitab al-Nikah, Abwab al-Muhur, Chapter 22, Hadith 4). [^1]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 409. [^1]: Regarding these discussions, we have had other discourses in Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh under Surah Ta-Ha, in Volume 6 under the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf, and in Volume 15 under the commentary of Surah Ash-Shu'ara.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّفْتَرًى وَمَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٦)
When Moses brought them Our manifest signs, they said, ‘This is nothing but concocted magic. We never heard of such a thing among our forefathers.’ (36)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنْ عِندِهِۦ وَمَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٣٧)
Moses said, ‘My Lord knows best who brings guidance from Him, and in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. The wrongdoers will not be felicitous.’ (37)

Commentary

Moses Before Pharaoh

Here, we encounter the eighth scene of this great saga.

Moses عليه السلام received the command of prophethood and mission from Allah on that dark night in that holy land. He came to Egypt, informed his brother Aaron (Harun), and conveyed the message of this great mission to him. Both went to Pharaoh, and after much effort, they managed to face him personally while his courtiers and elites surrounded him. Moses عليه السلام delivered the Divine invitation to them; now let us see what their reaction was to the message of Truth.

In the first verses under discussion, the Quran says: "But when Moses came to them with Our clear signs, they said, 'This is nothing but fabricated magic!'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ مُوسى‌ بِآياتِنا بَيِّناتٍ قالُوا ما هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُفْتَرىً‌).

"And we never heard of such a thing among our forefathers!" (وَ ما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي آبائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

In the face of the great miracles of Moses, they resorted to the same weapon that all tyrants and misguided people throughout history have used against the miracles of the Prophets: the weapon of "magic." This is because both are extraordinary; "but how different the two are!" Magicians are deviant and worldly people whose work is based on distorting realities, and they can be easily recognized by this trait, whereas the invitation of the Prophets and its content bear witness to the truthfulness of their miracles.

Furthermore, since magicians rely on human power, their work is always limited; however, the Prophets, who benefit from Divine power, possess miracles that are magnificent and limitless.

The expression "clear signs" (آيات بينات), which refers to the miracles of Moses, is used in the plural form because Moses may have presented other miracles to them in addition to these two, or each of these two miracles was itself a combination of multiple miracles: the staff turning into a great serpent is one miracle, and its return to its original state is another; similarly, the brilliance of Moses' hand in an instant is one miracle, and its return to its original state is another!

The term "fabricated" (مفترى), derived from the root faryah meaning slander and lie, is used because they wanted to say: Moses has falsely attributed this to Allah!

The statement "we never heard of such a thing among our forefathers"—despite the fact that the fame of the invitations of Noah, Abraham, and Joseph had spread in that land before Moses—is either due to the long passage of time and the distance of the era, or because they wanted to say that our ancestors never surrendered to such invitations either.

However, in response to them, Moses spoke with a threatening tone: "Moses said, 'My Lord knows best who has come with guidance from Him, and who will have the ultimate home [of success]'" (وَ قالَ مُوسى‌ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى‌ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ وَ مَنْ تَكُونُ لَهُ عاقِبَةُ الدَّارِ).

This is an indication that Allah is well aware of my state, even if you accuse me of lying. How could Allah place such an extraordinary power at the disposal of a liar, which would cause the misguidance of His servants? The fact that Allah knows my inner self and has granted me this ability is the best proof of the truthfulness of my invitation.

Moreover, a liar can only continue his work for a short time, and eventually, the veil will be lifted from his actions. You wait and see who will have the final outcome and victory, and who will face defeat.

Be certain that if I am a liar, I am an oppressor, "and the oppressors will never succeed" (إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

This expression is similar to another expression found in Surah Taha, verse 69: "And the magician will not succeed wherever he goes" (وَ لا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ‌ أَتى). This sentence may also implicitly refer to the condition of the stubborn and arrogant Pharaohs: that you have realized the truthfulness of my invitation through my miracles, yet you oppose me oppressively; but know that you will not be victorious, and the ultimate end belongs to me, not to you.

The term "the ultimate home" (عاقِبَةُ الدَّارِ) may refer to the end of the home of this world, or the home of the Hereafter, or both. Of course, the third meaning seems more comprehensive and appropriate.

With this logical and polite statement, Moses reminded them of their defeat and failure in this world and the next.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرِى فَأَوْقِدْ لِى يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلطِّينِ فَٱجْعَل لِّى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَطَّلِعُ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٣٨)
Pharaoh said, ‘O [members of the] elite! I do not know of any god that you may have besides me. Haman, light for me a fire over clay, and build me a tower so that I may take a look at Moses’ god, and indeed I consider him to be a liar!’ (38)
وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَجُنُودُهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٣٩)
He and his hosts unduly acted arrogantly in the land, and thought they would not be brought back to Us. (39)
فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٠)
So We seized him and his hosts, and threw them into the sea. So observe how was the fate of the wrongdoers! (40)
وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (٤١)
We made them leaders who invite to the Fire, and on the Day of Resurrection they will not receive any help. (41)
وَأَتْبَعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَقْبُوحِينَ (٤٢)
We made a curse pursue them in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the disfigured. (42)

Commentary

See What Was the End of the Wrongdoers!

Here, we encounter the ninth scene of this eventful and instructive history: Pharaoh’s staging of a spectacle by building his famous tower to drive Moses out of the field.

We know that one of the tactics of seasoned politicians is that whenever an important event occurs against their will, they immediately set about creating a new scene to "divert public opinion," attracting the masses' attention to it and turning them away from the undesirable event.

It appears that the story of building the massive tower took place after Moses's struggle with the sorcerers. This is inferred from Surah Al-Mu'min in the Holy Quran, which indicates that this act occurred when the Pharaohs were plotting to kill Moses, and the Believer from the Family of Pharaoh had risen to defend him. We know that before Moses's (عليه السلام) struggle with the sorcerers, there was no such talk; rather, the plan was to investigate Moses and crush him through the sorcerers. Since the Holy Quran has detailed the struggle between Moses and the sorcerers in Surahs Al-A'raf, Yunus, Taha, and Al-Shu'ara, it omits it here, focusing only on the construction of the tower, which is mentioned only in this Surah and Surah Al-Mu'min.

In any case, the news of Moses's (عليه السلام) victory over the sorcerers spread throughout Egypt. The sorcerers' conversion to Moses's faith added to the crisis, and the position of the Pharaonic government was severely endangered. The possibility of the enslaved masses awakening was very high. Public opinion had to be diverted from this issue at any cost, and a series of mental preoccupations—accompanied by the government's largesse—had to be provided to deceive and stupefy the people.

Pharaoh sat in consultation regarding this matter, and as a result, his mind arrived at what is mentioned in the first verse under discussion: "Pharaoh said: 'O chiefs! I know of no god for you other than myself!'" (وَ قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي‌).

The earthly god is certainly me! As for the God of the heavens, there is no evidence for His existence, but I will not abandon caution and will investigate! Then he turned to his minister, Haman, and said: "O Haman! Kindle for me a fire upon the clay" (and make strong bricks) (فَأَوْقِدْ لِي يا هامانُ عَلَى الطِّينِ‌).

"Then build for me a lofty palace and tower, so that I may climb it and seek news of the God of Moses! Though I do not believe he is truthful, and I think he is of the liars!" (فَاجْعَلْ لِي صَرْحاً لَعَلِّي أَطَّلِعُ إِلى‌ إِلهِ مُوسى‌ وَ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّهُ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌).

Why did Pharaoh not mention "bricks" by name and settled for the phrase "kindle a fire upon the clay"? Some say the reason is that until then, brick-making was not common, and this was an innovation of the Pharaohs. Others believe this manner of speaking was a type of arrogant expression consistent with the tradition of tyrants.

Some have also said that the word "brick" (آجر) is not an eloquent expression for the Quran to use, so it used this descriptive phrase instead.

Here, a group of commentators, such as Fakhr al-Razi and Alusi, have discussed whether Pharaoh actually carried out his command to build his skyscraper or not.

Apparently, what occupied the minds of these commentators is that this act was not rational by any standard. Had people not climbed mountains and seen the sky looking exactly as it does from the ground? Is a palace built by human hands higher than a mountain? What fool would believe that one could reach the heavens from the top of such a palace?! But those who think this way overlook the fact that, firstly, the land of Egypt was not mountainous, and furthermore, they forget the simplicity of the masses at that time and how they could be deceived and fooled by such matters. Even in our era, the so-called age of science and knowledge, we see issues that resemble these deceptions and tricks.

In any case, according to some histories, Haman ordered a vast area of land to be designated for this palace and high tower. He dispatched fifty thousand builders and architects for this task and commissioned thousands of workers to provide the materials. He opened the treasury doors, spent vast amounts of wealth, and employed so many workers that the news of this massive tower echoed everywhere.

The higher the building rose, the more people came to watch it, waiting to see what Pharaoh would do with it.

The building rose so high that it overlooked all the surroundings. Some have written that the architects built it such that a man on horseback could reach the top via its spiral stairs! When the construction was completed and they could raise it no further, Pharaoh arrived one day with great ceremony and personally climbed the massive tower. When he reached the top, he looked at the sky and saw it exactly as he saw it from the ordinary flat ground; there was not the slightest change!

It is famously said that he placed an arrow in a bow and shot it toward the sky. The arrow returned bloodied, either because it hit a bird or due to his own prior conspiracy. Pharaoh descended and told the people: "Go and be at ease, for I have killed the God of Moses!" [1] Surely, a group of simple-minded and blind followers of the government of the time believed this news and spread it everywhere, creating a new distraction to deceive the people of Egypt.

It is also narrated that this building did not last (and naturally, it should not have). Yes, this building collapsed and was destroyed, killing a group of people. Other stories have been narrated here, but since their authenticity was not clear, they were omitted.

It is noteworthy that Pharaoh, in his statement "I know of no god for you other than myself!", exercised the ultimate devilry. He took his own divinity as a given and framed the discussion only around whether there is another god besides him or not. Then, due to the lack of evidence, he denied that as well.

In the third stage, to provide evidence for the non-existence of another god, he brought up the story of the massive tower. All of this shows that he knew the facts well, but he played with words to stupefy the people of Egypt and preserve his position.

The Quran then addresses the arrogance of Pharaoh and his people and their refusal to submit to the "Origin" (God) and the "Return" (Hereafter), as the root of their crimes stemmed from the denial of these two principles. It says: "And he and his hosts were arrogant in the land without right (and denied God, the Great Creator of the heavens and the earth), and they thought that they would not be returned to Us" (وَ اسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَ جُنُودُهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنا لا يُرْجَعُونَ‌).

How can a weak human, who is sometimes unable to ward off a mosquito, and whose strongest individuals are sometimes sent under the earth by a microscopic organism called a microbe, present himself as great and claim divinity?! In a famous Hadith Qudsi, it is stated that Allah says:

"Grandeur is My cloak, and Greatness is My garment; whoever disputes with Me regarding either of them, I shall cast him into the Fire!" [1]

Clearly, Allah has no need for these descriptions. The point is that human rebellion and criminality begin when one loses oneself and the wind of pride and arrogance fills one's brain.

[1] Adapted from Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, under the verses discussed, Vol. 8, p. 462. [1] Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, Tafsir al-Mizan, and other commentaries under the verse.

But let us see where this pride and arrogance led. The Quran says: "So We seized him and his hosts and flung them into the sea!" (فَأَخَذْناهُ وَ جُنُودَهُ فَنَبَذْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِ‌).

Yes, We handed their death over to the very element that was the source of their life, and turned the Nile—which was the symbol of their greatness and power—into their graveyard! Interestingly, it uses the expression "فَنَبَذْناهُمْ" from the root "nabdh," which means to cast away worthless and insignificant things. Truly, what value can a selfish, arrogant, criminal tyrant have? Yes, We expelled these worthless beings from human society and cleansed the face of the earth from the filth of their existence.

At the end of the verse, addressing the Prophet of Islam, it says: "So see what was the end of the wrongdoers" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

This "seeing" is not with the physical eye but with the eye of the heart. And this expression is not exclusive to the wrongdoers of yesterday; the oppressors of today have no fate other than this!

Then it adds: "And We made them leaders (imams) who invite to the Fire, and on the Day of Resurrection they will not be helped!" (وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ لا يُنْصَرُونَ‌).

This expression has created a difficulty for some commentators: how is it possible for Allah to appoint people as leaders of falsehood? His work is to invite to good and to send Imams and leaders of truth, not falsehood.

But this is not a complex matter, because: Firstly, they are the heads of the people of Hell, and when groups of the damned move toward the Fire, they move ahead of them. Just as they were Imams of misguidance in this world, they are leaders of Hell there, for that world is a great manifestation of this world!

Secondly, being Imams of misguidance is, in reality, the result of their own actions. We know that the effect of every cause is by Allah's command. They took a path that led to the leadership of the misguided. This is their state on the Resurrection.

Again, for further emphasis, the Quran portrays their face in this world and the hereafter as follows: "And We followed them with a curse in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the loathed (those with ugly faces)" (وَ أَتْبَعْناهُمْ فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا لَعْنَةً وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ هُمْ مِنَ الْمَقْبُوحِينَ‌). [1]

The curse of Allah—which is expulsion from His mercy—and the curse of the angels and the believers—which is a prayer for their deprivation—is showered upon them every morning and evening, at all times. Sometimes they are included in the general curse upon wrongdoers and the arrogant, and sometimes they are specifically cursed and condemned, for whoever turns the pages of their history sends curses upon them! In any case, these people of ugly character in this world are the ones with ugly faces in that world, for that day is the "Day of Manifestation" and the day the veils are removed!

Key Point: Imams of "Light" and "Fire"

In the logic of the Quran, we have two types of "Imams": the Imam who is the leader of the pious on the path of guidance, as we read in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 73, regarding a group of prophets: وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَ إِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَ إِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَ كانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ‌: "And We made them leaders guiding by Our command. And We inspired to them the doing of good deeds, establishment of prayer, and giving of zakah; and they were worshippers of Us."

These were Imams with clear programs, for pure monotheism, the call to goodness, truth, and justice formed the core of their program. These are the Imams of Light whose line has continued through the chain of prophets and successors until the Final Prophet and his successors.

And the Imams who are leaders of misguidance and error, and in the words of the verses under discussion, are the Imams of Fire.

Among the characteristics of these two groups of leaders, as stated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), is that: "The first group prioritizes Allah's command over the command of the creation and their own will, and considers His judgment the highest of judgments. Whereas the second group prioritizes their own command over Allah's command and considers their own judgment before His judgment." [1]

With this criterion, identifying these two groups of Imams will be very clear! On the Day of Resurrection, when the ranks are distinguished, every group will follow their Imam. The people of Fire seek the people of Fire, and the people of Light seek the people of Light, as the Quran says: "The Day We shall call every people with their Imam" (يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ‌) (Al-Isra, 71).

We have said many times that the Resurrection is a massive manifestation of this small world, and those who have attached their hearts to an Imam here and walked in his line will be in his line there as well! Bishr ibn Ghalib narrates from Imam Abu Abdillah al-Husayn (عليه السلام) that he asked him about the interpretation of the verse "The Day We shall call every people with their Imam." The Imam (عليه السلام) said:

"An Imam who called to guidance and they responded to him, and an Imam who called to misguidance and they responded to him. These are in Paradise and those are in the Fire, and that is the meaning of His words: 'A party in Paradise and a party in the Blaze.'" [1]

Interestingly, Pharaoh, who moved ahead of his followers in this world and drowned them in the waves of the Nile, will also move ahead of them on the Day of Judgment and lead them into the sea of fire, as the Quran says: "He will precede his people on the Day of Resurrection and lead them into the Fire" (يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ) (Hud, 98).

We conclude this discussion with a saying from Ali (عليه السلام) where he says regarding a group of hypocrites:

"Then they remained after him (the Prophet), and they sought nearness to the Imams of misguidance and the callers to the Fire through falsehood and slander. So they (the Imams of misguidance) gave them positions of authority and made them rulers over the necks of the people!" [2]

[1] "Maqbuh" is from the root "qubh" meaning ugliness. Some commentators have interpreted "maqbuh" as rejected, disgraced, or object of wrath, which are all interpretations of the necessary consequence of the word; otherwise, the meaning of "maqbuh" is clear. [1] Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses discussed. [1] Amali al-Saduq, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 192. [2] Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 210.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ بَصَآئِرَ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٣)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, after We had destroyed the former generations, as [a set of] eye-openers, guidance and mercy for mankind, so that they may take admonition. (43)
وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلْغَرْبِىِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَمْرَ وَمَا كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٤٤)
You were not on the western side when We revealed the commandments to Moses, nor were you among the witnesses. (44)
