But We brought forth other generations and time took its toll on them. You did not dwell among the people of Midian reciting to them Our signs, but it is We who are the senders [of the apostles]. (45)
وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ إِذْ نَادَيْنَا وَلَـٰكِن رَّحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٦)
And you were not on the side of the Mount when We called out [to Moses], but [We have sent you as] a mercy from your Lord that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you, so that they may take admonition. (46)

Commentary

Only Allah Placed These Tidings of the Unseen at Your Disposal

In this section of the verses, we reach the "tenth scene," which is the final part of the verses related to the eventful story of Musa عليه السلام. It speaks of the revelation of the laws and the Torah—the time when the era of "negating the Taghut" (tyrant) ended and the era of construction and affirmation began.

First, He says: "And We gave Musa the Scripture after We had destroyed the earlier generations, as an enlightenment for mankind, and a guidance and a mercy, so that they might take heed" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ الْأُولى‌ بَصائِرَ لِلنَّاسِ وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

Regarding who is meant by the "earlier generations" (the peoples of previous ages who were destroyed):

Some commentators consider it an allusion to the disbelievers among the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, Thamud, and their like, because with the passage of time, the traces of previous prophets had been erased, and it was necessary for a new heavenly book to be placed at humanity's disposal.

Others consider it an allusion to the destruction of the people of Pharaoh, who were the remnants of previous generations, because Allah gave the Torah to Musa عليه السلام after their destruction.

However, there is no obstacle to the above phrase referring to all these peoples.

"Basa'ir" (بصائر) is the plural of "Basirah" (بصيرة), meaning insight or vision. Here, it refers to the verses and evidences that caused the illumination of the hearts of the believers. Guidance and mercy are also among the requirements of this insight, followed by the remembrance and awakening of prepared hearts. [1]

Then, He proceeds to state the reality that what We have described regarding Musa and Pharaoh, with all its precise details, is itself a proof of the truthfulness of your Quran; for you were never present at these scenes and did not witness these events with your own eyes. Rather, it was the grace of Allah that He revealed these verses to you for the guidance of the people.

He says: "And you were not on the western side when We decreed the commission to Musa, and you were not among the witnesses" (وَ ما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الْغَرْبِيِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسَى الْأَمْرَ وَ ما كُنْتَ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ‌).

It is necessary to note that Musa عليه السلام, on his path from Midian toward Egypt passing through the land of Sinai, was moving exactly from the "east" toward the "west." Conversely, when the Children of Israel came from Egypt toward the Levant and passed through Sinai, they were coming from the west toward the east (hence, some commentators have considered the phrase "فَأَتْبَعُوهُمْ مُشْرِقِينَ‌" in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 60, which speaks of the Pharaoh's pursuit of the Children of Israel, to be an allusion to this same meaning).

Then He adds: "But We brought forth generations in various ages, and long ages passed over them" (and the traces of the prophets and their guidance were erased from their hearts and minds; therefore, We brought you and your Quran and recounted the stories of the predecessors so that it may be an enlightener for mankind) (وَ لكِنَّا أَنْشَأْنا قُرُوناً فَتَطاوَلَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ).

"And you were not a dweller among the people of Midian (to obtain the verses and news of their lives) reciting Our verses to them (the people of Makkah)" (وَ ما كُنْتَ ثاوِياً فِي أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ تَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِنا). [1] It was We who sent you (and placed these precise reports related to thousands of years ago [2] at your disposal so that you may become a guide for this creation) (وَ لكِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ‌).

Again, to emphasize this same meaning, He adds: "And you were not at the side of the Mount (Tur) when We called out (and issued the command of prophethood in the name of Musa)" (وَ ما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الطُّورِ إِذْ نادَيْنا). [3]

[1] "Basa'ir" is the plural of Basirah, and Absar is the plural of Basar.

[2] The interval between the appearance of Musa عليه السلام and the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was approximately two thousand years.

[3] Some commentators have suggested the possibility that the "call" here refers to the call given by Allah on another occasion when Musa عليه السلام came to Mount Tur with seventy men from the Children of Israel. However, this possibility is very remote, because these verses refer to matters mentioned in the preceding verses which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله reported while not being a witness or observer. We know that the preceding verses speak of Musa's journey from Midian toward Egypt and hearing the word of Allah for the first time in the valley of Tur (reflect on this).

"But [We have revealed this] as a mercy from your Lord, so that you may warn a people to whom no warner had come before you, that they may take heed" (وَ لكِنْ رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

In short: We have recounted for you the awakening and warning events that occurred among distant peoples—events where you were not present or observing—so that you may recite them to this misguided people, that perhaps it may be a cause for their awakening.

Here, the question arises: How can the Quran say that no warner had come to this people (the Arabs contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) before you, while we know that the earth is never empty of a Divine Proof (Hujjah), and the successors of the prophets were also among this people?!

In response, we say: The meaning is the sending of a Prophet who possesses a Scripture and is an overt warner, for centuries passed between the era of Prophet Isa عليه السلام and the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and no Arch-Prophet (Ulu al-Azm) came. This very issue gave an excuse to the atheists and the corrupt.

Ali عليه السلام says in one of his speeches:

ان اللَّه بعث محمدا (ص) و ليس احد من العرب يقرأ كتابا و لا يدعى نبوة فساق الناس حتى بوأهم محلتهم و بلغهم منجاتهم:

"Allah sent Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله when none of the Arabs read a [heavenly] book or claimed prophethood. He led the people until He settled them in their proper place and brought them to their place of salvation" (Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 33).

[1] "Thawi" (ثاوى) is from the root "Thawa" (ثوى), meaning to reside with stability; for this reason, a dwelling place or station is called "Mathwa" (مثوى).

Ayahs

وَلَوْلَآ أَن تُصِيبَهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
And lest—if an affliction were to befall them because of what their hands have sent ahead —they should say, ‘Our Lord! Why did You not send us an apostle so that we might have followed Your signs and been among the faithful?’ (47)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُوتِىَ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سِحْرَانِ تَظَـٰهَرَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٨)
But when there came to them the truth from Us, they said, ‘Why has he not been given the like of what Moses was given?’ Did they not disbelieve what Moses was given before, and said, ‘Two magicians abetting each other,’ and said, ‘Indeed we disbelieve both of them’? (48)
قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمَآ أَتَّبِعْهُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘If you are truthful, bring some Book from Allah better in guidance than the two on them so that I may follow it.’ (49)
فَإِن لَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكَ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدًى مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٠)
Then if they do not respond to you[r] [summons], know that they only follow their desires, and who is more astray than him who follows his desires without any guidance from Allah? Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (50)

Commentary

Every Day They Flee from the Truth with a New Excuse

Since the previous verses spoke of the sending of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as a warner and cautioner, the first verse under discussion points to the grace resulting from the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) existence, stating: "And if we had punished them for their deeds before sending a prophet, they would have said: Our Lord! Why did You not send us a messenger so that we might follow Your signs and be among the believers?" If it were not for this, their punishment for their actions and disbelief would not even have required the sending of a prophet (وَلَوْلَا أَنْ تُصِيبَهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُوا رَبَّنَا لَوْلَا أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ آيَاتِكَ وَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) [1]. In reality, the verse points to the fact that the path of truth is clear, and every intellect judges the falsehood of polytheism and idol-worship; the ugliness of many of their actions, such as injustices and oppressions, are among the "independent judgments of reason" (mustaqillat al-aql), and they could be punished for these even without the sending of prophets. However, even in these matters where the judgment of reason is clear and manifest, Allah sends prophets with heavenly books and miracles to complete the proof (itmam al-hujjah) and negate any excuse, so that no one may say, "Our misery was due to the absence of a guide; had we possessed a divine leader, we would have been among the guided and the saved."

In any case, this verse is among those that indicate the necessity of Grace (Lutf) through the sending of prophets, and it shows that Allah's Sunnah (tradition) is not to punish any nation for their sins before sending a prophet, just as we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 165: رُسُلًا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللَّهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا ("Messengers as bearers of good news and warners, so that mankind might have no argument against Allah after the messengers. And Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise").

Then it points to their excuse-making, noting that even after the sending of the messengers, they did not cease their quibbling and continued their deviant ways, saying:

"When the truth came to them from Us, they said: 'Why has he not been given the like of what was given to Moses?'" (فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ عِنْدِنَا قَالُوا لَوْلَا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ مَا أُوتِيَ مُوسَى).

Why is the staff of Moses not in his hand? Why does he not have the "White Hand"? Why is the sea not split for him? Why are his enemies not drowned? Why and why?!...

The Quran proceeds to answer this excuse-making, saying: "Did they not disbelieve in what was given to Moses before?" (أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا بِمَا أُوتِيَ مُوسَى مِنْ قَبْلُ).

"Did they not say: 'Two works of magic supporting each other (to mislead us),' and they said: 'Indeed, we are disbelievers in each of them'!" (قَالُوا سِحْرَانِ تَظَاهَرَا وَقَالُوا إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كَافِرُونَ).

The use of the term "two magics" (sihran), whereas normally "two magicians" (sahiran) should be said, is for extreme emphasis; for when an Arab speaks emphatically about someone, he describes them as the very essence of "justice," "injustice," or "magic."

There is also the possibility that "two magics" refers to the two great miracles of Moses: the "Staff" and the "White Hand."

And if it is asked: what do these denials have to do with the polytheists of Mecca? This pertains to the disbelieving people of Pharaoh. The answer is clear: the point is that the issue of excuse-making is nothing new; they are all of the same cloth, their words bear a great resemblance to one another, and their path, method, and program are one.

The clear commentary of the above verse was what we mentioned, but a group of commentators have interpreted the verse differently, saying: the meaning of "two magics supporting each other" refers to Prophet Moses and the Great Prophet of Islam, because the Arab polytheists said both were magicians and we disbelieve in both. Here, they also cite a historical event: that the people of Mecca sent a group to the leaders of the Jews during one of their festivals and asked them about the Prophet of Islam, whether Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) was truly a prophet of God. They replied: "We have found him with his descriptions in the Torah."

The representatives returned and told the story to the polytheists of Mecca; it was then that they said the sentence: "Two magics supporting each other" and "Indeed, we are disbelievers in each" [1]. However, considering two points, this interpretation seems unlikely:

First, it is rarely seen in history or narrations that the Arab polytheists accused Moses (عليه السلام) of being a magician; perhaps such a possibility is only suggested here.

Second, how can someone claim that Moses and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), with a gap of nearly two thousand years between them, were magicians who rose to support one another? How could a magician from thousands of years ago know who would appear in the future and what claim he would make?!

In any case, the stubborn polytheists insisted: why did the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) not have miracles like those of Moses? On the other hand, they paid no heed to the words and testimony of the Torah regarding the signs of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), nor to the Holy Quran and its magnificent verses. Therefore, the Quran addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), saying: "Say: If you are truthful that these two books are not from God, then bring a book from God that is more guided than these two, so that I may follow it" (قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِكِتَابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ هُوَ أَهْدَى مِنْهُمَا أَتَّبِعْهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

In other words, they are looking for a book of guidance and for miracles; what miracle is greater than the Quran and what book of guidance is better than it? If the Prophet of Islam had nothing in his hand but this Quran, it would have sufficed to prove the truthfulness of his call, but they are not seekers of truth; rather, they are a bunch of excuse-makers.

Then it adds: "If they do not respond to you, then know that they only follow their own desires" (فَإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكَ فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ). For a person who is not a slave to his desires would submit to such a logical proposal, but they are not upright on any path and reject every proposal with an excuse.

But "who is more astray than one who follows his own desire without any guidance from Allah?" (وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ اتَّبَعَ هَوَاهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدًى مِنَ اللَّهِ).

"Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

If they were seekers of truth and had merely lost the way, Divine Grace—according to the principle of "And those who strive for Us, We will surely guide them to Our ways"—would have included them. But they are wrongdoers; they wrong both themselves and the society in which they live. They have no goal but stubbornness and enmity; how then could Allah assist in their guidance?

Key Point: Self-Desire as a Factor of Misguidance

In the above verses, the relationship between these two is explicitly stated, and even the most astray of people are identified as those who have made their own desires their leader and have never accepted Divine guidance.

Self-desire (hawa al-nafs) is a thick veil before the eyes of human reason.

Self-desire creates such an attachment in a person toward a subject that he loses the power to perceive realities; for to perceive the truth, absolute submission to facts and the abandonment of any prejudice or attachment is a prerequisite. Unconditional submission to whatever has external reality, whether it be sweet or bitter, whether it agrees with our inner inclinations or opposes them, whether it aligns with personal interests or not. But self-desire is incompatible with these principles.

A detailed discussion on this matter was also provided under verse 43 of Surah Al-Furqan (Volume 15).

Interestingly, in numerous narrations, the above verse has been interpreted as referring to those who have not accepted the Imam and Divine leader and rely only on their own opinions [1]. These narrations, which are quoted from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and some other Imams of Guidance (عليه السلام), are in fact of the nature of "clear applications" (misdaq). In other words, man is in need of Divine guidance; this guidance is sometimes reflected in a Heavenly Book, sometimes in the existence of the Prophet and his Sunnah, sometimes in his infallible successors, and sometimes in the logic of reason and intellect.

The important thing is that man should be on the path of Divine guidance and not self-desire, so that he may benefit from these lights of guidance.

[1]: Many commentators have stated that the response to the first lawla is omitted, and its penalty was "We would not have sent a messenger" or "the sending of messengers would not have been necessary." Of course, the second expression is more correct and realistic. In any case, this statement pertains to rulings that reason perceives independently; otherwise, the sending of prophets is necessary for other reasons as well, although one of the benefits of the prophets' coming is the emphasis on "rational rulings" such as the falsehood of polytheism and the ugliness of oppression and corruption (reflect on this).

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 20, p. 81.

[1]: These narrations are found in Usul al-Kafi and Basa'ir al-Darajat—as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 132.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ وَصَّلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥١)
Certainly We have carried on this discourse for them so that they may take admonition. (51)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ هُم بِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Those to whom We gave the Book before it are the ones who believe in it, (52)
وَإِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦٓ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مُسْلِمِينَ (٥٣)
and when it is recited to them, they say, ‘We believe in it. It is indeed the truth from our Lord. Indeed we were Muslims [even] before it [came].’ (53)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٥٤)
Those will be given their reward two times for their patience. They repel evil [conduct] with good, and spend out of what We have provided them, (54)
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱللَّغْوَ أَعْرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا نَبْتَغِى ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٥٥)
and when they hear vain talk, they avoid it and say, ‘Our deeds belong to us, and your deeds belong to you. Peace be to you. We do not court the ignorant.’ (55)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the aforementioned verses, commentators and narrators of reports have related various narrations, the common denominator of all being the conversion to Islam of a group of scholars from the Jews and Christians, as well as pure-hearted individuals, upon encountering the verses of the Quran and the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

It is narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr that these verses were revealed concerning seventy Christian priests whom the Negus (Najashi) sent from Abyssinia to Mecca to investigate. When the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited Surah Ya-Sin to them, they shed tears of joy and embraced Islam [^1]. Others have said these verses were revealed regarding a group of Christians from Najran (a city in northern Yemen) who came to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), heard the verses of the Quran, and believed [^2]. Some consider them to be about the Negus and his companions [^3], while others believe they were revealed concerning Salman al-Farsi and a group of Jewish scholars such as Abdullah ibn Salam, Tamim al-Dari, and Jarud al-Abdi [^4]. Finally, some suggest they refer to forty Christian scholars and enlightened souls, thirty-two of whom came from Abyssinia to Medina with Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and eight from Syria, among whom was Bahira, the famous Syrian monk [^5]. Of course, the first three narrations are consistent with these verses being revealed in Mecca and support the view of those who believe this entire Surah is Meccan. However, the fourth and fifth narrations indicate that these few verses were revealed as an exception in Medina and serve as evidence for those who consider them "Medinan."

In any case, these verses are a clear testament to the fact that a group of scholars from the People of the Book accepted Islam upon hearing the verses of the Quran. It would have been impossible for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to make such a claim if no one from the People of the Book had believed in Him, as the polytheists would have immediately risen to deny and negate it, creating a great uproar.

Commentary

The Truth-Seekers of the People of the Book Believe in Your Quran

Since the previous verses discussed the excuses raised by the polytheists for not submitting to the truths of the Quran, the verses under discussion speak of receptive hearts that, upon hearing these verses, found the truth, remained deeply loyal to it, and submitted to it with soul and heart—whereas the dark hearts of the prejudiced ignorant ones showed not the slightest effect! It says: "And indeed We have conveyed the Word to them one after another, so that they may take heed" (وَ لَقَدْ وَصَّلْنا لَهُمُ الْقَوْلَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ) [^1]. These verses descended upon them like continuous drops of rain, in diverse forms and different qualities: sometimes a promise of reward, sometimes a threat of Hell, sometimes advice and counsel, sometimes warning and admonition, sometimes rational arguments, and sometimes the instructive and rich history of the predecessors. In short, a complete and highly harmonious collection that attracts any heart with even a slight readiness; yet the blind-hearted did not accept.

However, "Those to whom We gave the Scripture before it (from the Jews and Christians) believe in it" (الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ). This is because they find it consistent with the signs they found in their own Divine Books.

Interestingly, these were only a group from the People of the Book, yet the verse refers to them as "The People of the Book" without any qualification, perhaps implying that these were the true People of the Book, and the others were nothing!

[^1]: "Wassalna" is from the root "wasl," meaning to connect and join. Since it is in the "Taf'il" form, it also implies frequency and emphasis.

Then it adds: "And when it is recited to them, they say: 'We believe in it; surely it is the truth from our Lord'" (وَ إِذا يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِهِ إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّنا). Indeed, the recitation of these verses was enough for them to say "We believe" and to testify to its truth.

Then it adds: Not only are we submissive to the words of the Lord today, but "indeed we were Muslims even before this" (إِنَّا كُنَّا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مُسْلِمِينَ). We had found the signs of this Prophet in our Divine Books, had set our hearts upon Him, and were waiting for Him with impatience; and at the first opportunity when we found our lost treasure, we took it and accepted it with all our soul and heart.

Then the Quran addresses the great reward of this group that broke the chains of imitation and sought the truth, saying: "These are the ones who will be given their reward twice because of their patience and steadfastness!" (أُولئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُمْ مَرَّتَيْنِ بِما صَبَرُوا). Once for their faith in their own Divine Book, to which they were truly loyal and committed, and once for believing in the Prophet of Islam, the promised Prophet whom the previous Books had foretold.

It is also possible that their receiving a double reward is because they believed in the Prophet of Islam both before His appearance and after His appearance, as can be inferred from the preceding verses.

They exhibited great patience and steadfastness in fulfilling their duty in both stages. Neither the deviants among the Jews and Christians approved of their actions, nor did the imitation of ancestors and the social environment allow them to abandon their former religion. Yet they stood firm, trampled over their own interests and whims, and earned the great Divine reward twofold.

Then it mentions a series of their righteous deeds, each more valuable than the last. These deeds consist of "repelling evil with good," "spending from Divine blessings," and "gracious conduct toward the ignorant," which, added to the "patience and steadfastness" mentioned in the previous sentence, make four distinguished attributes.

First, it says: "And they repel evil with good" (وَ يَدْرَؤُنَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ السَّيِّئَةَ). With good speech, they repel foul words; with the recognized good (Ma'ruf), they repel the forbidden (Munkar); with forbearance, the ignorance of the ignorant; with love, enmity and malice; and with the bonds of friendship and kinship, the severance of ties. In short, they try to repel evil with good instead of responding to evil with evil! This is a very effective method in combating corruption, especially against a group of stubborn people, and the Quran has repeatedly emphasized it.

Secondly, "And they spend out of what We have provided them" (وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ). Not only from their wealth and property, but also from their knowledge, intellectual and physical strength, and social status—all of which are Divine gifts and provisions—they give in the way of the needy.

Finally, their last practical distinction is that "whenever they hear vain and idle talk, they turn away from it" (وَ إِذا سَمِعُوا اللَّغْوَ أَعْرَضُوا عَنْهُ). They never respond to "vanity" with "vanity," nor do they answer ignorance with ignorance. Rather, they say to the idle talkers: "Our deeds are for us, and your deeds are for you!" (وَ قالُوا لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَ لَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ). Neither will you be held accountable for our deeds, nor will we be held for yours; but soon you will know what fruit the deeds of each of us have produced.

Then it adds that they bid farewell to the ignorant idle talkers and those who try to disturb the nerves of the believers and the righteous with hurtful words, and their statement is: "Peace be upon you; we do not seek the ignorant!" (سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لا نَبْتَغِي الْجاهِلِينَ). We are neither people of foul language, ignorance, and corruption, nor do we desire it. We seek enlightened scholars, practicing sages, and true believers.

In this way, instead of wasting their energy in confronting the blind-hearted ignorant and the uninformed foul-mouthed, they pass by them with nobility and attend to their fundamental goals and programs. It is noteworthy that when they encounter such individuals, they do not say "Peace" as a greeting, but rather their "Peace" is a peace of farewell!

Key Point: Hearts Ready for Faith

In the above verses, a very expressive and beautiful portrayal is given of the hearts that receive and nurture the seed of faith. They are not of the sort of characterless individuals who are reservoirs of ignorance, prejudice, foul language, idle talk, stinginess, and malice.

They are great men and pure women who have, above all, broken the chains of the slavery of blind imitation. Then, they carefully listened to the call of the herald of Monotheism, and as soon as they found sufficient evidence of the truth, they became devoted to it.

Undoubtedly, they must pay a heavy price for breaking this imitation and separating from the deviations of their environment, and they must endure many deprivations and hardships. But they possess such patience and steadfastness that they overcome these difficulties for the sake of their great goal.

They are neither malicious, to respond to every evil with worse, nor are they stingy and miserly, to reserve Divine blessings only for themselves. They are noble souls who, in addition to all this, are free from lies, unhealthy entertainment, vain arguments, meaningless words, vulgar jokes, and the like. They have a pure tongue and an even purer heart, and they never waste their active and constructive energies in conflict with the ignorant. In many cases, they even prefer silence—which is the best response to these fools—over speaking.

They are concerned with their own deeds and responsibilities. Like thirsty people who seek a spring of water, they are thirsty for knowledge and desire to attend the gatherings of scholars and sages. Indeed, it is these noble ones who can accept the mission of faith within themselves and receive their reward from the presence of Allah not once, but twice. These are the Salmans, the Bahiras, the Neguses, and the truth-seekers of their rank and path who show resistance against all kinds of unpleasantness to reach the destination of faith.

Interestingly, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "We are the patient ones, and our followers (Shi'a) are more patient than us! That is because we are patient regarding what we know, while they are patient regarding what they do not know."

Consider two self-sacrificing soldiers of Allah who step into the field of Jihad. One knows the end of the matter and knows that the result of this Jihad is victory. But the second one does not know. Is not the patience of the second one, from one perspective, greater than the patience of the first? Or, for example, the signs indicate that both will drink the nectar of martyrdom, but one knows what secrets are hidden in his martyrdom, what wave it will create in the future across centuries, and what a model it will become for the free-spirited, while the other is unaware of the secrets of the future. Undoubtedly, the second one exhibits more patience in this regard.

In another Hadith in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is stated that "Laghw" (vain talk) in the above verse refers to "lies," "idle play," and "sinful music," and those who avoid it are the Imams. It is clear that both parts of the Hadith are by way of explaining the most prominent examples; otherwise, "Laghw" has a broad meaning that includes other things as well, and those who turn away from it are all true believers, though the Imams stand in the foremost rank.

Ayahs

إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٥٦)
You cannot guide whomever you wish, but [it is] Allah [who] guides whomever He wishes, and He knows best those who are guided. (56)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِن نَّتَّبِعِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنَآ ۚ أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا يُجْبَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَرَٰتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رِّزْقًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٧)
They say, ‘If we follow the guidance with you, we will be forced out of our territory.’ Did We not establish a secure sanctuary for them where fruits of all kinds are brought as a provision from Us? But most of them do not know. (57)

Commentary

Guidance is Solely in the Hands of Allah

Although many discussions have been raised regarding the circumstances of revelation (Sha’n al-Nuzul) for the first of these verses, as we shall see, these are unreliable and worthless narrations that seem to have been fabricated for specific agendas. Therefore, we deemed it better to seek the interpretation of the verse from the Holy Quran itself and then proceed to critique and analyze those suspicious or fabricated narrations.

Considering that the preceding verses spoke of two groups: one group being the stubborn polytheists of Makkah—who, despite the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) insistence on their guidance, did not allow the light of faith to penetrate their hearts—and conversely, a group from the People of the Book and distant individuals who accepted divine guidance and passionately persevered and sacrificed in the path of Islam, showing no fear even of the opposition of their close relatives and selfish ignorant kin.

In light of these matters, the first verse under discussion unveils this reality: "Indeed, [O Muhammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills. And He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided." (إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ).

He knows which individuals are worthy of accepting faith; He knows which hearts beat for the Truth; He knows in which heads the passion for Allah's love resides. Yes, He knows these worthy individuals well, grants them success (tawfiq), and makes His grace their companion on the path so they may be led toward faith.

However, as for the dark-hearted ones of evil nature who are enemies of the Truth in their hearts and have risen with all their might to combat the messengers of Allah, and who are so polluted and disgraceful in their lives that they are unworthy of the light of faith—Allah never places the lamp of success in their path.

Therefore, the meaning of "guidance" here is not "showing the way" (ira’at al-tariq), because showing the way is the primary task of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and he shows the path to everyone without exception. Rather, guidance here means "conveying to the objective" (isal ila al-matlub) and bringing one to the final destination. This is solely the work of Allah—to sow the seeds of faith in hearts—and His work is not arbitrary; He looks upon prepared hearts and sprinkles this heavenly light upon them.

In any case, this verse serves as a form of consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to realize that neither the insistence on polytheism by a group of idolaters in Makkah is without reason, nor is the sincere faith of the people of Abyssinia or Najran, and individuals like Salman and Bahira.

Never allow yourself to be distressed by the lack of faith of the first group, for this divine light seeks out prepared hearts, enters them, and pitches its tent there.

Similar themes are abundant in the verses of the Quran:

In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 272, we read: "Not upon you [O Muhammad] is their guidance, but Allah guides whom He wills." (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُداهُمْ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

And in Surah An-Nahl, verse 37, we read: "If you should desire for their guidance, indeed, Allah does not guide those He sends astray." (إِنْ تَحْرِصْ عَلى‌ هُداهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُ‌).

And in Surah Yunus, verse 43, it says: "Then will you guide the blind even though they do not see [and perceive the truth]?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يُبْصِرُونَ‌).

Finally, in Surah Ibrahim, verse 4, as a general law, He says: "So Allah sends astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (فَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

The final phrase of this last verse clearly shows that the Divine Will regarding these two groups is not without measure; rather, it is according to Wisdom and the program of merits, efforts, and endeavors of individuals. It is only on this basis that Allah grants the success of guidance to one group or withholds it from another.

In the second verse under discussion, it speaks of those who acknowledged the truth of Islam in their hearts but were unwilling to accept faith due to considerations of personal interest. It says: "And they say, 'If we were to follow the guidance with you, we would be swept from our land.'" (وَ قالُوا إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى‌ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا) [1]. It is mentioned in commentaries that this statement was made by "Harith ibn Nawfal." He came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: "We know that your word is the truth, but what prevents us from following your program and believing is the fear of an Arab assault upon us, that they would snatch us from our land, and we do not have the power to confront them!" [2]

This is said by someone who considers the power of the Lord insignificant and the power of a handful of ignorant Arabs as great. This is said by someone who is not yet acquainted with the depth of Divine care and support, and does not know how He aids His allies and crushes His enemies. Therefore, the Quran responds to them: "Have We not established for them a safe sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things?" (أَ وَ لَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَهُمْ حَرَماً آمِناً يُجْبى‌ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَراتُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ) [3] "But most of them do not know." (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The God who made a salty, rocky land without water or trees a safe sanctuary, and turned hearts toward it such that the best products from different parts of the world are brought to it, has well demonstrated His power. One who has displayed such power and placed all this "security" and "blessing" in such a land—the effects of which you see with your own eyes and have benefited from for years—how is He not able to protect you against the assault of a handful of idol-worshipping Arabs?! While in a state of disbelief, you were covered by these two great Divine blessings, "security" and "provisions of life"; how is it possible that Allah would deprive you of them after your Islam? Take heart, believe, and stand firm, for the God of the Kaaba and Makkah is with you.

Here, a question arises: history shows that the sanctuary of Makkah was not all that safe and peaceful for the Muslims. Were not a group of Muslims harassed and tortured there? Did they not throw all those stones at the body of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)? Were not some Muslims killed in Makkah? Did not a group with Ja'far, and later others with the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), eventually migrate from there due to insecurity?!

In response, we say: Firstly, despite all these things, Makkah still had more security than other places, and the Arabs held a certain respect and sanctity for it. They could not commit the same crimes there that they committed elsewhere. In short, even amidst insecurity, the sanctuary of Makkah enjoyed significant relative security, especially since this security was mostly respected by the Arabs outside of Makkah.

Secondly, it is true that at the beginning of Islam, for a short period, this safe Divine land was subject to some insecurity, but it did not take long before it became a great center of lasting security and a massive hub for all kinds of blessings. Therefore, enduring these fleeting difficulties to reach great blessings was not a hard or complicated task.

In any case, there are many who, out of fear of their personal interests being shaken—like Harith ibn Nawfal—abandon guidance and faith, while faith in Allah and submission to His command not only secures their "spiritual interests" but is also extraordinarily effective in securing legitimate material interests and a safe, healthy environment. The insecurities, killings, and plundering that we see in today's so-called civilized world—a world that has fallen far from faith and guidance—are living witnesses to this claim.

It is also necessary to note that Allah first mentions the blessing of security and then the attraction of provisions from everywhere toward Makkah. This expression may reflect the reality that until security prevails in a city or country, its economic situation will not be organized. We have provided an explanation of this under verse 35 of Surah Ibrahim (Volume 10, page 366).

It is also interesting that yujba (are brought) appears in the present/future tense (mudari'), which indicates continuity in the present and future. Today, after fourteen centuries, we witness with our own eyes the meaning of this statement and the continuous attraction of all kinds of bounties toward this land. Those who visit the House of Allah see with their own eyes that this dry, scorching land, devoid of water and vegetation, is drowned in the best of all kinds of blessings, and perhaps in no other part of the world is there such an abundance of blessings.

Key Point: The Faith of Abu Talib and the Controversy Stirred in This Regard!

Initially, this subject appears strange to those who are well-read: why did a group of narrators insist on portraying Abu Talib, the Prophet’s uncle—who, by the consensus of all Muslims in the world, was among those who performed the maximum sacrifice, altruism, and support for the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله)—as faithless and a polytheist, and claim he died a disbeliever?! Why is there not such insistence regarding others who played no role in Islamic history?

It is here that we realize the issue is not an ordinary one. Then, with a little scrutiny, we find that behind these historical and narrative discussions, a dangerous political game was being played by the rivals and enemies of Ali (عليه السلام). They insisted on stripping him of every virtue and even portraying his self-sacrificing father as a polytheist who left the world without faith! Certainly, the Umayyads and their supporters in their era, and even before they came to power, fanned this idea wherever they could and tried to piece together evidence—however weak and baseless—to prove this claim.

Regardless of this foul and polluted political wave of deviation, which itself is worthy of careful study from several aspects, we examine the issue as a purely historical and exegetical matter in a very condensed manner (as the nature of the book requires), so it becomes clear that there is no valid evidence behind these controversies; rather, we have living evidence to the contrary:

  1. The verse under discussion ("Indeed, you do not guide whom you like...") as we have seen, has no connection to Abu Talib, and the verses preceding it clearly indicate that it concerns a group of believers from the People of the Book in contrast to the polytheists of Makkah.

Interestingly, Fakhr al-Razi, who himself cites the so-called consensus of Muslims that the verse was revealed about Abu Talib, explicitly states that in the apparent wording of the verse, there is not the slightest indication of Abu Talib's disbelief [1]. Yet, why they insist on linking it to Abu Talib's polytheism is truly frightening.

  1. The most important reason they have provided in this regard is the claim of a consensus among Muslims that Abu Talib died a polytheist! However, such a consensus is a pure lie, as the famous Sunni commentator Al-Alusi has stated in Ruh al-Ma'ani that this issue is not a matter of consensus, and the report of a consensus of Muslims or commentators that the above verse was revealed about Abu Talib does not seem correct. This is because the Shia scholars and many of their commentators believe in the Islam of Abu Talib and have claimed a consensus of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام) on this meaning. Furthermore, most of Abu Talib's poems bear witness to his faith [2].

  2. Careful examination shows that this claim of consensus originates from isolated reports (akhbar al-ahad) that have no credibility, and in the chains of these narrations are suspicious individuals or liars.

Among them is a narration that Ibn Mardawayh narrated with his chain from Ibn Abbas that the verse "Indeed, you do not guide whom you like" was revealed about Abu Talib; the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) urged him to accept Islam but he refused! [3] However, in the chain of this narration is "Abu Sahl al-Sari," who, according to the explicit statements of some masters of the science of men (ilm al-rijal), was one of the fabricators, forgers, and thieves of hadith. Also, "Abd al-Quddus Abi Sa'id al-Dimashqi," who is also in the chain of this hadith, is among the "liars"! [1]

The apparent phrasing of the hadith suggests that Ibn Abbas narrated this hadith without an intermediary and was himself a witness and observer, whereas we know that Ibn Abbas was born three years before the Hijrah. Therefore, at the time of Abu Talib's death, he was still a nursing infant! This shows that the fabricators of the hadith were even amateurish in their work.

Another hadith in this regard is narrated from "Abu Hurayrah," who says: When Abu Talib's death approached, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to him: "O uncle, say 'There is no god but Allah' so that I may testify to monotheism for you before Allah on the Day of Resurrection." Abu Talib said: "If it were not for the fear that the Quraysh would reproach me, saying he expressed faith at the time of death out of fear, I would testify to monotheism and brighten your eyes! At this moment, the verse 'Indeed, you do not guide whom you like' was revealed!" [2]

Again, the apparent hadith is that Abu Hurayrah was personally a witness to such an event, while we know that Abu Hurayrah expressed Islam in the year of the conquest of Khaybar, i.e., seven years after the Hijrah. Where was he and where was the death of Abu Talib, which occurred before the Hijrah?! Therefore, the signs of amateurish fabrication are also evident in this hadith.

And if it is said that Ibn Abbas and Abu Hurayrah were not themselves witnesses to this event and heard this story from someone else, we ask: from whom? The person who related this narration to these two is unknown and anonymous, and such a hadith is called mursal (disconnected), and everyone knows that mursal hadiths have no credibility.

Unfortunately, a group of commentators and narrators have taken and quoted such hadiths from one another in their books without scrutiny or study, and gradually created a "consensus" for themselves. But what consensus? And what reliable hadith?

  1. Beyond all this, the text of these very fabricated hadiths shows that Abu Talib had faith in the truth of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), even if he did not utter it due to certain considerations. We know that faith is in the heart, and the tongue serves as a medium. In some Islamic hadiths, Abu Talib's situation is likened to the People of the Cave, who had faith in their hearts even though for certain reasons they were unable to manifest it [1].

  2. Can one discuss such an important issue one-sidedly and be content only with mursal narrations from Abu Hurayrah, Ibn Abbas, and the like? Why is the consensus of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) and the consensus of the Shia scholars not taken into account here?! Especially since they are more familiar with the situation of the Prophet's family.

Today, we have many poems from Abu Talib in our hands that express his faith in Islam and the mission of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) as the Prophet of Islam with complete clarity. These poems have been cited by many great figures and scholars in their books, and we quoted eloquent examples of them in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 194 onwards (under verse 26 of Surah Al-An'am) from well-known Sunni sources.

  1. Beyond all this, the history of Abu Talib's life, his immense sacrifice for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the intense affection of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Muslims toward him—to the extent that the year of his death was named "The Year of Sorrow" (Am al-Huzn)—all show that he loved Islam. His defense of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was not merely the defense of a relative, but rather the defense of a sincere believer, a devoted lover, and a self-sacrificing soldier for his leader and guide. Despite this, how much negligence, ignorance, ingratitude, and injustice it is that a group insists on portraying this sincere monotheistic believer as having left the world without faith! [1]

[1] "Ma'aka" (with you) in the above verse is related to "nattabi'" (we follow); there is also a possibility that it relates to "al-huda" (the guidance), which would result in a slight difference in meaning. [2] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question. [3] "Yujba" is from the root "jabayah" meaning to collect, and thus a pool that collects water is called "jabiyah." Additionally, "numakkin" in the above verse means "naj'al" (We make/place) and "haraman" is its object; otherwise, "numakkin" in its original meaning of "giving power/establishment" becomes transitive with the preposition "fi."
