Ayahs

وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍۭ بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَهَا ۖ فَتِلْكَ مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ لَمْ تُسْكَن مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ وَكُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٥٨)
How many a town We have destroyed that exulted in its lifestyle! There lie their dwellings, uninhabited after them except by a few, and We were the [sole] inheritors. (58)
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يَبْعَثَ فِىٓ أُمِّهَا رَسُولًا يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۚ وَمَا كُنَّا مُهْلِكِى ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا وَأَهْلُهَا ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٥٩)
Your Lord would not destroy the towns until He had raised an apostle in their mother city to recite to them Our signs. We would never destroy the towns except when their people were wrongdoers. (59)
وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتُهَا ۚ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٠)
Whatever things you have been given are only the wares of the life of this world and its glitter, and what is with Allah is better and more lasting. Will you not exercise your reason? (60)

Commentary

Do Not Let Worldly Attachments Deceive You

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on how some of the pagans of Makkah resorted to the excuse that if they were to believe, the Arabs would attack them, drive them out of their land, and disrupt their lives. An eloquent response to this claim was provided in those verses.

In the verses under discussion, two additional responses are given:

First, it states: Even assuming you do not accept the faith and manage to achieve a prosperous material life under the shadow of disbelief and polytheism, do not forget: "And how many a city have We destroyed that was exultant in its means of subsistence [and prosperous life]" (وَ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَها).

Indeed, the arrogance born of blessings invited them to rebellion, and rebellion became the source of oppression and injustice, and injustice set the roots of their lives ablaze.

"And these are their dwellings, which have not been inhabited after them except a little" (فَتِلْكَ مَساكِنُهُمْ لَمْ تُسْكَنْ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Yes, their ruined cities and houses remain empty, silent, and ownerless; and if anyone did visit them, it was only a few individuals for a short duration.

"And We were the inheritors!" (وَ كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْوارِثِينَ‌).

Do you, the polytheists of Makkah, also wish to reach that same prosperous life under the shadow of disbelief, the end of which is what has been described? What value can such a life hold?! "Batirat" is derived from the root "batar" (on the weight of bashar), meaning rebellion and arrogance resulting from an abundance of blessings.

The use of "tilka" (that), which is a distal demonstrative pronoun and is often used for observable things, may refer to the lands of "‘Ad," "Thamud," and the "People of Lot," which were located at a not-so-great distance from the people of Makkah—namely in the land of Ahqaf (between Yemen and Sham), or in Wadi al-Qura, or in the land of Sodom. All of these were on the route of the Meccan trade caravans to Sham, and the Arabs could see these empty dwellings with their own eyes, where hardly anyone had resided after them.

The phrase illa qalila (except a little), which appears as an exception, holds three possibilities:

First, that the exception refers to the "inhabitants"; second, to the "dwellings"; and third, to the "habitation" itself.

In the first case, the meaning is that only a small group inhabited these lands after them. In the second case, it means only a few of their houses were later inhabited. In the third case, the concept is that they were inhabited for only a short time, because whoever chose to reside in these ominous and calamity-stricken cities soon had the scroll of their life folded up.

Of course, intending all three meanings does not create a problem according to our methodology, although the first meaning seems closer.

Some have also considered it a reference to the temporary stay of travelers while passing through these regions, and some have interpreted "little" as referring to owls and wild animals. What is certain is that these cities, polluted by sin and polytheism, were so devastated that they never saw prosperity again.

The expression "And We were the inheritors" refers to those lands remaining empty and is also an indication of Allah's true ownership of everything. Even if He temporarily grants the conventional ownership of certain things to some humans, it is not long before it all vanishes, and He shall be the inheritor of all.

The next verse is, in fact, an answer to a hypothetical question: If it is so that Allah destroys the transgressors, then why did He not destroy the polytheists of Makkah and Hijaz with His punishment, given that they had reached the peak of rebellion and there was no ignorance or crime they did not commit?

The Quran says: "And never would your Lord have destroyed the cities until He had sent to their mother [city] a messenger reciting to them Our verses" (وَ ما كانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ الْقُرى‌ حَتَّى يَبْعَثَ فِي أُمِّها رَسُولًا يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِنا).

Indeed, until We establish the proof and send messengers with explicit instructions, We will not punish.

Furthermore, even after the proof is established, We watch their actions; if oppression and injustice stem from them and they become deserving of torment, We punish them: "And We would not destroy the cities unless its people were wrongdoers" (وَ ما كانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ الْقُرى‌ ...إِلَّا وَ أَهْلُها ظالِمُونَ‌).

The expression "ma kana Rabbuka" (your Lord would never) or "ma kunna" (We would never) is evidence that this has been and is the eternal and perpetual Sunnah (way) of Allah: that He does not punish without sufficient establishment of proof.

The phrase "until He had sent to their mother [city] a messenger" indicates that it is not necessary for a prophet to be sent to every single town and village. It is sufficient for a prophet to be sent to a major hub which is the center for disseminating news and the place of the thinkers and intellectuals of a people. This is because the people of that entire region regularly travel there due to the needs of life, and any news there quickly spreads throughout the region and to distant and near points alike.

Just as the fame of the uprising of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in the land of Makkah spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula in a short time and even beyond it, because Makkah was the "Umm al-Qura" (Mother of Cities)—it was both the spiritual center of Hijaz and a commercial center—and it even reached the major centers of civilization of that time within a short period.

Therefore, the verse states a general and universal rule, and the claim of some commentators that it refers specifically to Makkah is completely without evidence. The expression "fi ummiha" is also a general term, as "umm" means mother and primary center, and is not exclusive to Makkah. [1]

The last verse under discussion is a third response to the words of the excuse-seekers who said: "If we believe, the Arabs will attack us and ruin our lives." The Quran says: "And whatever thing you [people] have been given is [only] the enjoyment of worldly life and its adornment" (وَ ما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَمَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ زِينَتُها).

"But what is with Allah (of the endless blessings of the next world and His spiritual gifts in this world) is better and more lasting" (وَ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقى‌).

This is because all material blessings of the world are accompanied by unpleasant side effects and various problems, and no material blessing is found pure from harm or danger.

Moreover, the blessings that are with Allah are incomparable due to their eternity and the fleeting nature of the gifts of this world; therefore, they are both better and more lasting.

In this way, through a simple comparison, every rational person understands that one should not sacrifice the latter for the former. Thus, at the end of the verse, He says: "Will you not then reason?" (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

[1]: Regarding whether this verse includes the independent judgments of reason (mustaqillat al-aqliyyah) or not, we had a discussion in Volume 12, page 51 (under verse 15 of Surah Al-Isra).

"Fakhr Razi" quotes one of the jurists as saying: "If someone bequeaths that a third of his wealth be given to the wisest of people, my fatwa is that this third should be given to those who obey the command of the Truth (Allah). For the wisest of people is the one who gives away a small (fleeting) commodity and takes a vast (lasting) capital, and this is only true in the case of those who obey Allah's command."

Then Fakhr Razi adds that it seems he derived this jurisprudential ruling from the verse under discussion. [1]

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Vol. 25, p. 6.

Ayahs

أَفَمَن وَعَدْنَـٰهُ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ لَـٰقِيهِ كَمَن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُ مَتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ (٦١)
Is he to whom We have given a good promise, which he will receive, like him whom We have provided the wares of the life of this world, but who will be arraigned on the Day of Resurrection? (61)
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٦٢)
The day He will call out to them and ask, ‘Where are My ‘partners’ that you used to claim?’ (62)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنَآ أَغْوَيْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا غَوَيْنَا ۖ تَبَرَّأْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِيَّانَا يَعْبُدُونَ (٦٣)
Those against whom the word had become due will say, ‘Our Lord! These are the ones whom we have perverted. We perverted them as we were perverse ourselves. We repudiate them before You: it was not us that they worshipped.’ (63)
وَقِيلَ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَهْتَدُونَ (٦٤)
It will be said, ‘Invoke your partners!’ So they will invoke them, but they will not respond to them, and they will sight the punishment, wishing they had followed guidance. (64)

Commentary

They Worshiped Their Own Desires

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned those who, for the sake of enjoying and benefiting from worldly blessings, preferred disbelief over faith and polytheism over monotheism. In the verses under discussion, the state of this group on the Day of Resurrection is contrasted with that of the true believers.

First, through a comparison presented in the form of a rhetorical question, Allah calls upon the conscience of all to judge, saying: "Is he then to whom We have promised a goodly promise, which he shall meet, like him whom We have provided with the enjoyment of the life of this world, then on the Day of Resurrection he shall be among those brought up (for punishment)?"

(أَ فَمَنْ وَعَدْناهُ وَعْداً حَسَناً فَهُوَ لاقِيهِ كَمَنْ مَتَّعْناهُ مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ).

Undoubtedly, every awakened conscience prefers the goodly divine promises and His magnificent, eternal blessings over a few days of utilizing vanishing bounties and fleeting pleasures that are followed by eternal pain and suffering.

The phrase "فَهُوَ لاقِيهِ" (which he shall meet) is an emphasis that the divine promise is unfailing, and it must be so, because breaking a promise stems either from ignorance or inability, neither of which has any path to the Essence of Allah.

The phrase "هُوَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ" (he shall be among those brought up) refers to being summoned into the presence of Allah for the accounting of deeds. Some have interpreted it as being brought into the fire of Hell, but the first interpretation is more appropriate. In any case, this expression indicates that these defiled individuals are dragged to this scene reluctantly and against their will—and it must be so, for the terror of the reckoning and punishment has overwhelmed their entire being.

The expression "the life of this world" (al-hayat al-dunya), which appears many times in various Surahs of the Holy Quran, refers to the lowliness of this life in comparison to the "Hereafter" and its eternal, imperishable life. This is because "dunya" is derived from the root "dunuw" (on the weight of ghuluw), which originally means closeness in place, time, or status. Subsequently, "dunya" and "adna" are sometimes applied to small things that are within reach, as opposed to great things; sometimes to lowly matters as opposed to good and sublime ones; and sometimes to the near as opposed to the far. Since the life of this world, compared to the other world, is small, worthless, and immediate, the name "the life of this world" is perfectly suited to it.

Following this statement, the Quran visualizes the scenes of the Resurrection for the disbelievers—scenes that make one's hair stand on end and the body tremble at the mere thought. It says: "And (remember) the day when He will call them and say: Where are My partners whom you used to assert?" (وَ يَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ).

Clearly, this is a reproachful question. On the Day of Resurrection, which is the day when veils and curtains are removed, neither does polytheism have any meaning, nor do the polytheists remain firm in their belief. This question is, in reality, a type of rebuke and punishment, a form of reprimand and penalty.

However, instead of them answering, their deities begin to speak and express their loathing and disavowal of them. We know that the deities were sometimes idols of stone and wood, sometimes holy figures like the angels and Christ (peace be upon him), and sometimes Jinn and devils. Here, the third group speaks, and we read their words in the following verse:

"Those against whom the word has become due will say: Our Lord! These are they whom we led astray; we led them astray as we were astray ourselves (but they followed us of their own will). We declare our innocence before Thee; it was not us that they worshiped." Rather, in reality, they worshiped their own desires (قالَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنا أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا تَبَرَّأْنا إِلَيْكَ ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ).

Therefore, the above verse is similar to verse 28 of Surah Yunus, which says: "And their partners shall say: It was not us that you worshiped." On the Day of Resurrection, the deities turn toward their worshipers and say, "You were not worshiping us!" In this way, the misleading deities—such as Pharaoh, Nimrod, the devils, and the Jinn—declare their disavowal and loathing of such worshipers and rise to defend themselves, even denying that they led them astray, saying: "They followed us of their own volition."

But it is obvious that neither this denial has any effect, nor that disavowal and renunciation; they are certainly partners and participants in the sins of their worshipers [1].

[1]: This possibility has also been suggested in the interpretation of the above verse: that the "speakers" are the leaders of the polytheists and the heads of shirk and kufr (i.e., only a group of the worshipers). While evading the Lord's question regarding the identification of their deities, they speak about their followers and rise to defend themselves, saying: "O Allah, we ourselves were astray when we pursued the path of polytheism, and this group also followed us of their own will, and we led them astray, but in reality, they were not obeying us" (note that here worship means obedience). They were obeying their own desires. However, the interpretation we mentioned above seems closer to the context.

It is noteworthy that on that day, each of these wrong-doing deviants disavows the other, and each tries to cast his sin upon the other.

This is exactly like what we see with our own eyes in a small sample in this world: a group joins hands to commit a crime, but after being arrested and appearing in court, they disavow one another and cast the blame on the other. This is the fate of the misguided and criminal group in this world and the Hereafter.

As we read in verse 22 of Surah Ibrahim: "And Satan will say when the matter is decided: ... I had no authority over you except that I called you and you responded to me; so do not blame me, but blame yourselves" (وَ ما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَ لُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ).

And regarding the polytheists in verse 30 of Surah Saffat, we read that they begin to dispute with one another, each considering the other guilty. But the misleaders explicitly say in response: "We had no authority over you; nay, you were a rebellious people" (وَ ما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ).

In any case, following the question asked of them regarding their deities, to which they remain unable to answer, it is said to them: "Call your partners whom you attributed to Allah" so that they may come to your aid! (وَ قِيلَ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ) [1]. Although they know that the deities can do nothing there, due to the intensity of terror and being cut off from all means, or for the sake of obeying Allah's command—who wishes to disgrace the polytheists and the misguided deities before everyone through such an act—they "stretch out the hand of supplication toward their deities and call them for help" (فَدَعَوْهُمْ).

But it is clear that "they will not answer them" and will not respond to their call (فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُمْ). It is at this moment that "they see the divine punishment before their eyes" (وَ رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ).

"And they wish that they had been guided" (لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَهْتَدُونَ) [2].

[1]: The expression "your partners" (shuraka'akum), despite these partners being attributed to Allah, indicates that "you made these partners and they belong to you."

[2]: Great commentators have held extensive discussions here regarding the phrase "لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَهْتَدُونَ". A large group has considered "law" as a conditional particle and then discussed its result (jaza'). Some consider its result to be a sentence inferred from "ra'aw al-'adhab," taking the implied meaning as: "If they had been guided, they would have seen the punishment in the world with the eye of certainty" (similar to the verse "You shall certainly see the Hellfire"—At-Takathur, verse 6). Others take the implied meaning as: "If they had been guided in the world, they would not have seen the punishment in the Hereafter." Others have suggested different results. However, some believe that the result of the condition is not omitted at all and consider the phrase "ra'aw al-'adhab" as the result of the condition; thus, the meaning of the sentence becomes: "If they had seeing eyes on the Day of Resurrection and were guided, they would see the punishment, but they do not have seeing eyes."

However, beyond all these meanings, there is another meaning which we preferred above, which is that "law" is for expressing a wish (tamanni). You can study more details on this in linguistic books, including "Mughni al-Labib."

For whatever effort they make there will result in nothing but failure and disgrace, because the only way to salvation was faith and righteous deeds, which they lack.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أَجَبْتُمُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٦٥)
The day He will call out to them and say, ‘What response did you give to the apostles?’ (65)
فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَهُمْ لَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٦٦)
That day all information will be withheld from them, so they will not question one another. (66)
فَأَمَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْلِحِينَ (٦٧)
As for him who repents, has faith and acts righteously, maybe he will be among the felicitous. (67)
وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيَخْتَارُ ۗ مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٨)
Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses: they have no choice. Immaculate is Allah and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (68)
وَرَبُّكَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٦٩)
Your Lord knows whatever their breasts conceal, and whatever they disclose. (69)
وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٧٠)
He is Allah, there is no god except Him. All praise belongs to Him in this world and the Hereafter. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back. (70)

Commentary

The verses under discussion follow the discourse presented in the previous verses regarding the polytheists and the questions they will be asked on the Day of Resurrection.

Following the question about their deities, another question is posed regarding their reaction to the prophets. It says: "And [mention] the Day He will call them and say, 'What response did you give to the messengers?'" (وَ يَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أَجَبْتُمُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

Certainly, they will have no answer to this question—just as they had none for the first. Should they say they accepted their call? That would be a lie, and lies find no buyers in that arena. Or should they say they denied them, slandered them, called them sorcerers, labeled them madmen, waged armed conflict against them, and killed them and their followers?! What can they say? Whatever they say will only lead to misery and disgrace! In a place where the great prophets of Allah, when asked on the Day of Resurrection what response the people gave to their call, say: "We have no knowledge [compared to Your knowledge]; indeed, You are the Knower of the unseen" (Al-Ma'idah: 109), what can these blind-hearted polytheists say in response to this question?

Therefore, in the next verse, it says: "Then the news will be obscured from them on that Day," and they will have no answer to provide (فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَنْباءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ).

"Nor will they [be able to] ask one another" to hear a response from each other (فَهُمْ لا يَتَساءَلُونَ).

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, the quality of "blindness" is attributed to the "news" (reports) rather than to the people themselves. It does not say they become blind, but rather: "The news is blind to finding them!" This is because it often happens that a person is unaware of something, yet the news seems to travel from mouth to mouth and seeks him out, just as many social reports are spread this way. However, there, neither do the individuals possess awareness, nor does the news have the capacity to spread! Thus, all news becomes obscured to them; they find no answer to the question of how they responded to the prophets, and an absolute silence envelops them from head to toe.

Since the method of the Quran is to always keep the doors open for disbelievers and sinners so that they may return to the path of truth regardless of their stage of corruption and pollution, the next verse adds: "But as for he who repented, believed, and did righteous work, it is promised that he will be among the successful" (فَأَمَّا مَنْ تابَ وَ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَعَسى‌ أَنْ يَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُفْلِحِينَ). Therefore, the path to your salvation is summarized in three phrases: returning to Allah, faith, and righteous deeds, which are certainly followed by salvation, prosperity, and success.

The use of the expression "عسى" (it is hoped/perhaps), despite the fact that one who possesses faith and righteous deeds will certainly be among the successful, might be because this salvation is conditional upon the persistence and permanence of this state. Since permanence and persistence are not certain for all who repent, the term "عسى" is used.

Some commentators have also stated that when such an expression (عسى) is issued by a Generous Being, its meaning implies certainty, and Allah is the Most Generous of the generous.

The next verse is, in fact, a proof for the negation of polytheism and the invalidity of the polytheists' beliefs. It says: "And your Lord creates what He wills and chooses" (وَ رَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يَخْتارُ).

Creation is in His hand, and management, choice, and selection are also by His will and command.

"They have no choice [in the matter]" (ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ) [1]. The choice of creation is His, the choice of intercession is in His hand, and the choice of sending prophets is also by His will. In short, the choice of everything depends on the will of His Pure Essence.

Therefore, neither can idols do anything, nor even angels or prophets, except by His permission.

In any case, the absolute nature of "choice" here is evidence of its generalization; meaning Allah is the possessor of choice in both cosmic (takwini) and legislative (tashri'i) matters, and both stem from His status as Creator.

Despite this, how can they pursue the path of polytheism and how can they turn toward other than Allah?! Thus, at the end of the verse, it says: "Exalted is Allah and high above what they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

In narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, the above verse has been interpreted regarding the issue of the choice and selection of the Infallible Imam by Allah. The phrase "ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ" (they have no choice in this matter) has also been applied to this meaning. This is, in fact, an instance of explaining a clear manifestation (misdaq), for the matter of preserving the religion and its laws, and choosing an infallible leader for this purpose, is not possible except from Allah's side [1].

The next verse, which speaks of Allah's vast knowledge, is in fact an emphasis or a proof for what was stated in the previous verse regarding Allah's extensive choice. It says: "And your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they declare" (وَ رَبُّكَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَ ما يُعْلِنُونَ).

This encompassment of His over all things is a proof of His choice over all things, and simultaneously a threat to the polytheists so they do not imagine that Allah is unaware of their intentions and conspiracies.

The final verse under discussion, which serves as a conclusion and explanation for the previous verses regarding the negation of polytheism, reflects four attributes of the Divine attributes, all of which are branches of His Creatorship and His being the Absolute Chooser. First, it says: "And He is Allah; there is no deity except Him" (وَ هُوَ اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

How is it possible for there to be a deity other than Him, while the Creator is exclusively Him, and all choices belong to Him? Those who cling to idols under the pretext of intercession and the like are gravely mistaken.

[1] The "ما" in the phrase "ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ" is negative, though some have suggested it might be relative (mawsulah) and conjoined to the omitted object of "يختار," but this possibility is very remote.

[1] "Usul al-Kafi" and the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim" (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 136).

Secondly, all blessings, whether in this world or the next, are from Him, and this is a necessity of His absolute Creatorship. Therefore, it adds: "To Him belongs [all] praise in the first [life] and the hereafter" (لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى‌ وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

Thirdly: "To Him belongs [all] adjudication" (وَ لَهُ الْحُكْمُ).

It is obvious that when He is the Creator and the Chooser, the sovereignty of both the cosmic and legislative realms will also be in His control.

Fourthly: "And to Him you will be returned" (for accounting, reward, and punishment) (وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

It is He who created you, He who is aware of your deeds, and He who is the Judge of the Day of Recompense; therefore, your accounting and recompense will also be in His hand.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِضِيَآءٍ ۖ أَفَلَا تَسْمَعُونَ (٧١)
Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the night perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what god other than Allah can bring you light? Will you not listen?’ (71)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّهَارَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٧٢)
Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the day perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what god other than Allah can bring you night wherein you can rest? Will you not perceive?’ (72)
وَمِن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
He has made for you the night and the day out of His mercy, so that you may rest therein and that you may seek His bounty and so that you may give thanks. (73)
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٧٤)
The day He will call out to them and say, ‘Where are My ‘partners’ that you used to claim?’ (74)
وَنَزَعْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا فَقُلْنَا هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ فَعَلِمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٧٥)
We shall draw from every nation a witness and say, ‘Produce your evidence.’ Then they will know that all reality belongs to Allah and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (75)

Commentary

The Great Blessing of Day and Night

The verses under discussion speak of a vast portion of Divine favors which serve both as a proof for the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of polytheism—thereby completing the previous discussion—and as an example of Allah's blessings for which He is worthy of praise and glorification (the praise alluded to in the preceding verses). Furthermore, they serve as a testimony to His free will in the system of creation and the governance of this world.

First, referring to the great blessing of light and the brightness of day, which is the source of all movement and activity, He says: "Say: 'Tell me, if Allah should make the night continuous for you until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you light? Will you not then hear?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ اللَّيْلَ سَرْمَداً إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِضِياءٍ أَفَلا تَسْمَعُونَ) [^1]. Here, the term diya’ (light) is used because the primary purpose of the day is that very light—the light upon which the life of all living beings depends. If there were no sunlight, no tree would grow, no flower would smile, no bird would fly, no living human would exist, and not a drop of rain would fall. Sarmad means eternal and perpetual. (Some consider it derived from the root sard, meaning "successive," with the mim being extra, but it appears that this root independently means eternal and perpetual.) [^1]

[^1]: Ara’aytum is a phrase usually interpreted to mean "tell me" (akhbiruni), but as we have previously mentioned, it sometimes means "do you know?" (hal ‘alimtum).

The next verse speaks of the blessing of "darkness and obscurity," stating: "Say: 'Tell me, if Allah should make the day continuous for you until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you a night wherein you may rest? Will you not then see?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ النَّهارَ سَرْمَداً إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ).

In the third verse, which is effectively a conclusion drawn from the two previous verses, He says: "It is out of His mercy that He has made for you the night and the day, that you may rest therein [during the night] and that you may seek of His bounty [during the day], and perhaps you will be grateful." (وَ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ).

Indeed, the vastness of Allah's mercy necessitates that He provides all the means for your life. On one hand, you need work, effort, and movement, which is impossible without the light of day; on the other hand, you need rest and tranquility, which is not complete without the darkness of night.

Today, it has been scientifically proven that in the presence of light, all systems of the human body become active and vibrant: blood circulation, the respiratory system, heart movement, and other organs. If light shines excessively, the cells become exhausted, and vitality gives way to fatigue. Conversely, the body's systems sink into deep rest and relaxation in the darkness of night, renewing their strength and vigor under its shadow. (We have explained this concept in Volume 8, page 343, and Volume 12, page 41).

It is noteworthy that when He speaks of eternal night, He says at the end of the verse: "Will you not then hear?" And when He speaks of perpetual day, He says: "Will you not then see?" This difference in expression may be because the sense most compatible with night is the ear and hearing, while the sense most compatible with day is the eye and sight. To this extent, the Holy Quran exercises precision in its expressions.

It is also worth noting that at the end of this discourse, He raises the issue of "gratitude"—gratitude for the calculated system of light and darkness; a gratitude that inevitably compels man toward the recognition and knowledge of the Benefactor, and a gratitude that serves as the motive for faith in ideological discussions.

Once again, after mentioning a portion of the proofs for Monotheism and the refutation of polytheism, He returns to the same question posed in the previous verses, saying: "And [mention] the Day He will call them and say, 'Where are My partners whom you used to claim?'" (وَ يَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ).

This verse is exactly the same as the one that appeared previously (Verse 62 of this same Surah).

This repetition may be because, on the Day of Resurrection, in the first stage, an individual question is put to them so they may return to their conscience and feel ashamed. However, in the second stage, a question is posed in the presence of the public and the witnesses (alluded to in the next verse) so that they may be disgraced and ashamed of their actions before them.

Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "And on that Day, We will extract a witness from every nation" (وَ نَزَعْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً) [^1]. Then, "We will say to the ignorant and straying polytheists: 'Bring your proof [for your polytheism]!'" (فَقُلْنا هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ). It is here that all matters become clear as day, "and they will know that the truth belongs to Allah" (فَعَلِمُوا أَنَّ الْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ).

"And all that they used to invent [as falsehoods] will be lost from them" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ).

These witnesses, based on the context of other Quranic verses, are the Prophets; every Prophet is a witness over his own nation, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who is the Seal of the Prophets, is a witness over all Prophets and nations, as we read in Verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa: "So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and We bring you [O Muhammad] against these [people] as a witness?" (فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً).

In this manner, an assembly is formed in the presence of the Prophets, and these blind-hearted and stubborn polytheists are interrogated in that great gathering. It is there that they become acquainted with the depth of the tragedy of polytheism and clearly see the truth of the Lord and the futility of idols.

Interestingly, the Quran here uses the expression "ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ" (what they used to invent will be lost from them), meaning their baseless fantasies regarding idols will all vanish and disappear from their sight. This is because the arena of Resurrection is the arena of Truth, and there is no place for falsehood there; falsehood is lost and obliterated from that scene.

If falsehood here [in this world] covers itself with a veil of truth and engages in deception for a few days, there [in the hereafter], the veils of deceit and trickery are pulled aside, and nothing remains but the Truth.

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ نَزَعْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً", we read:

"And from this nation, its Imam [will be the witness]."

This statement indicates that in every age and time, an infallible witness and authority is necessary for the nation, and the above hadith is an explanation of a manifestation of this meaning. [^1]

[^1]: The term naza‘na is from the root naz‘, meaning to pull something out from its place, and here it refers to summoning a witness from among every group and nation. [^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 20.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ قَـٰرُونَ كَانَ مِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ فَبَغَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ مَآ إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُۥ لَتَنُوٓأُ بِٱلْعُصْبَةِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ لَا تَفْرَحْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَرِحِينَ (٧٦)
Korah indeed belonged to the people of Moses, but he bullied them. We had given him so much treasures that their chests indeed proved heavy for a band of stalwarts. When his people said to him, ‘Do not boast! Indeed Allah does not like the boasters. (76)
وَٱبْتَغِ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۖ وَلَا تَنسَ نَصِيبَكَ مِنَ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَأَحْسِن كَمَآ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَلَا تَبْغِ ٱلْفَسَادَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٧٧)
Seek the abode of the Hereafter by means of what Allah has given you, while not forgetting your share of this world. Be good [to others] just as Allah has been good to you, and do not try to cause corruption in the land. Indeed Allah does not like the agents of corruption.’ (77)
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عِندِىٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَهْلَكَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مَنْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرُ جَمْعًا ۚ وَلَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذُنُوبِهِمُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٧٨)
He said, ‘I have indeed been given [all] this because of the knowledge that I have.’ Did he not know that Allah had already destroyed before him some of the generations who were more powerful than him and greater in amassing [wealth]? The guilty will not be questioned about their sins. (78)

Commentary

The Self-Centered Wealthy Man of the Israelites

The extraordinary story of Moses (peace be upon him) and his struggle against Pharaoh was detailed in a portion of the previous verses of this Surah, and what needed to be said about them was mentioned—a discourse that was sufficiently inspiring.

In another section of the verses of this Surah, the text speaks of another conflict of the Israelites with a wealthy and rebellious man from among themselves named Qarun (Korah); a Qarun who was the manifestation of wealth mixed with pride, arrogance, and transgression.

In principle, throughout his life, Moses (peace be upon him) fought against three oppressive, transgressive powers: "Pharaoh," who was the manifestation of governmental power; "Qarun," who was the manifestation of wealth; and "the Samiri," who was the manifestation of industry, deception, and delusion.

Although the most important struggle of Moses (peace be upon him) was against the power of the government, the latter two struggles also possess their own importance and contain great instructive lessons.

It is well-known that Qarun was a close relative of Moses (peace be upon him) (his cousin, uncle, or maternal cousin), and he possessed considerable knowledge regarding the Torah. Initially, he was in the ranks of the believers, but the pride of wealth drew him into the embrace of disbelief and sent him to the depths of the earth. It drove him to challenge the Prophet of Allah, and his exemplary death became a lesson for all, the details of which we read in the verses under discussion.

First, it says: "Indeed, Qarun was from the people of Moses, but he tyrannized them" (إِنَّ قارُونَ كانَ مِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى‌ فَبَغى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

The cause of this rebellion and tyranny was that he had acquired immense wealth, and because he lacked sufficient capacity and strong faith, this abundant wealth deceived him and led him toward deviation and arrogance.

The Quran says: "And We gave him of treasures whose keys would burden a band of strong men" (وَ آتَيْناهُ مِنَ الْكُنُوزِ ما إِنَّ مَفاتِحَهُ لَتَنُوأُ بِالْعُصْبَةِ أُولِي الْقُوَّةِ).

"Mafatih" is the plural of "maftah" (on the pattern of maktab), meaning a place where something is stored, such as chests in which wealth is kept.

Accordingly, the meaning of the verse becomes that Qarun possessed so much gold, silver, and precious valuables that their chests were moved with difficulty by a group of powerful men.

Considering that "usbah" refers to a group that has joined hands, are powerful, and hold together like nerves (asab), it becomes clear how vast the volume of Qarun's jewelry and high-priced assets was (some say "usbah" refers to ten to forty people).

The phrase "tanu’" is from the root "naw’," meaning to rise with difficulty and heaviness, and it is used for heavy loads that, when a person carries them, cause him to lean from side to side due to their weight! What we mentioned above regarding the interpretation of "mafatih" is something that a vast group of commentators and linguists have accepted, while others have considered "mafatih" to be the plural of miftah (on the pattern of minbar with a kasra on the mim), meaning "key," and they say the keys to Qarun's treasures were so numerous that several strong men struggled to carry them! Those who chose this meaning have themselves struggled to justify how so many keys for treasures are possible; in any case, the first interpretation is clearer and more correct.

Furthermore, since linguists have given multiple meanings for this very word (maftah with a kasra), including "treasury"—meaning the place for collecting wealth—the first meaning is closer to reality and far from any exaggeration.

In any case, this word should not be confused with "mafatih," which is the plural of miftah meaning "key" [1]. Let us move past this discussion and see what the Israelites said to Qarun.

The Quran says: "Recall when his people said to him: 'Do not exult [with pride and negligence], for Allah does not love the exultant'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُ قَوْمُهُ لا تَفْرَحْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْفَرِحِينَ‌) [2].

[1]: Some commentators have also mentioned a metaphorical meaning for "key" here, saying it means that the "key" to obtaining all that wealth and preserving it was difficult for strong men, but this interpretation seems very unlikely. (For more information on the linguistic concept of this word, refer to Lisan al-Arab).

[2]: "Farihin" is the plural of "farih," meaning one who has become proud and arrogant due to acquiring something, is intoxicated by the wine of victory, and is bursting with joy.

After this advice, they gave him four other rich and life-altering counsels, which together form a complete five-link chain.

First, they said: "But seek, through that which Allah has given you, the home of the Hereafter" (وَ ابْتَغِ فِيما آتاكَ اللَّه الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ).

This points to the fact that wealth and riches, contrary to the imagination of some narrow-minded people, are not bad things; what matters is to see in which path they are utilized. If they are used to "seek the home of the Hereafter," what could be better? But if they become a means for pride, negligence, tyranny, transgression, and following whims and lusts, what could be worse?! This is the same logic clearly mentioned in the famous statement of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), regarding the world:

"Whoever looks at it [as an end], it blinds him; and whoever looks through it [as a means], it gives him sight!" [1]

And Qarun was someone who, possessing those vast assets, had the power for many social good works, but what use was it when his pride did not allow him to see the truths?

In the second advice, they added: "And do not forget your share of the world" (وَ لا تَنْسَ نَصِيبَكَ مِنَ الدُّنْيا).

It is a reality that every human has a limited share and portion of the world; that is, the wealth that is absorbed by his body or spent on his clothing and housing is a specific amount, and anything beyond that is in no way absorbable, and man must not forget this truth.

How much can one person eat? How much clothing can he wear? How many houses and how many mounts can he have? And when dying, how many shrouds can he take with him? Therefore, the rest is inevitably the share of others, and man is merely their trustee!

How beautifully the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), said:

"O son of Adam! Whatever you earn beyond your basic needs, you are only a treasurer for others regarding it!" [1]

Another interpretation for this sentence is seen in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators, which can be reconciled with the above interpretation, and it is possible that both meanings are intended (since using a word for more than one meaning is permissible). That is:

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), regarding the interpretation of the phrase "And do not forget your share of the world," it is stated:

"Do not forget your health, your strength, your free time, your youth, and your vitality, to seek the Hereafter through them!"

According to this interpretation, the above sentence is a warning to all humans not to lose opportunities and capitals, for opportunity passes like the clouds [2].

The third counsel was: "And do good as Allah has done good to you" (وَ أَحْسِنْ كَما أَحْسَنَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ‌).

This is also a reality: that man always has his eyes fixed on Allah's grace and requests every kind of good and blessing from His presence, and has all sorts of expectations from Him. In such a state, how can he ignore the explicit or implicit requests of others and pass by them all with indifference?! In other words, just as Allah has bestowed upon you, bestow upon others. We read a similar statement in verse 22 of Surah An-Nur regarding pardon and forgiveness:

"And let them pardon and overlook. Would you not love that Allah should forgive you?"

This sentence can be interpreted in another way: that sometimes Allah gives great blessings to a person that he does not need entirely for his personal life. He gives an intellectual capacity that is effective not just for managing one individual, but for managing a country. He gives knowledge that not just one person, but a society can benefit from. He gives wealth and riches suitable for great social programs.

The implicit meaning of such divine blessings is that not all of it belongs to you; rather, you are the deputy of the Lord in transferring it to others. Allah has given you this blessing so that He may manage His servants through your hand.

Finally, the "fourth" counsel was: "Lest these material possibilities deceive you and you use them in the way of 'corruption' and 'causing mischief': 'And desire not corruption in the land. Indeed, Allah does not love the corrupters'" (وَ لا تَبْغِ الْفَسادَ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُفْسِدِينَ‌).

This is also a reality: that many faithless wealthy people, sometimes due to the madness of seeking more and sometimes for the sake of superiority, resort to corruption. They drive society toward deprivation and poverty, monopolize everything, want people as their slaves and servants, and destroy anyone who opens their mouth in protest. If they cannot do so directly, they isolate them through slander via their mysterious agents; in short, they drive society toward corruption and ruin.

In a brief summary, we reach the point that these advisors first tried to break Qarun's pride. In the second stage, they warned that the world is a means, not an end. In the third stage, they alerted him that of what you have, you can only consume a small portion. In the fourth stage, they reminded him of the truth that you must not forget Allah has done good to you, so you must do good, otherwise He will take His blessings back from you. And in the fifth stage, they cautioned him against corruption on earth, which is the direct result of forgetting the previous four principles.

It is not exactly clear who these advisors were. What is certain is that they were learned, pious, vigilant, perceptive, and courageous men. However, the possibility some have raised that it was Moses himself is very unlikely, because the Quran says: "When his people said to him" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُ قَوْمُهُ‌).

Now the time has come to see what response the rebellious and tyrannical man of the Israelites gave to these sincere preachers. Qarun, with the same state of pride and arrogance stemming from his countless wealth, said: "I was only given it because of knowledge I have!" (قالَ إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي‌).

"It is not your concern how I deal with my wealth! Since I was involved in creating it with my knowledge and awareness, I do not need anyone's guidance or direction in spending it! Furthermore, Allah must have considered me worthy of this wealth to have granted it to me; He has also taught me the way to spend it, I know better than others, and there is no need for your interference!"

"And beyond all this, I have worked hard, suffered, and endured great toil to accumulate this wealth. If others have the merit and ability, why don't they work hard? I am not obstructing them! And if they do not have it, it is better that they stay hungry and die!" [1]

These are the decayed and scandalous logics that faithless wealthy people often express to those who advise them.

It is also noteworthy that the Quran has left the meaning of which "knowledge" Qarun relied upon in acquiring this wealth unspecified. Was it the science of alchemy (kimia), as some commentators have said? Or was it the knowledge of trade, agriculture, and industrial techniques? Or was it his specific management skills through which he was able to seize this enormous wealth? Or all of these? It is not unlikely that the concept of the verse has a broad meaning and includes all of them. (Regardless, of course, of whether alchemy—the science by which gold can be made from copper and the like—is a myth or a reality).

Here, the Quran gives a crushing response to Qarun—and the Qaruns of the world: "Did he not know that Allah had destroyed before him of generations those who were greater than him in power and greater in accumulation [of wealth]?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ أَهْلَكَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ مَنْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُ قُوَّةً وَ أَكْثَرُ جَمْعاً).

You say that what you have is through your knowledge and science, but you forgot that there were many more learned, powerful, and wealthy than you. Were they able to escape from the clutches of divine punishment?

The enlightened ones among the Israelites had told Qarun: "What Allah has given you..." (ما آتاك اللَّه ...), but this insolent, bold man countered this statement and said: "What I have is from my own knowledge." However, Allah, in the above sentence, reveals the insignificance of his power and strength against His will and decree.

And at the end of the verse, with a short and meaningful sentence, He gives him another warning and says: "At the time of the descent of divine punishment, the criminals are not asked about their sins" (وَ لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذُنُوبِهِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ‌). There is no room at all for question and answer; it is a decisive, painful, crushing, and sudden punishment.

That is, today the aware ones among the Israelites advise Qarun and give him room for thought and response, but when the proof is completed and the divine punishment arrives, there is no longer room for thought or discordant and arrogant words; divine punishment and destruction occur simultaneously!

Here, the question arises: what is meant by this "questioning" that is denied regarding the criminals? In this world or the Hereafter? Some commentators have chosen the first, and some the second, and there is no obstacle to both being intended. That is, neither at the time of the punishment of extirpation in this world are they asked so that they might try to procrastinate, make excuses, or portray themselves as innocent; nor in the Resurrection, because there everything is clear without questioning, and according to the Quran, the faces themselves testify to the state of the criminals! "The criminals will be known by their marks" (Surah Ar-Rahman, Verse 41). Thus, the verse under discussion is consistent with Verse 39 of Surah Ar-Rahman which says: "Then on that Day none will be asked about his sin, neither man nor jinn."

Here another question arises: how is this expression compatible with Verse 92 of Surah Al-Hijr which says: "So by your Lord, We will surely question them all"?

This question can also be answered in two ways: First, the Resurrection has multiple stations (mawaqif); in some stations they will be questioned, but in other stations everything is clear and does not need questioning. Second, questioning is of two types: "questioning for investigation" and "questioning for reproach." In the Resurrection, there is no need for "investigation," as everything is manifest and there is no need for explanation, but "reproachful questioning" exists there, which is itself a type of psychological punishment for the criminals. Just like the question a father asks his wayward son, saying: "Did I not serve you all this much? And was the reward for all that service betrayal and corruption?!" (While both are aware of the events, and the father's purpose is to reproach the son).

Ayahs

فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فِى زِينَتِهِۦ ۖ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا يَـٰلَيْتَ لَنَا مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ قَـٰرُونُ إِنَّهُۥ لَذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ (٧٩)
So he emerged before his people in his finery. Those who desired the life of the world said, ‘We wish we had like what Korah has been given! Indeed he is greatly fortunate.’ (79)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوَابُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ (٨٠)
Those who were given knowledge said [to them], ‘Woe to you! Allah’s reward is better for someone who has faith and acts righteously, and no one will receive it except the patient.’ (80)
فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِۦ وَبِدَارِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ مِن فِئَةٍ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنتَصِرِينَ (٨١)
So We caused the earth to swallow him and his house, and he had no party that might protect him from Allah, nor could he rescue himself. (81)
وَأَصْبَحَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَمَنَّوْا۟ مَكَانَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَقُولُونَ وَيْكَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن مَّنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا لَخَسَفَ بِنَا ۖ وَيْكَأَنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٢)
By dawn those who longed to be in his place the day before were saying, ‘Don’t you see that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it? Had Allah not shown us favour, He might have made the earth swallow us too. Don’t you see that the faithless do not prosper?’ (82)

Commentary

The Madness of Displaying Wealth!

Usually, arrogant wealthy individuals fall prey to various forms of madness, one branch of which is the "madness of displaying wealth." They derive pleasure from flaunting their riches to others; they feel a sense of tranquility by riding their expensive, swift mounts through the crowds of the barefooted, casting dust upon their faces and humiliating them! Although this very display of wealth is often the bane of their existence—as it nurtures grudges in hearts and mobilizes emotions against them—it frequently happens that this ugly and shameful act folds the scroll of their lives or scatters their wealth to the wind!

It is possible that such a mad act is driven by motives like "enticing the greedy" or "subjugating the rebellious," yet they perform this act even without such motives; it is a type of whim rather than a calculated plan or strategy.

In any case, Qarun (Korah) was no exception to this rule; rather, he was considered its prime example. The Quran expresses this in a single sentence in the verses under discussion, stating:

"So he came out before his people (the Children of Israel) in his adornment" (فَخَرَجَ عَلى‌ قَوْمِهِ فِي زِينَتِهِ‌).

The expression "in his adornment" (فِي زِينَتِهِ) speaks to the fact that he employed all his power and capability to display his ultimate finery and greatest wealth. It goes without saying what a man with such wealth could do! In historical accounts, many stories—or perhaps legends—have been narrated in this regard. Some have written that Qarun paraded among the Children of Israel with a crowd of four thousand people, while four thousand men rode expensive horses with red coverings. He brought with him fair-skinned maidens seated upon golden saddles placed on white mules, their clothes red, and all of them drenched in gold ornaments! Some have recorded the number of his retinue as seventy thousand, along with other similar details.

However, even if we consider these accounts exaggerated, it cannot be denied that he had much at his disposal to put on display.

At this point—as is usual—the people split into two groups: the worldly-minded majority, whose hearts were shaken by this dazzling scene and who heaved a burning sigh from their souls, wishing they were in Qarun's place, even for a day, an hour, or a moment! What a sweet and attractive life! What an exhilarating and pleasurable world! As the Quran says: "Those who desired the life of this world said, 'Oh, would that we had the like of what has been given to Qarun!'" (قالَ الَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا يا لَيْتَ لَنا مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ قارُونُ‌).

"Indeed, he is the possessor of a great fortune!" (إِنَّهُ لَذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ‌).

Bravo to Qarun and his overflowing wealth! What glory and majesty! What grandeur! History does not remember the like of him; this is a God-given greatness! ... and similar such remarks.

In truth, at this moment, the great furnace of Divine trial grew hot. On one side, Qarun was placed in the center of the furnace and had to undergo the test of his own stubbornness; on the other side, the worldly-minded among the Children of Israel stood around this furnace.

And certainly, the painful punishment is that which follows such a display, causing one to fall from that peak of greatness into the depths of the earth!

However, in contrast to this large group, a small group of scholars, thinkers, the pious, and the faithful—whose horizon of thought was higher and loftier than these matters—were present there. These were people who did not measure personality by the standard of gold and power; people who did not seek values in material possibilities; people who always cast a mocking smile at such ridiculous displays and despised these hollow minds. Yes, a group of them were there, as the Quran says: "But those who had been given knowledge said, 'Woe to you! What are you saying? The reward of Allah is better for those who believe and do righteous deeds'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوابُ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِمَنْ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً).

And then they added: "And none shall attain it except those who are patient" (وَ لا يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا الصَّابِرُونَ‌).

Those who show resilience against the exciting glitz and ornaments of the world; those who stand manfully against deprivations and do not bow their heads before the ignoble; those who stand firm like a mountain in the crucible of Divine trial—the trial of wealth, property, fear, and calamity. Yes, these are the ones worthy of Allah's reward.

Certainly, the phrase "those who were given knowledge" (الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ) refers to the faithful scholars of the Children of Israel, among whom were great men like Joshua (Yusha). But it is interesting that in contrast to the phrase "those who desire the life of this world," which was used for the first group, the text does not say "those who desire the life of the Hereafter," but rather emphasizes "knowledge." This is because "knowledge" is the raw material and the root of faith, resilience, and the love for Allah's reward and the abode of the Hereafter.

Furthermore, the expression "those who were given knowledge" is a crushing response to Qarun, who considered himself a scholar. The Quran says: The scholars are these people whose horizon of thought is so high, not you, the stubborn and arrogant one! Thus, we see once again that the root of all blessings and goodness returns to true knowledge and wisdom.

With this act, Qarun brought his rebellion and defiance to its peak. However, in histories and narrations, another event is recounted here which indicates the ultimate shamelessness of Qarun: One day, Musa عليه السلام said to Qarun: "Allah has commanded me to take the right of the needy (Zakat) from your wealth." When Qarun became aware of the quantity and quality of the Zakat and realized through a simple calculation what a massive sum he would have to pay, he refused. To exonerate himself, he rose to challenge Musa عليه السلام. He stood among a group of wealthy Israelites and said: "O people! Musa wants to consume your wealth. He brought the command for prayer and you accepted; you accepted all other matters as well. Will you now go under this burden of giving your wealth to him?!" They said: "No, but how can we oppose him?"

Here, a satanic thought occurred to Qarun. He said: "I have thought of a good way. In my opinion, we must build a case of indecency against him! We must send for a prostitute from the harlots of the Children of Israel to go to Musa and accuse him of having an affair with her!" They liked the idea and sent for the woman, saying: "We will give you whatever you desire if you testify that Musa had an illicit relationship with you!" She accepted the proposal. This was on one hand. On the other hand, Qarun came to Musa and said: "It would be good if you gathered the Children of Israel and recited Allah's commands to them." Musa accepted and gathered them.

They said: "O Musa! Recite the commands of your Lord." He said: "Allah has commanded me that you worship none but Him, maintain family ties, and do such and such. And regarding the adulterer, He has commanded that if it be a married person, they should be stoned!" They (the conspiring wealthy men of Israel) said here: "Even if it were you yourself?!" He said: "Yes, even if it were I myself!!" At this point, they reached the ultimate degree of insolence and said: "We know that you yourself have committed this act, and have gone to such-and-forth prostitute." They immediately sent for that prostitute and asked: "What do you testify?"

Musa عليه السلام turned to her and said: "I adjure you by Allah to speak the truth openly!" Upon hearing this, the prostitute was deeply shaken; she trembled and was transformed. She said: "Now that you say this, I will reveal the truth. These people invited me and set a heavy reward for me to accuse you, but I bear witness that you are pure and the Messenger of Allah!" In another narration, it is said the woman cried: "Woe to me! I have done every wrong deed, but I have not slandered a Prophet of Allah." She then showed the two bags of money they had given her and told the whole story.

Musa عليه السلام fell into prostration and wept. It was then that the command for the punishment of the evil-natured, conspiring Qarun was issued.

In this same narration, it is mentioned that Allah placed the command of "Khasf" (sinking into the earth) at the disposal of Musa عليه السلام [1]. Here, the Holy Quran says: "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his home" (فَخَسَفْنا بِهِ وَ بِدارِهِ الْأَرْضَ‌).

Yes, when rebellion, defiance, the humiliation of the destitute believers, and conspiracy against the pure Prophet of Allah reach their peak, the hand of Divine power emerges from the sleeve and ends the life of the transgressors. It crushes them so thoroughly that their lives become a lesson for all.

The issue of "Khasf," which here means sinking and being hidden in the earth, has occurred many times throughout human history—where a severe earthquake occurs, the earth splits open, and swallows a city or settlements. But this Khasf was different from other cases; its primary prey was only Qarun and his treasures.

How strange! Pharaoh sinks in the waves of the Nile, and Qarun in the depths of the earth. The water, which is the source of life, is commissioned to destroy the Pharaohs, and the earth, which is the cradle of tranquility, becomes the graveyard of Qarun and his followers.

It is certain that Qarun was not alone in that house; he and his associates, he and his comrades, he and his cruel and oppressive companions all sank into the depths of the earth.

"And he had no host to help him against Allah, nor was he of those who could save themselves!" (فَما كانَ لَهُ مِنْ فِئَةٍ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَ ما كانَ مِنَ المُنْتَصِرِينَ‌).

Neither his hirelings, nor his close friends, nor his property and wealth—none of them saved him from the clutches of Divine punishment, and all sank to the bottom of the earth!

[1]: Al-Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 84), as well as Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani and other commentators, have recorded this hadith with variations under the verses discussed.

The final verse under discussion reflects the strange transformation of yesterday's spectators, who had been filled with joy and excitement at the sight of Qarun's glory and majesty, wishing they could be in his place forever or at least for a moment. It is truly remarkable and instructive. It says: "And those who had longed for his position the day before began to say, 'Ah! It is indeed Allah who extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts it'" and the key to it is only in His hand (وَ أَصْبَحَ الَّذِينَ تَمَنَّوْا مَكانَهُ بِالْأَمْسِ يَقُولُونَ وَيْكَأَنَّ اللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَ يَقْدِرُ).

Today it has been proven to us that no one possesses anything of their own; whatever exists is from His side. Neither His giving is a proof of His satisfaction and pleasure with someone, nor is His withholding a proof of their lack of value before Allah.

He tests individuals and nations with these very riches and reveals their inner nature and character.

Then they fell into the thought that if their insistent prayer of yesterday had been answered and Allah had placed them in Qarun's position, what calamity would have befallen them today?

Therefore, they set out to thank Him for this blessing and said: "Had Allah not been gracious to us, He would have caused the earth to swallow us too!" (لَوْ لا أَنْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنا لَخَسَفْنا بِنا).

"Ah! It seems that the disbelievers never prosper" (وَيْكَأَنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْكافِرُونَ‌).

Now we see the truth with our own eyes, and the result of arrogance, negligence, and the end of disbelief and lust.

And we also understand how terrifying such lives are, which have such an enchanting outward appearance.

Furthermore, from the final sentence of this story, it becomes clearly evident that in the end, the arrogant Qarun died in disbelief and lack of faith, even though he was once numbered among the reciters of the Torah and the scholars of the Children of Israel and had a close kinship with Musa.

Key Points

1- The Qaruns of Yesterday and Today!

The story of Qarun—the symbol of the arrogant wealthy man—which the Quran has beautifully expressed in seven verses, unveils many truths in human life.

This story illustrates the fact that the arrogance and intoxication of wealth sometimes lead a person to various forms of madness: the madness of displaying wealth and flaunting it to others, and the madness of deriving pleasure from humiliating the poor! Again, this very arrogance, intoxication, and boundless love for silver and gold sometimes cause one to commit the ugliest and most shameful sins, to rise against the pure Prophet of Allah, to struggle and fight against truth and reality, and even to level the most shameless accusations against the purest individuals—even using one's wealth to enlist the help of fallen women to achieve one's goal.

The arrogance and intoxication resulting from wealth do not allow a person to hear the advice of counselors or to act upon the words of well-wishers.

These oblivious, arrogant people consider themselves more knowledgeable and aware than everyone else. Under the assumption that their wealth—which is sometimes obtained through usurping the rights of others—is proof of their intellect, intelligence, and ingenuity, they perceive everyone as ignorant and themselves as wise! Their state even reaches a point where they assert their existence before the Lord, speak of independence, and say: "What we have found was due to our own initiative, overflowing intelligence, creativity, talent, and unique information."

And we saw where the end of such wicked, arrogant people leads! If Qarun sank into the depths of the earth with his associates and all his wealth and was destroyed, others are destroyed in different ways. Sometimes even the earth swallows their wealth in another form; that is, they turn their massive riches into palaces, gardens, and lands that they will never use. Sometimes they even give their property to buy barren and dead lands, with the fantasy of dividing and selling them at exorbitant prices—and in this way, the earth swallows their wealth!

Such shallow-minded individuals, because they find no way to spend their massive wealth, turn toward imaginary values. They collect a set of old broken bowls and pots as "precious antiques!", and sometimes a collection of colorless paintings or even a collection of postage stamps, banknotes, and the like belonging to past years or centuries, as the most valuable commodities in their castles and palaces—which, if we look at them closely, we sometimes see their place is only in the trash!

They have provided this luxurious and imaginary life while in their city and land, and sometimes in their immediate neighborhood, there are deprived individuals who go to sleep hungry at night. And how strange that their consciences have so withered that they do not feel even the slightest discomfort from this situation! Sometimes their animals have the most comfortable lives, even benefiting from teachers, doctors, and medicine, while oppressed humans live near them in the worst conditions, or groan in the bed of illness with neither a doctor present at their bedside nor a drop of medicine.

All these discussions sometimes concern specific individuals in a society, and sometimes a country—meaning a country becomes "Qarunic" in contrast to other countries of the world! Just as we see in our own era regarding countries like America and many European nations.

They have provided the most luxurious life for themselves at the cost of exploiting the people of the Third World and poor, destitute countries, such that sometimes their excess food thrown into trash bins would be sufficient to feed millions of hungry people if collected properly.

When we say "poor countries," it is not that they are truly poor; they are, in fact, robbed and plundered. Sometimes the best and most precious underground resources are at their disposal, but these "super-plunderers" take everything and leave them on the black soil.

These are the leech-like Qaruns who have built the foundations of their palaces of injustice upon the ruins of the huts of the oppressed (Mustad'afin). Until the oppressed of the world join hands and send them to the depths of the earth like Qarun, the state of the world will remain as such: they drink wine and let out drunken laughs, while these others must sit with their knees hugged in grief and weep!

2- Where did Qarun get this wealth?

Interestingly, from verses 23 and 24 of Surah Ghafir (al-Mu'min), it is clearly understood that the mission of Musa عليه السلام from the beginning was to struggle against three people: Pharaoh, his minister Haman, and the arrogant wealthy man Qarun: "And We certainly sent Musa with Our signs and a clear authority to Pharaoh, Haman, and Qarun; but they said, '[He is] a magician and a liar.'"

From this verse, it is understood that he was a collaborator with the Pharaohs and aligned with them. In histories, we also read that he was, on one hand, Pharaoh's representative among the Children of Israel [1] and, on the other hand, the treasurer of Pharaoh's treasures [2]. It is here that Qarun's file becomes clearer: to chain the Children of Israel and plunder all their existence, Pharaoh chose a hypocritical, cunning, and sufficiently ruthless man from among the Children of Israel and gave him the reins of authority over them, so that he might exploit them for the benefit of his tyrannical apparatus and leave them on the black soil, and through this path, acquire a massive fortune for himself as well.

Evidence suggests that after the destruction of the Pharaohs, a massive amount of their wealth and treasures remained in Qarun's hands, and Musa عليه السلام had not yet found the opportunity to take this windfall Pharaonic wealth from him for the benefit of the oppressed.

In any case, whether he found this wealth during the era of Pharaoh, or through plundering his treasures, or as some say through the science of alchemy, or knowledge of the arts of sound trade, or familiarity with the principles of exploiting the oppressed—whatever it was, Qarun chose faith after Musa's victory over the Pharaohs. He quickly changed his appearance and, with the particular dexterity unique to this group, inserted himself into the ranks of the reciters of the Torah and the scholars of the Children of Israel, while it is unlikely that a grain of faith ever penetrated such a heart.

Finally, when Musa عليه السلام decided to take Zakat from him, the veil was removed from his face, and the ugly and ominous countenance he had behind the deceptive mask of faith appeared to everyone. And we saw where the end of this hypocritical man's affair led.

3- Islam's Position on the Issue of Wealth

It should not be inferred from what we have said that Islam has a negative position toward property and wealth and is opposed to it. It should not be imagined that Islam prefers poverty, invites to poverty, and considers it a means of spiritual perfection.

On the contrary, Islam emphasizes it as an effective and useful tool. In verse 180 of Surah al-Baqarah, "wealth" is referred to as "Khayr" (goodness).

And in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The world is a good help for seeking the Hereafter" [1]. Rather, the verses under discussion, which most severely condemn the arrogant wealthy Qarun, are a clear witness to this subject. However, Islam approves of wealth through which "the seeking of the abode of the Hereafter" is achieved, just as the scholars of the Children of Israel said to Qarun: "But seek, through that which Allah has given you, the home of the Hereafter."

Islam approves of wealth in which "do good as Allah has done good to you" and kindness to all is realized.

Islam praises wealth in which "do not forget your share of the world" is fulfilled. Ultimately, Islam desires wealth that does not become a source of corruption on earth, the forgetting of human values, and becoming trapped in the mad race of "Takathur" (accumulation), and does not lead a person to "self-superiority," "humiliating others," and even confronting the Prophet of Allah.

It should be a means for everyone's use, for filling existing economic gaps, for placing a balm on the agonizing wounds of the deprived, and for reaching the needs and problems of the oppressed.

The love for such wealth with such holy goals is not love for the world; it is love for the Hereafter. As we read in a hadith, one of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام came to him and complained that "we are after the world and are fond of it" (fearing that they might be worldly-minded).

The Imam (who knew the purity and piety of that man) asked: "What do you want to do with this worldly wealth?" In response, he said: "To provide for the expenses of myself and my family, to help my relatives, to spend in the way of Allah, and to perform Hajj and Umrah." The Imam said: "This is not seeking the world; this is seeking the Hereafter" [1]. From here, the falsity of the belief of two groups becomes clear: a group of "so-called Muslims" ignorant of Islamic teachings who present Islam as a supporter of the arrogant, and a group of biased enemies who want to show the face of Islam as distorted and present it as anti-wealth and pro-poverty.

Fundamentally, a poor nation cannot live free and proud. Poverty is a means of dependency. Poverty is a cause of disgrace in this world and the Hereafter. And poverty invites man to sin and pollution.

As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Wealth that prevents you from oppression is better than poverty that carries you toward sin" [1]. Islamic societies must strive to become as rich and self-sufficient as possible, to reach the stage of self-reliance and stand on their own feet, and not sacrifice their honor, dignity, and independence due to poverty by becoming dependent on others. They should know that this is the authentic line of Islam.
