Ayahs

تِلْكَ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُهَا لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٨٣)
This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to domineer in the earth nor to cause corruption, and the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary. (83)
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٨٤)
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] better than it, but whoever brings vice—those who commit misdeeds shall not be requited except for what they used to do. (84)

Commentary

The Consequence of Seeking Dominance and Corruption on Earth

After mentioning the shaking story of the criminal and arrogant wealthy man, Qarun, the first verse under discussion provides a statement that is, in fact, a general conclusion from this narrative. It says:

"That home of the Hereafter We assign to those who do not desire exaltedness upon the earth or corruption" (تِلْكَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُها لِلَّذِينَ لا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فَساداً).

Not only are they not seekers of superiority or corrupters, but they do not even "desire" it; their hearts are pure from such matters, and their souls are sanctified from these pollutions.

What causes a human to be deprived of the blessings of the home of the Hereafter is, in truth, these two things: seeking superiority (arrogance) and "corruption on earth," which encompasses all sins. This is because whatever Allah has forbidden is certainly contrary to the system of human creation and the perfection of existence; therefore, committing it disrupts the system of his life and is thus a cause of corruption on earth.

Even the issue of "exaltation" and seeking superiority is itself one of the instances of "corruption on earth," but its extraordinary importance has caused it to be mentioned specifically.

In the biography and fate of Qarun, we saw that what caused his misery, destruction, and annihilation was that very arrogance and seeking of superiority.

In Islamic narrations, this issue has been emphasized greatly, to the extent that in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"A man might be pleased that the strap of his shoe is better than the strap of his companion's shoe, and thus he falls under [the warning of] this verse!" (Because even this is a small branch of seeking superiority!) [^1]

It is interesting that the author of the Kashshaf exegesis, after mentioning this hadith, states: "Some greedy individuals attribute 'exaltedness' in the verse under discussion to Pharaoh, according to the verse: 'Indeed, Pharaoh exalted himself in the land' (Al-Qasas: 4), and 'corruption' to Qarun, according to the verse: 'and desire not corruption in the land' (Al-Qasas: 77). They say that whoever is not like Pharaoh and Qarun, Paradise and the eternal home belong to him. In this way, they exclude only Pharaoh, Qarun, and their likes from Paradise, and consider it theirs! These people have not carefully reflected on the phrase 'and the [best] outcome is for the righteous' (وَ الْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ) which appears at the end of this verse, in the way that Ali عليه السلام reflected on it." [^1]

The point that must be added to this statement is that this group has even erred in identifying Pharaoh and Qarun, for Pharaoh both sought exaltedness on earth and was a corrupter—"Indeed, he was of the corrupters" (Al-Qasas: 4)—and Qarun also committed corruption on earth and sought exaltedness and superiority, according to: "So he came out before his people in his adornment" (Al-Qasas: 79).

In another hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that during his apparent caliphate, he would personally walk through the markets, guiding those who were lost, helping the weak, and passing by the sellers and tradesmen while reciting this verse to them: تِلْكَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُها لِلَّذِينَ لا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فَساداً. Then he would say:

"This verse was revealed concerning the just and humble rulers, as well as other powerful people among the masses." [^2]

Meaning, just as I have not made governance a means for my own superiority, you too must not make your financial power a means of dominance over others, for the good end and outcome belong to the group that does not wish to seek superiority or cause corruption.

As the Quran states at the end of the verse under discussion: "and the [best] outcome is for the righteous" (وَ الْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).

"Outcome" (Al-Aqibah) in the broad sense of the word means a good end, victory in this world, and Paradise and its blessings in the next world. We saw what kind of end the Qaruns and Pharaohs met, despite their power being unparalleled. Because they lacked piety (Taqwa), they were afflicted with the most painful fate.

We conclude the discussion on this verse with a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: when the Imam recited this verse, he began to weep and said:

"By Allah, all hopes are gone with [the weight of] this verse!" And reaching the home of the Hereafter is difficult! [^1]

[^1]: Jami' al-Jami' Commentary, under the verse discussed. [^1]: Fakhr Razi Commentary, under the verse discussed. [^2]: This narration was reported by Zadhan from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام (Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed). [^1]: Ali ibn Ibrahim Commentary, under the verse discussed.

After mentioning the reality that the home of the Hereafter and its blessings do not belong to the seekers of dominance and the arrogant, but are reserved for the pious, humble, and truth-seeking, the second verse under discussion states a general law that is a blend of "justice" and "grace" regarding reward and punishment. It says: "Whoever comes [on the Day of Judgement] with a good deed will have better than it" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها).

This is the stage of "grace" (Tafaddul); meaning Allah is not like narrow-minded people who, when observing justice, try to make the wage and reward exactly equal to the action. Out of His infinite grace, He sometimes gives ten times, sometimes hundreds of times, and sometimes thousands of times the reward for an action. The minimum is ten times, as we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 160: "Whoever comes [with] a good deed will have ten times the like thereof" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها). The maximum is known only to Allah, a glimpse of which is mentioned regarding spending in the way of Allah in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 261.

Of course, this multiplying of the reward is not without reason; it depends on the level of purity of the action, sincerity, good intention, and clarity of heart. This is the stage of Divine grace for the doers of good. Then, mentioning the punishment of the evildoers, it says: "but those who did evil deeds will not be recompensed except [for] what they used to do" (وَ مَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَلا يُجْزَى الَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السَّيِّئاتِ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

And this is the stage of the Lord's "justice," for they are not punished even a fraction more than what they have done.

It is interesting that it says: their own actions are their punishment. This means that their action—which, according to the law of the conservation of entities in the universe, remains with its effects both within the soul and externally in this world—will manifest in a form suitable to itself on the Day of Resurrection, which is the day of the disclosure of secrets (Yawm al-Buruz). it will accompany the sinners and will torture and torment them.

There are three questions here that must be answered

1- Why is "evil deed" (Sayyi'ah) repeated twice in this verse?

The reason might be that the Quran wants to emphasize that for "evil deeds," no punishment shall overtake them except the very action they performed. In other words, "there is no remedy for what one has done to oneself."

2- Does "good deed" (Hasanah) in the above verse include faith and monotheism?

If so, what is the meaning of the phrase saying We provide a reward "better than it"? Is there anything better than that to be its reward?

In response, we say that undoubtedly Hasanah has a broad meaning and includes belief systems, speech, and external actions. As for what is better than the belief in monotheism, it is the pleasure and satisfaction of the Lord, which is the reward of the doers of good, as we read in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 72: "and acceptance from Allah is greater" (وَ رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ)—the pleasure of Allah is superior to every reward.

3- Why is "good deed" (Hasanah) in the above verse singular, while "evil deeds" (Sayyi'at) is plural?

Some believe this difference in expression refers to the abundance of sinners and the fewness of the doers of good. [^1] There is also the possibility that good deeds are essentially summarized in monotheism (Tawhid), and all good deeds return to the root of monotheism, whereas evil deeds return to the root of polytheism (Shirk), which is the center of dispersion, fragmentation, and multiplicity.

[^1]: Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِى فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لَرَآدُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَمَنْ هُوَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٨٥)
Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the place of return. Say, ‘My Lord knows best him who brings guidance and him who is in manifest error.’ (85)
وَمَا كُنتَ تَرْجُوٓا۟ أَن يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٦)
You did not expect that the Book would be delivered to you; but it was a mercy from your Lord. So do not be ever an advocate of the faithless. (86)
وَلَا يَصُدُّنَّكَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أُنزِلَتْ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٨٧)
Do not ever let them bar you from Allah’s signs after they have been sent down to you. Invite to your Lord, and never be one of the polytheists. (87)
وَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۘ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ كُلُّ شَىْءٍ هَالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُۥ ۚ لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٨)
And do not invoke another god besides Allah; there is no god except Him. Everything is to perish except His Face. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back. (88)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators has narrated an occasion of revelation for the first verse above from Ibn Abbas, the essence of which is as follows:

When the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله was leaving "Mecca" for "Medina" during the Migration (Hijrah), he reached the land of Juhfah, which is not very far from Mecca. He remembered his homeland, "Mecca"—the city that is Allah’s secure sanctuary and where the House of the Kaaba is located, to which the Prophet’s heart and soul were inextricably linked. The signs of this longing, mingled with sorrow and grief, appeared on his blessed face. At this moment, the messenger of Allah’s revelation, Gabriel عليه السلام, descended and asked: "Do you truly long for your city and birthplace?" The Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله replied: "Yes." Gabriel said: "Allah has sent this message to you: إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى‌ مَعادٍ 'Indeed, He who has ordained the Quran upon you will surely take you back to your place of return.'" [^1] And we know that this great promise was eventually fulfilled; the Prophet of Islam صلے الله عليه وآله returned to Mecca triumphantly with a powerful army and immense glory, and Allah’s secure sanctuary was surrendered to him without war or bloodshed.

Therefore, the above verse is one of the miraculous prophecies of the Quran, which—

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir al-Mizan, and other commentaries.

—announced such news with certainty and without any conditions, and it was realized after a short period.

Commentary

The Promise of Return to Allah’s Secure Sanctuary

These verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Qasas, address the Prophet of Islam صلے الله عليه وآله. Following the narration of parts of the life of Moses son of Imran عليه السلام and his struggle against the Pharaohs, they give a glad tiding to the Prophet of Islam صلے الله عليه وآله along with emphatic instructions.

As mentioned, the first of these verses was revealed, according to the popular view, in the land of "Juhfah" while the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله was on his way to Medina. He is heading to Yathrib to make it "Madinat al-Rasul" (the City of the Messenger), to form the core of the Islamic government there, to nurture latent talents, and to use it as a base and platform to achieve the expansive Divine government and its goals. Yet, despite this, his love and attachment to Mecca deeply pained him, and being away from this secure Divine sanctuary was very difficult for him.

It is here that the light of revelation shines upon his pure heart and gives him the glad tiding of returning to his beloved land, saying: "Indeed, He who ordained the Quran upon you will surely take you back to your place of return" (إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى‌ مَعادٍ).

Do not grieve; the same God who returned Moses to his mother in infancy, the same God who returned him to his original birthplace after a ten-year absence from Egypt so he could ignite the lamp of Monotheism (Tawhid), establish the government of the oppressed, and shatter the power of the tyrannical Pharaohs—that same God will return you to Mecca with full power and strength, and will ignite the lamp of Monotheism in this holy land by your hand.

The same God who revealed the Quran to you, made its delivery an obligation, and its rulings mandatory—yes, the God of the Quran, the magnificent God of the earth and the heavens—these matters are easy and simple before His power.

Then He adds, in the face of the stubbornness of the fierce opponents: "Say: My Lord knows best who has brought guidance from Him, and who is in manifest error" (قُلْ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ مَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ مَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

The path of guidance is clear and their deviation is obvious; they exhaust themselves in vain. Allah knows well, and truth-seeking hearts are also aware of this reality.

Of course, the clear interpretation of the above verse is what we mentioned, but a group of commentators has suggested other possibilities for the word "Ma'ad" (place of return), such as: "return to life after death," "the land of Resurrection," "death itself," "the station of the Great Intercession," "Paradise," or "Jerusalem" (Bayt al-Maqdis) which was the Prophet's first point of Ascension (Mi'raj), and the like. However, considering the overall content of Surah al-Qasas, what was mentioned in the story of Moses and the Children of Israel, and the occasion of revelation cited above, all these meanings seem unlikely except for the interpretation of "Ma'ad" as the place of return, meaning the land of Mecca.

Furthermore, the "Ma'ad" of the Day of Resurrection is not something exclusive to the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله, whereas the verse speaks only of the Prophet. The placement of this verse after the verse regarding reward and punishment in the Hereafter not only fails to prove that point but might even suggest the opposite; for the previous verse speaks of victory in the other world, and it is fitting that the verse under discussion speaks of victory in this world.

The next verse addresses another of the greatest blessings of the Lord upon the Noble Prophet, saying: "And you did not expect that the Book would be sent down to you, but it was a mercy from your Lord" (وَ ما كُنْتَ تَرْجُوا أَنْ يُلْقى‌ إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابُ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌). [^1] Many people had heard the glad tidings of the emergence of a new religion, and perhaps a group from the People of the Book and others were waiting for revelation to descend upon them and for God to entrust them with this responsibility. But you did not expect it, yet Allah deemed you the most worthy of all, and this great religion was to be spread throughout the world only through your hands.

Some great commentators have considered this sentence to be in harmony with the sentences previously mentioned regarding the story of Moses—addressed to the Prophet of Islam—where He said: "And you were not on the western side when We decreed the matter to Moses... nor were you a dweller among the people of Midian... nor were you on the side of the Mount (Tur) when We called, but it is a mercy from your Lord":

"You were never in the valley of Tur when We sent revelation to Moses... you did not live among the people of Midian... you were not present at the hour when the command of messengership was revealed to Moses; these are the mercies of Allah that have encompassed you and placed their precise accounts at your disposal." Therefore, the "Book" here refers to the accounts of the previous prophets.

However, this interpretation does not contradict the first one and is, in fact, considered a part of it.

Then He adds: Now, in gratitude for this great blessing, "never be a supporter of the disbelievers" (فَلا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيراً لِلْكافِرِينَ‌).

Certainly, the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله would never support the disbelievers; this command is an emphasis for him and the statement of an important duty for others.

This statement is actually in harmony with what we read in the previous verses regarding Moses, who said: "My Lord, for the favor You have bestowed upon me, I will never be a supporter of the criminals" (We discussed the matter of helping oppressors in detail under verse 17 of Surah al-Qasas).

The final two verses of this Surah are an emphasis on the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) using various expressions and arguments—a Monotheism that is the essence of all religious matters, a Monotheism that is both the root and the branch, the whole and the part. In these two verses, four commands are given to the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله and four descriptions of the Lord are provided, completing the collective discussions presented in the verses of this Surah.

First, He says: "And let them not avert you from the verses of Allah after they have been revealed to you" (وَ لا يَصُدُّنَّكَ عَنْ آياتِ اللَّهِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أُنْزِلَتْ إِلَيْكَ‌).

Although the prohibition is directed at the disbelievers, its meaning is the Prophet's non-submission to their obstructions and conspiracies. It is exactly like saying: "That person must not tempt you," meaning "Do not submit to his temptations."

In this way, He commands the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله that when the Divine verses are revealed, he must stand by them with decisiveness, cast away any doubt or hesitation, remove obstacles whatever they may be, and move toward the destination with firm steps, for Allah is with him and is his supporter.

According to the famous commentator Ibn Abbas, although the addressee is the person of the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله, the intended audience is the general public, following the well-known Arabic proverb: "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor."

Following this command, which was negative in nature, He issues the second command, which is positive: "And invite to your Lord" (وَ ادْعُ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ‌).

The Lord who is your Master, your Disposer of affairs, and your Sustainer and Educator.

The third command, after inviting toward Allah, is the negation of any kind of polytheism and idol-worship: "And never be of the polytheists" (وَ لا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌).

For the path of Monotheism is clear and luminous, and its travelers are on the Straight Path.

Finally, the fourth command is a renewed emphasis on the negation of any kind of polytheism: "And do not invoke with Allah another deity" (وَ لا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ). These successive commands, each emphasizing the other, clarify the importance of the issue of Monotheism in Islamic programs, without which everything is lost.

Following these four commands, He provides four descriptions of Allah, which are also successive emphases on the issue of Monotheism.

First, He says: "There is no deity except Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"Everything is perishing except His [pure] Face" (كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ‌).

"To Him belongs the judgment [and sovereignty in the world of creation and legislation]" (لَهُ الْحُكْمُ‌).

"And to Him you will all be returned" (وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌).

The last three descriptions can serve as evidence to prove Monotheism and the abandonment of all idol-worship mentioned in the first description.

For when we are all mortal and permanence is exclusive to Him;

And when the management of the system of existence and sovereignty belongs to Him;

And when the return of all of us on the Day of Resurrection is toward Him; what role can other deities have? And what besides Him is worthy of worship?

Great commentators have various views regarding the interpretation of the phrase "Everything is perishing except His Face," revolving around the interpretation of "Face" (Wajh) and the word "Perishing" (Halik).

Linguistically, "Wajh" refers to the face and the part of the body that is in front of a person, but when used regarding the Lord, it means His Pure Essence.

The word "Halik" is from the root "Halak," meaning death and destruction.

Therefore, the above sentence refers to the annihilation of all beings except His Pure Essence—not just annihilation after the end of this world, but even now they are annihilated and perishing before Him! For contingent beings are dependent on His Pure Essence and receive the grace of existence from Him moment by moment; they have nothing in their own essence, and whatever they have is from Allah—such that if He "withdraws His favor for a moment, the forms would collapse"! Beyond this, the beings of this world are all changing and subject to transformations; even according to the theory of Substantial Motion (al-Harakah al-Jawhariyyah), their essence is identical to change and transformation. We know that motion and change mean constant perishing and constant renewal; at every moment, the beings of the material world die and are brought to life. Therefore, even now they are perishing and annihilated; the only Essence in which change and annihilation have no way, and which is absolute independence, is His Holy Essence.

We also know that at the end of this world, annihilation and non-existence will manifest more clearly, as the Quran says: "Everyone upon it will perish, and there remains the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor" (Surah ar-Rahman, verses 26 and 27).

Not only those on earth, but those in the heavens will also perish: "And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will fall dead" (az-Zumar, 68).

This is an interpretation consistent with the literal meaning of the verse and other verses of the Quran.

However, some commentators have mentioned other interpretations for it, including:

"Wajh" refers to righteous deeds, and the meaning of the verse is that all actions will be lost except the action performed for the sake of Allah’s Pure Essence.

Others have said: "Wajh" refers to the aspect of the attribution of things to Allah; thus, the meaning of the verse is that everything is inherently non-existent except from the perspective of its attribution to the Lord.

Some have said: "Wajh" means religion, and the meaning of the verse is that all religions are perishing and false except the religion of Allah. They consider the phrase "To Him belongs the judgment" as referring to legislative sovereignty and an emphasis on this interpretation. Likewise, they interpret the phrase "And to Him you will be returned" as returning to Allah to receive the Sharia, as a renewed emphasis on this meaning. [^1] These interpretations do not actually contradict what we said above, because once we know that the only thing that remains in this world is Allah’s Pure Essence, it becomes clear that whatever is somehow related to His Pure Essence also takes on the color of permanence and eternity: Allah’s religion, which is from Him, is eternal; righteous deeds, which are for Him, are eternal; Divine leaders, who are connected to Him, have an eternal quality because they are related to Him. In short, anything that has a link or connection to His Pure Essence in some way is not subject to annihilation or destruction from that perspective (reflect carefully).

Key Points

1- How do all things perish?

Among the questions raised regarding the above verse is that if everything is destroyed at the end of the world, then the dust resulting from human bodies must also vanish, whereas the Quran repeatedly states that We gather this dust and bring humans into existence from it once more, or that humans will rise from their graves on the Day of Resurrection.

Also, according to the literal meaning of Quranic verses, Paradise and Hell have already been created (the expression "prepared for the righteous" or similar phrases indicating the readiness of Paradise for the pious appear in two places in the Quran: Al-Imran, 133 and al-Hadid, 21; and the expression "prepared for the disbelievers" regarding Hell appears in two places: al-Baqarah, 24 and Al-Imran, 131).

[^1]: Several narrations have been recorded in Nur al-Thaqalayn regarding the interpretation of this verse, in some of which "Wajh" is interpreted as the religion of Allah, and in others as "Divine leaders" and that which is attributed to Allah.

Will Paradise and Hell also perish and be destroyed at the end of the world?! Beyond all this, we believe in the Purgatory (Barzakh) life of humans and have derived it from Quranic verses regarding souls; will they also be destroyed?! The answer to all these questions becomes clear with the following explanation:

Often, "destruction" (halak), non-existence, and annihilation refer to the "disruption of the system," not the disappearance of matter. For example, if a building is demolished by an earthquake, "perishing" and "annihilation" apply to it, while its materials exist; only its system has been dismantled.

And we know that at the end of this world, the sun will lose its light, the moon will darken, the mountains will crumble, and all living beings will die; this is the meaning of their destruction—this is one aspect.

On the other hand, destruction and annihilation pertain to the world and what is in the world. As for Paradise and Hell—whether we consider them to be within the inner dimension of this world or outside and encompassing this world—they are not part of this world such that the decree of annihilation and the destruction of their system would include them; they belong to the other world and the Hereafter, not this world.

Thirdly, as we said above, destruction and annihilation regarding contingent beings are not limited to the end of the world; they are perishing and annihilated even now, for they have nothing of their own within their essence and whatever they have is from another. They are changing and constantly in motion, and we know that motion means gradual annihilation and a combination of existence and non-existence.

With these explanations, the answers to the above questions become completely clear.

2- A Deviant Interpretation for the Phrase "And do not invoke with Allah another deity"

A group of "Wahhabis" who insist that the issue of "Tawassul (Intercession) and Shafa'ah" is inconsistent with the reality of Monotheism have sometimes argued using the above verse and similar verses. They say: The Quran explicitly forbids the worship of other than Allah and mentions not calling upon anyone along with Allah: "So do not invoke anyone with Allah" (Surah al-Jinn, verse 18).

However, the meaning of such verses is certainly not that we should not call out to other people; the meaning is exactly what is understood from the phrase "with Allah" (ma'a Allah)—that is, if someone seeks Allah’s work from other than Allah and considers them independent in performing it, he is a polytheist.

But if we consider all powers to be exclusive to Allah and do not recognize anyone as being alongside Him or as an independent source of effect, yet believe that the friends (Awliya) of Allah intercede by His permission and command, and we turn to them to intercede for us in the presence of Allah, this is the essence of Monotheism. This is the very thing that has been repeatedly mentioned in the verses of the Quran.

When the brothers of Joseph said to their father: "O our father, ask forgiveness for us" (Surah Yusuf, 97), was this polytheism?! Or when the Quran says: "And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful" (an-Nisa, 64), is this an invitation to polytheism?! The reality of intercession and Tawassul is nothing other than this. [^1]

O Lord! Cast the light of Monotheism and Your gnosis into our hearts so that we see none but You, seek none but You, and want none but You.

O Allah! Strengthen our connection to Your Pure Essence day by day, so that through this, the permanence and eternity of Your Essence may shine upon our souls.

O God! Remove the love of the world, seeking superiority, and corruption on earth from our souls, and place us among the ranks of the righteous for whom is the good end (And the [best] outcome is for the righteous).

End of Surah al-Qasas – from Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 16, 21st of Ramadan al-Mubarak 1403 – July 3, 1983.
